topics.title: Diode Characteristics (Silicon, Zener)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Diode Characteristics (Silicon, Zener)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Diode Characteristics (Silicon, Zener)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Semiconductor Diode** is a two-terminal, asymmetrically conducting electronic device formed by joining **p-type** and **n-type** semiconductor materials, creating a **P-N Junction**. Its electrical behavior is highly non-linear and is described by plotting the **Voltage-Current (V-I) Characteristics** — a graph that maps the current flowing through the diode against the voltage applied across its terminals, under both **Forward Bias** and **Reverse Bias** conditions.

In the KTU PCBML307 Analog Electronics Lab context, this experiment focuses on characterizing two specific diode families:
- **Silicon (Si) Power/Signal Diode (e.g., 1N4007 / 1N4148):** Used for rectification and switching.
- **Zener Diode (e.g., 1N4733 / BZX55 series):** A specially doped diode designed to operate reliably in the **reverse breakdown region** for voltage regulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This experiment directly addresses **CO2: *Analyze the characteristics of electronic devices such as diodes and transistors*.** Students are expected to experimentally plot and interpret the static V-I curves of both Si and Zener diodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **check valve** used in household water plumbing:
- When you push water in the correct direction (forward bias), the valve opens smoothly after a small "threshold pressure" is overcome. Water flows freely.
- When water tries to push backwards (reverse bias), the valve snaps shut and virtually no water flows.
- However, if the reverse pressure becomes dangerously high (beyond the structural limit of the valve), the valve will **rupture deliberately** — in a Zener diode, this controlled "rupture" is called **Zener Breakdown**, and it is exploited for voltage regulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Pressure Analogy:**
> For a Silicon diode, the threshold pressure is **0.7 V**. For a Germanium diode, it is **0.3 V**. This threshold is called the **Knee Voltage (Vₖ)** or **Cut-in Voltage (Vᵧ)**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following parameters are critical for the experiment:

- **Thermal Voltage (Vₜ)** at room temperature (T = 300 K): **Vₜ = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV (≈ 26 mV)**
- **Ideality Factor (n):** Between **1 and 2** (typically 1–1.5 for Silicon).
- **Forward Voltage Drop (V_F):** **0.6 V – 0.7 V** for Silicon at rated current.
- **Zener Voltage (V_Z):** The nominal reverse breakdown voltage, ranging from **2.4 V to 200 V** depending on the diode.
- **Boltzmann Constant (k):** **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Electron Charge (q):** **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Regions of a Diode

The V-I characteristic is divided into **three distinct regions** of operation. Each region is mathematically and physically distinct.

### Region A — Forward Bias Region

- The **P-side** is connected to the positive terminal and the **N-side** to the negative terminal of the battery.
- The applied voltage opposes the built-in potential barrier (V_bi ≈ 0.7 V for Si).
- When V > Vₖ, the diode conducts heavily, and current rises exponentially with voltage.
- This is the normal **ON-state** operation used in rectifiers.

### Region B — Reverse Bias Region

- The polarity is reversed; **P-side** to negative, **N-side** to positive.
- The depletion region widens, and only a tiny **Reverse Saturation Current (I_s)** flows — typically in **nA** (Silicon) or **µA** (Germanium) range.
- The diode behaves like an open switch.

### Region C — Breakdown Region (Critical for Zener Diodes)

- When the reverse voltage exceeds a critical value **V_BR (Zener Voltage, V_Z)**, the diode enters breakdown.
- Two mechanisms are responsible:
  1. **Zener Breakdown** (V_Z < 5.5 V): Caused by quantum tunneling of electrons through the thin depletion layer due to a high electric field (>10⁶ V/cm).
  2. **Avalanche Breakdown** (V_Z > 5.5 V): Caused by impact ionization where carriers gain enough kinetic energy to knock out new electron-hole pairs.
- In this region, the voltage across the diode remains nearly **constant** at V_Z, while current increases sharply — this property is exploited in **voltage regulator circuits**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $I_D$ | $I_D = I_s \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_t}} - 1 \right)$ | **Shockley Diode Equation** | Amperes (A) |
| $V_t$ | $V_t = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26\ \text{mV}$ | Thermal Voltage at 300 K | Volts (V) |
| $V_F$ | $\approx 0.7\ \text{V (Si)},\ 0.3\ \text{V (Ge)}$ | Forward Voltage Drop | Volts (V) |
| $r_d$ | $r_d = \dfrac{n V_t}{I_D}$ | **Dynamic / AC Resistance** of diode | Ohms (Ω) |
| $R_{DC}$ | $R_{DC} = \dfrac{V_D}{I_D}$ | Static DC Resistance | Ohms (Ω) |
| $V_Z$ | Manufacturer specified | **Zener Voltage** (Reverse Breakdown) | Volts (V) |
| $I_{ZT}$ | Test current at V_Z | Zener Test Current | mA |
| $P_D$ | $P_D = V_Z \times I_Z$ | Power Dissipation in Zener | Watts (W) |
| $P_{max}$ | $V_Z \times I_{ZM}$ | Maximum rated power | Watts (W) |
| $R_S$ | $R_S = \dfrac{V_S - V_Z}{I_L}$ | Series Resistance in regulator | Ohms (Ω) |
| $I_S$ | $\approx 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-15}$ | Reverse Saturation Current | Amperes (A) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on LaTeX:** All absolute-value notation uses `\vert` or `\mid` instead of vertical pipes to preserve markdown table integrity. For example, use $\vert V_D \vert$ instead of $|V_D|$ inside tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rectifiers in Power Supplies:** Silicon diodes (1N4007) convert AC to pulsating DC. The 0.7 V drop is acceptable for high-voltage applications.
- **Voltage Regulators:** Zener diodes (e.g., BZX55C5V6) provide a stable reference voltage in power supplies, ADC references, and protection circuits.
- **Signal Demodulators:** In AM radio receivers, signal diodes (1N4148) extract the audio envelope from the RF carrier.
- **Clipper & Clamper Circuits:** Diodes are used to shape waveforms by removing portions above/below a threshold.
- **Overvoltage Protection (TVS Diodes):** Zener-based Transient Voltage Suppressors protect microcontrollers and ICs from ESD and voltage spikes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Semiconductor Physics to Shockley's Equation

### Step 1 — Carrier Concentration at the Junction

Under forward bias, electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side are injected across the junction. The minority carrier concentration at the depletion edges increases exponentially with applied voltage $V_D$.

$$\Delta n_p = n_{p0} \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{V_t}} - 1 \right)$$

Here, $n_{p0}$ is the thermal equilibrium minority electron concentration on the p-side. The ratio $V_D / V_t$ represents the energy gained by carriers relative to thermal energy.

### Step 2 — Diffusion Current from Minority Carriers

The injected minority carriers diffuse away from the junction, producing a diffusion current. Integrating the diffusion equation across the quasi-neutral regions gives the total diode current:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &= q A \left( \frac{D_p}{L_p} p_{n0} + \frac{D_n}{L_n} n_{p0} \right) \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{V_t}} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the junction
- $D_p, D_n$ = diffusion coefficients for holes and electrons
- $L_p, L_n$ = diffusion lengths for holes and electrons
- $p_{n0}, n_{p0}$ = thermal equilibrium minority carrier concentrations

### Step 3 — Introducing Ideality Factor and Saturation Current

The bracketed quantity is defined as the **Reverse Saturation Current** $I_S$, and an ideality factor $n$ is introduced to account for recombination in the depletion region:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_S &= q A \left( \frac{D_p}{L_p} p_{n0} + \frac{D_n}{L_n} n_{p0} \right) \\[4pt]
I_D &= I_S \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_t}} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Shockley Diode Equation** — the cornerstone of all PN junction analysis.

### Step 4 — Simplification for Forward Bias ($V_D \gg V_t$)

When $V_D \gg V_t$ (typically $V_D > 0.1$ V), the exponential term dominates and the "-1" becomes negligible:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &\approx I_S \cdot e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_t}}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(I_D) &= \ln(I_S) + \frac{V_D}{n V_t} \\[4pt]
V_D &= n V_t \ln\left(\frac{I_D}{I_S}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This shows that $V_D$ depends **logarithmically** on $I_D$, which is why a diode's forward voltage changes only slightly over a wide range of currents.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Forward vs Reverse vs Zener Breakdown

| Parameter | Forward Bias (Si) | Reverse Bias (Si) | Zener Breakdown |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Applied Voltage $V_D$ | $V_D > 0$ (typically 0.6–0.8 V) | $V_D < 0$ | $V_D = -V_Z$ |
| Depletion Width | Narrows | Widens | Widens to limit |
| Dominant Current | Diffusion current (large) | Drift current ($I_S$, tiny) | Tunneling / Avalanche (large) |
| Resistance | Low ($r_d \sim$ few Ω) | Very High (MΩ) | Low (Zener impedance $Z_Z$) |
| Diode State | **ON** | **OFF** | **Controlled Breakdown** |
| Typical Magnitude | mA to A | nA | mA to A |
| V-I Curve Slope | Steep (exponential) | Flat (near zero) | Nearly vertical at $V_Z$ |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Dynamic Resistance Calculation

**Problem:** A Silicon diode carries a forward current of $I_D = 10$ mA at room temperature. Calculate the dynamic (AC) resistance of the diode. Assume ideality factor $n = 1.5$.

**Given:**
- $I_D = 10 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- $n = 1.5$
- $V_t = 26 \times 10^{-3}$ V

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_d &= \frac{n V_t}{I_D} \\[4pt]
r_d &= \frac{1.5 \times 26 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
r_d &= \frac{39 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
r_d &= 3.9\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The dynamic resistance is **3.9 Ω** — meaning a small AC signal superimposed on the DC bias will see this small resistance.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Zener Regulator Design

**Problem:** Design a Zener regulator for the following specifications:
- Input voltage $V_S = 15$ V (DC)
- Zener voltage $V_Z = 5.6$ V
- Load current $I_L = 20$ mA
- Zener current at minimum $I_{ZK} = 5$ mA

**Find:** Series resistance $R_S$ and Zener power rating.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_S &= \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_L + I_Z} \\[4pt]
R_S &= \frac{15 - 5.6}{(20 + 5) \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
R_S &= \frac{9.4}{25 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
R_S &= 376\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Power dissipated by Zener:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_Z &= V_Z \times I_Z \\[4pt]
P_Z &= 5.6 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
P_Z &= 0.14\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Choose a Zener diode rated at least **0.5 W** (standard safety derating factor of ~3×) and a standard resistor value of **R_S = 390 Ω, 0.5 W**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forward Bias Test Circuit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV[DC Voltage Source 0 to 5V] --> nodeR[Series Resistor R 1kOhm]
    nodeR --> nodeA[Anode P side of Diode]
    nodeC[Cathode N side of Diode] --> nodeM[Ammeter mA range]
    nodeM --> nodeGND[Ground Reference 0V]
    nodeV2[DC Voltmeter across Diode] -.Measures V_D.-> nodeA
    nodeV2 -.Measures V_D.-> nodeC
```

**Description:** A variable DC source supplies voltage through a current-limiting resistor R. The ammeter reads forward current $I_F$, and the voltmeter reads $V_F$ across the diode. Readings are tabulated to plot the forward V-I curve.

## 4.2 Reverse Bias Test Circuit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVS[DC Voltage Source 0 to 30V] --> nodeRS[Series Resistor Rs 10kOhm]
    nodeRS --> nodeCA[Cathode N side of Diode]
    nodeAN[Anode P side of Diode] --> nodeMI[Microammeter uA range]
    nodeMI --> nodeGR[Ground Reference 0V]
    nodeVV[DC Voltmeter across Diode] -.Measures V_R.-> nodeCA
    nodeVV -.Measures V_R.-> nodeAN
```

**Description:** The polarity of the source is reversed compared to forward bias. A microammeter is used because the reverse saturation current is in the µA range. The voltmeter reads the reverse voltage $V_R$.

## 4.3 Zener Diode Reverse Breakdown Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc[Variable DC Source 0 to 20V] --> nodeRs[Series Resistor Rs 470 Ohm]
    nodeRs --> nodeK[Cathode K of Zener]
    nodeA[Anode A of Zener] --> nodeGr[Ground 0V]
    nodeVm[Voltmeter across Zener] -.Reads V_Z.-> nodeK
    nodeVm -.Reads V_Z.-> nodeA
    nodeAm[Ammeter in series] --> nodeK
```

**Description:** The Zener is reverse-biased. As input voltage is increased beyond the breakdown voltage, the voltmeter reading stabilizes at $V_Z$ while current increases sharply.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: VI Curve Interpretation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Apply Voltage V_D] --> stepB{Region Check}
    stepB -->|V_D greater than 0| stepC[Forward Bias: Measure I_F]
    stepB -->|V_D less than 0| stepD[Reverse Bias: Measure I_R]
    stepC --> stepE{I_F significant above V_k?}
    stepE -->|Yes| stepF[Exponential Rise Region]
    stepE -->|No| stepG[Knee / Cut-in Region near 0.7V]
    stepD --> stepH{Is V_R magnitude greater than V_Z?}
    stepH -->|No| stepI[Reverse Saturation: I_R approx I_S in nA]
    stepH -->|Yes| stepJ[Zener Breakdown: Constant V_Z]
    stepF --> stepK[Plot Point on Forward Quadrant]
    stepG --> stepK
    stepI --> stepL[Plot Point on Reverse Quadrant]
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepK --> stepM[Generate V-I Characteristic Graph]
    stepL --> stepM
```

**Description:** This flowchart represents the logical decision process followed during the experiment to classify each data point and plot it on the correct quadrant of the V-I curve.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Knee Voltage and Reverse Saturation Current. [`KTU University Exam – May 2024`]

**Model Answer:**
The **Knee Voltage (Vₖ)**, also called the **Cut-in Voltage** or **Threshold Voltage**, is the minimum forward voltage at which the diode begins to conduct appreciable current. For a Silicon diode, $V_k \approx 0.7$ V; for Germanium, $V_k \approx 0.3$ V.

The **Reverse Saturation Current (I_S)** is the small current that flows through the diode under reverse bias due to minority carrier drift. It is independent of the reverse voltage magnitude (up to breakdown) and is highly temperature-dependent, doubling for every **10 °C** rise in temperature. For Silicon, $I_S$ is in the nA range; for Germanium, in the µA range.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Avalanche and Zener Breakdown. [`KTU University Exam – Dec 2023`]

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Zener Breakdown | Avalanche Breakdown |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mechanism** | Quantum mechanical tunneling of electrons | Impact ionization / carrier multiplication |
| **Doping** | Heavily doped (narrow depletion region) | Lightly doped (wide depletion region) |
| **Voltage Range** | $V_Z < 5.5$ V | $V_Z > 5.5$ V |
| **Temperature Coeff.** | Negative (voltage decreases with temperature) | Positive (voltage increases with temperature) |
| **Junction Field** | Very high electric field | Moderate field with high carrier energy |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Diode Characteristics — Silicon Diode Focus [`KTU University Exam – June 2024`]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to obtain the forward and reverse V-I characteristics of a Silicon diode. What inferences can be drawn from the curve?

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.2 of these notes for the forward and reverse bias setups.)

**Procedure:**
1. Connect the circuit as shown with the diode in **forward bias** (P to +, N to –).
2. Vary the input DC voltage in steps of **0.1 V** from 0 to 1.5 V using a regulated power supply.
3. Record the corresponding forward current $I_F$ from the ammeter and forward voltage $V_F$ from the voltmeter.
4. Reverse the polarity of the supply to apply **reverse bias**.
5. Vary the input voltage in steps of **1 V** from 0 to the rated peak inverse voltage (e.g., 50 V for 1N4007).
6. Record reverse current $I_R$ and reverse voltage $V_R$.
7. Plot $I_F$ vs $V_F$ in the **first quadrant** and $I_R$ vs $V_R$ in the **third quadrant**.

**Inferences:**
- The forward curve remains near zero current until $V_F = V_k \approx 0.7$ V (knee voltage).
- After the knee, current rises sharply — confirming diode acts as a closed switch.
- In reverse bias, only a tiny leakage current ($I_S$) flows.
- The curve is **non-linear**, validating the Shockley equation $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_t} - 1)$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A Silicon diode has $I_S = 10$ nA at 300 K. Calculate the forward current when (i) $V_D = 0.5$ V and (ii) $V_D = 0.7$ V. Assume ideality factor $n = 1$.

**Model Solution:**

Using Shockley's equation:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_t}} - 1 \right)$$

With $I_S = 10 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $V_t = 26$ mV, $n = 1$:

**Case (i): $V_D = 0.5$ V:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &= 10 \times 10^{-9} \left( e^{\frac{0.5}{0.026}} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
I_D &= 10 \times 10^{-9} \left( e^{19.23} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
I_D &= 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (2.34 \times 10^{8}) \\[4pt]
I_D &= 2.34\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Case (ii): $V_D = 0.7$ V:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_D &= 10 \times 10^{-9} \left( e^{\frac{0.7}{0.026}} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
I_D &= 10 \times 10^{-9} \left( e^{26.92} - 1 \right) \\[4pt]
I_D &= 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (4.75 \times 10^{11}) \\[4pt]
I_D &= 4.75\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Inference:** A mere **0.2 V** increase in forward voltage causes the current to rise by **three orders of magnitude** — this exponential sensitivity is the defining property of diode characteristics.

### Question B: Zener Diode Characteristics & Voltage Regulator Design [`KTU University Exam – Dec 2023`]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Draw and explain the V-I characteristics of a Zener diode in both forward and reverse bias. Why is the Zener diode always operated in reverse bias?

**Model Solution:**

**Characteristics:** (Refer to Section 4.4 flow chart for the operational regions.)

In **forward bias**, the Zener diode behaves identically to a normal Silicon diode with $V_F \approx 0.7$ V. In **reverse bias**, the diode initially shows negligible current (only $I_S$), but as reverse voltage increases and reaches the **Zener voltage $V_Z$**, the curve bends sharply into a near-vertical region. Beyond this point, voltage remains constant at $V_Z$ over a wide range of current — this is the **breakdown region**.

**Why reverse bias?**
1. The defining property of a Zener diode is its ability to maintain a constant voltage $V_Z$ in breakdown.
2. This constant-voltage behavior is unique to the **reverse breakdown region**.
3. In forward bias, it behaves just like a normal diode and offers no special advantage.
4. Reverse bias operation is also safer due to the well-defined voltage-clamping action.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Design a Zener voltage regulator for the following specifications: Input DC voltage $V_S = 12$ V, Output voltage $V_O = 5.1$ V, Load current $I_L = 10$ mA to 50 mA. Assume Zener knee current $I_{ZK} = 5$ mA.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Zener Current Range:**
- Minimum Zener current = $I_{ZK} = 5$ mA (when load draws maximum)
- Maximum load current = 50 mA

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{Z(max)} &= I_L + I_{ZK} \\[4pt]
I_{Z(max)} &= 50 + 5 = 55\ \text{mA (when load is minimum 10 mA)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Series Resistance $R_S$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_S &= \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_{Z(max)}} \\[4pt]
R_S &= \frac{12 - 5.1}{55 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
R_S &= \frac{6.9}{0.055} \\[4pt]
R_S &= 125.45\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Choose Standard Resistor:** Use **R_S = 130 Ω, 0.5 W** (nearest standard E12 value).

**Step 4 — Zener Power Rating:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_Z &= V_Z \times I_{Z(max)} \\[4pt]
P_Z &= 5.1 \times 55 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
P_Z &= 0.28\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

Choose a Zener rated at **1 W** for safety.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Diode Equation:** Always convert $V_t$ to **Volts** (0.026 V) and $I_S$ to **Amperes** before substitution. Mixing nA and Volts without conversion is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 2. **Confusing $V_k$ for Si vs Ge:** Students often write 0.3 V as the knee voltage even for Silicon. **Always remember: Si = 0.7 V, Ge = 0.3 V.**
> 3. **Forgetting the "-1" in Shockley's Equation:** In forward bias ($V_D > 0.1$ V), the "-1" is negligible. But if the question gives a very small $V_D$ (e.g., 0.05 V), **retain the "-1"**.
> 4. **Zener Direction Confusion:** Many students connect the Zener in forward bias during the experiment. **Always connect CATHODE to the positive supply and ANODE to ground** for reverse-bias regulator operation.
> 5. **Power Rating Oversights:** A 0.28 W Zener requires a 1 W device due to derating — examiners deduct 1 mark if you don't include a safety margin.
> 6. **Plotting Errors:** In the lab record, the forward curve must be plotted with **V on x-axis** and **I on y-axis**, with appropriate scale breaks near zero (since $I_S$ is in nA but $I_F$ is in mA).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Diode V-I Curve is Non-Linear:** Always described by Shockley's exponential equation; never use Ohm's law.
- **Knee Voltage (Cut-in):** Silicon = **0.7 V**, Germanium = **0.3 V**. This is the "turn-on" threshold.
- **Reverse Saturation Current $I_S$:** Tiny (nA for Si), doubles every 10 °C temperature rise.
- **Zener Diode is operated in REVERSE bias** to exploit the constant-voltage breakdown property.
- **Zener Breakdown vs Avalanche Breakdown:** Zener dominates below 5.5 V; Avalanche above 5.5 V.
- **Dynamic Resistance Formula:** $r_d = nV_t / I_D$ — decreases with increasing forward current.
- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_t \approx 26$ mV at 300 K — memorize this value.
- **Power Dissipation in Zener:** $P_Z = V_Z \times I_Z$ — must include safety derating (typically 2× to 3×).
- **Series Resistor in Regulator:** $R_S = (V_S - V_Z) / (I_L + I_Z)$ — calculated using worst-case (maximum Zener current) scenario.
- **Lab Precautions:** Always use a current-limiting resistor; never exceed $V_{RRM}$ (rated reverse voltage); verify diode polarity (band = cathode) before powering the circuit.
- **Plotting Convention:** Forward bias → Quadrant I; Reverse bias → Quadrant III; Same graph, common origin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BJT Characteristics CE Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** BJT Characteristics CE Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Common Emitter (CE) Configuration** of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) is a fundamental three-terminal circuit arrangement in which the **emitter terminal is common to both the input and output loops**, serving as the reference point for input signal application (between base and emitter) and output signal measurement (between collector and emitter).

In the CE configuration, the input is applied between the **Base-Emitter (B-E) junction** (forward-biased) and the output is taken across the **Collector-Emitter (C-E) junction** (reverse-biased). The CE configuration is the most widely used BJT configuration because it provides **simultaneous current and voltage amplification**, making it the workhorse of analog amplifier design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The CE configuration is the standard arrangement where the input port is the base-emitter port and the output port is the collector-emitter port, with the emitter as the common reference. The static (DC) characteristics consist of **(i) Input Characteristics** and **(ii) Output Characteristics**, from which the small-signal **h-parameters** ($h_{ie}$, $h_{fe}$, $h_{re}$, $h_{oe}$) are graphically determined.

## 1.2 Two Sets of Static Characteristics

The performance of a BJT in CE configuration is completely described by two families of static curves:

| # | Characteristic Curve | X-Axis (Independent) | Y-Axis (Dependent) | Parameter Held Constant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Input Characteristic** | $V_{BE}$ (Base-Emitter Voltage) | $I_B$ (Base Current) | $V_{CE}$ (Collector-Emitter Voltage) |
| 2 | **Output Characteristic** | $V_{CE}$ (Collector-Emitter Voltage) | $I_C$ (Collector Current) | $I_B$ (Base Current) |
| 3 | **Transfer Characteristic** | $I_B$ (Base Current) | $I_C$ (Collector Current) | $V_{CE}$ (constant) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Valve Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (valve)** connected to a pipeline:

- The **base terminal** is like the **small control knob** of the tap — a tiny turn (small base current $I_B$) controls a massive flow of water (large collector current $I_C$).
- The **emitter** is the **common pipe connection** where both the control signal input and the main output flow share the same reference — hence "common emitter."
- The **collector** is the **main outlet** delivering the high-volume flow.
- The **collector-emitter voltage** $V_{CE}$ is analogous to the **pressure difference** driving the water through the main pipe.

Just as a small force on a tap handle releases a large volume of water, a small change in $I_B$ produces a large change in $I_C$ — this is **current amplification ($\beta$ or $h_{fe}$)**. The CE configuration is essentially a **current-controlled current source** with simultaneous voltage gain capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Thermal Voltage: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx$ **26 mV** at $T = 300\text{ K}$
> - Boltzmann Constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\text{ J/K}$
> - Charge of Electron: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$
> - Silicon BJT Base-Emitter Cut-in Voltage: $V_{BE(on)} \approx$ **0.7 V** (for Si)
> - Germanium BJT Base-Emitter Cut-in Voltage: $V_{BE(on)} \approx$ **0.3 V** (for Ge)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why "Common" Emitter?

In the CE configuration:
- The **input loop** (Base–Emitter loop) is forward-biased by $V_{BB}$, with $V_{BE}$ controlling $I_B$.
- The **output loop** (Collector–Emitter loop) is reverse-biased by $V_{CC}$, with $V_{CE}$ controlling $I_C$ for a given $I_B$.
- The emitter is physically common (grounded in AC small-signal analysis).

The relationship between collector and emitter currents follows the fundamental BJT current equation:

$$I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

where $I_S$ is the **reverse saturation current** of the B-E junction (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-15}$ A for small-signal silicon BJTs).

## 2.2 The Three Regions of Operation

The output characteristic plot divides into three distinct regions, which a KTU examiner will almost certainly test:

| Region | Condition | Behavior | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active Region** | B-E forward biased, C-B reverse biased | $I_C = \beta I_B$ (linear amplification) | Amplifier circuits |
| **Saturation Region** | Both junctions forward biased | $I_C$ no longer follows $I_B$; $V_{CE} \approx V_{CE(sat)} \approx$ **0.2 V** | Switch ON state |
| **Cut-off Region** | Both junctions reverse biased | $I_C \approx 0$; $I_B \approx 0$ | Switch OFF state |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula** required for the BJT CE Characteristics experiment — both for drawing curves and for computing h-parameters from the graphs.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Definition / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC Current Gain | $\beta_{DC} = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}\bigg\vert_{V_{CE}=\text{const}}$ | dimensionless | Slope from transfer characteristic |
| 2 | AC Current Gain | $\beta_{ac} = h_{fe} = \dfrac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}\bigg\vert_{V_{CE}=\text{const}}$ | dimensionless | Slope of output characteristic |
| 3 | Input Resistance | $h_{ie} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_B}\bigg\vert_{V_{CE}=\text{const}}$ | $\Omega$ (k$\Omega$) | Reciprocal slope of input characteristic |
| 4 | Reverse Voltage Ratio | $h_{re} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta V_{CE}}\bigg\vert_{I_B=\text{const}}$ | dimensionless | From input characteristics at two $V_{CE}$ values |
| 5 | Output Admittance | $h_{oe} = \dfrac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta V_{CE}}\bigg\vert_{I_B=\text{const}}$ | Siemens (mho, $\mu$S) | Slope of output characteristic in active region |
| 6 | Output Resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe}}$ | $\Omega$ (k$\Omega$) | Reciprocal of $h_{oe}$ |
| 7 | Emitter Current | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | A (mA, $\mu$A) | KCL at transistor |
| 8 | Emitter Efficiency | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | dimensionless | Common-base current gain (< 1) |
| 9 | $\alpha$–$\beta$ Relation | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ | dimensionless | Crucial inter-conversion |
| 10 | Early Voltage Effect | $I_C = I_{CO}\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right)$ | A | $V_A$ is the Early Voltage |
| 11 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26\text{ mV}$ at 300 K | V | Used in Ebers-Moll model |
| 12 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{h_{fe}}{h_{ie}}$ | Siemens (mA/V) | Small-signal parameter |
| 13 | Voltage Gain (CE) | $A_V = -g_m R_C = -h_{fe} \dfrac{R_C}{h_{ie}}$ | dimensionless | Negative sign = phase inversion |
| 14 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | W (mW) | Maximum rating consideration |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Relationship:** The h-parameters form the **hybrid (h) model** of the BJT because they have mixed units: $h_{ie}$ in ohms, $h_{re}$ dimensionless, $h_{fe}$ dimensionless, and $h_{oe}$ in siemens. Hence the name "hybrid."

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The CE configuration forms the heart of virtually every analog system:

- **Audio Amplifiers** — microphone pre-amps, op-amp input stages, push-pull audio output stages
- **RF Amplifiers** — tuned RF amplifiers in radio receivers
- **Switching Circuits** — digital logic gates (TTL), relay drivers, solenoid drivers
- **Oscillators** — Colpitts, Hartley, and Crystal oscillators all use CE stages
- **Power Electronics** — DC-DC converters, motor drivers, SMPS controllers
- **Sensor Signal Conditioning** — thermocouple and strain gauge amplifiers
- **Communication Systems** — IF amplifiers, AGC circuits, mixer stages
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the h-Parameters from the Graphical Method

The h-parameters are the **most common small-signal parameters** specified for BJTs at a given quiescent operating point (Q-point). They are obtained directly from the static characteristics by taking slopes at the Q-point.

### 3.1.1 Input Resistance $h_{ie}$

Consider the **input characteristic curve** at a fixed $V_{CE} = V_{CEQ}$. This curve plots $I_B$ on the y-axis versus $V_{BE}$ on the x-axis.

$$h_{ie} = \left.\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial I_B}\right|_{V_{CE} = V_{CEQ}} \approx \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_B}\bigg|_{V_{CE}=\text{const}}$$

**Step-by-step graphical method:**
1. Mark the Q-point $(V_{BEQ}, I_{BQ})$ on the input characteristic.
2. Draw a tangent line to the curve at the Q-point.
3. Select two convenient points on the tangent: $(V_{BE1}, I_{B1})$ and $(V_{BE2}, I_{B2})$.
4. Compute:

$$h_{ie} = \frac{V_{BE2} - V_{BE1}}{I_{B2} - I_{B1}} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_B}$$

The result is typically in **k$\Omega$** for small-signal BJTs (e.g., 1 k$\Omega$ to 5 k$\Omega$).

### 3.1.2 Forward Current Gain $h_{fe}$

From the **output characteristic** (family of $I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ curves at constant $I_B$):

$$h_{fe} = \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_B}\right|_{V_{CE} = V_{CEQ}} \approx \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}\bigg|_{V_{CE}=\text{const}}$$

**Step-by-step graphical method:**
1. On the output characteristic, select the Q-point curve at $I_B = I_{BQ}$.
2. Take two adjacent curves (e.g., $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$) intersecting the vertical line $V_{CE} = V_{CEQ}$ at points $(V_{CEQ}, I_{C1})$ and $(V_{CEQ}, I_{C2})$.
3. Compute:

$$h_{fe} = \frac{I_{C2} - I_{C1}}{I_{B2} - I_{B1}}$$

Typical values range from **50 to 300** for small-signal silicon BJTs.

### 3.1.3 Output Admittance $h_{oe}$

From the **output characteristic curve** corresponding to the base current $I_{BQ}$:

$$h_{oe} = \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{CE}}\right|_{I_B = I_{BQ}} \approx \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta V_{CE}}\bigg|_{I_B=\text{const}}$$

**Step-by-step graphical method:**
1. On the output curve for $I_B = I_{BQ}$, mark the Q-point $(V_{CEQ}, I_{CQ})$.
2. Draw a tangent to the curve at the Q-point (note: the slope is very small in the active region — this curve is nearly horizontal).
3. Select two points on the tangent and compute the slope.

The output resistance is then:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{h_{oe}}$$

Typical values: $h_{oe} \approx 25\text{ }\mu\text{S}$, giving $r_o \approx$ **40 k$\Omega$**.

### 3.1.4 Reverse Voltage Ratio $h_{re}$

This is the most subtle parameter and is found by comparing **two input characteristics** taken at two different values of $V_{CE}$.

$$h_{re} = \left.\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial V_{CE}}\right|_{I_B = I_{BQ}} \approx \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta V_{CE}}\bigg|_{I_B=\text{const}}$$

**Step-by-step graphical method:**
1. On the input characteristic plot, draw the horizontal line at $I_B = I_{BQ}$.
2. Find its intersection with the curve at $V_{CE} = V_{CE1}$ → reading $V_{BE1}$.
3. Find its intersection with the curve at $V_{CE} = V_{CE2}$ → reading $V_{BE2}$.
4. Compute:

$$h_{re} = \frac{V_{BE2} - V_{BE1}}{V_{CE2} - V_{CE1}}$$

Typical values: $h_{re} \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ (very small).

## 3.2 Derivations of Key Analytical Relationships

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the $\alpha$–$\beta$ Relationship

By Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) applied at the transistor:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

The common-base current gain $\alpha$ is defined as:

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E}$$

The common-emitter current gain $\beta$ is defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B}$$

**Step 1:** From the KCL equation:

$$I_B = I_E - I_C$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $I_E = I_C / \alpha$:

$$I_B = \frac{I_C}{\alpha} - I_C = I_C \left(\frac{1}{\alpha} - 1\right) = I_C \left(\frac{1 - \alpha}{\alpha}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{I_C}{I_C \left(\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\right)} = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $\alpha$ in terms of $\beta$:

$$\beta(1 - \alpha) = \alpha \implies \beta - \beta\alpha = \alpha \implies \beta = \alpha(1 + \beta)$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}}$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Worked Example — From Typical Lab Data

**Given:** For a silicon BJT in CE configuration, the following readings are obtained from the input characteristic at $V_{CE} = 2$ V:

| $V_{BE}$ (V) | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.65 | 0.7 | 0.75 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_B$ ($\mu$A) | 0 | 5 | 15 | 40 | 100 |

**Find:** The input resistance $h_{ie}$ at the Q-point $I_B = 40\text{ }\mu\text{A}$.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta V_{BE} = 0.75 - 0.65 = 0.10\text{ V}$$

$$\Delta I_B = (100 - 15)\text{ }\mu\text{A} = 85\text{ }\mu\text{A} = 85 \times 10^{-6}\text{ A}$$

$$h_{ie} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_B} = \frac{0.10}{85 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{h_{ie} \approx 1.176\text{ k}\Omega}$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example — $h_{fe}$ from Output Characteristics

**Given:** Output characteristics at $V_{CE} = 5$ V:

| $I_B$ ($\mu$A) | 20 | 40 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_C$ (mA) | 1.2 | 2.5 | 3.8 |

**Find:** $h_{fe}$ and $\beta_{DC}$ at the Q-point $I_B = 40\text{ }\mu\text{A}$.

**Solution:**

$$h_{fe} = \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B} = \frac{(3.8 - 1.2)\text{ mA}}{(60 - 20)\text{ }\mu\text{A}} = \frac{2.6 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{h_{fe} = 65}$$

$$\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B}\bigg|_{Q} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 62.5$$

**Note:** $h_{fe} \approx \beta_{DC}$ for small-signal operation in the active region. ✓

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define input and output characteristics of a BJT in CE configuration. Sketch typical curves and label the three regions of operation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **input characteristic** is the curve relating the base current $I_B$ to the base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ at a constant collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$. It resembles the forward-biased diode characteristic of the B-E junction. For a given $V_{CE}$, $I_B$ rises exponentially with $V_{BE}$, with cut-in voltage $\approx 0.7$ V for silicon.

The **output characteristic** is the family of curves relating the collector current $I_C$ to the collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ for various constant values of base current $I_B$. In the active region, $I_C$ is nearly independent of $V_{CE}$ (curves are nearly horizontal) and depends primarily on $I_B$ as $I_C = \beta I_B$.

The three regions of operation visible on the output characteristic are:
- **Cut-off region:** $I_B = 0$, both junctions reverse biased, $I_C \approx I_{CEO}$ (leakage current)
- **Saturation region:** $V_{CE} < V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V, $I_C$ independent of $I_B$
- **Active region:** $0.2\text{ V} < V_{CE} < V_{CEO}$, $I_C = \beta I_B$ (linear amplification)

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List the four h-parameters of a BJT in CE configuration and state their units. Why is the model called "hybrid"?**

**Model Answer:**

The four h-parameters of a BJT in CE configuration (also called the **hybrid-$\pi$ equivalent** in low-frequency form) are:

| Parameter | Name | Unit | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h_{ie}$ | Input resistance | ohm ($\Omega$) | $\Delta V_{BE} / \Delta I_B$ |
| $h_{re}$ | Reverse voltage ratio | dimensionless | $\Delta V_{BE} / \Delta V_{CE}$ |
| $h_{fe}$ | Forward current gain | dimensionless | $\Delta I_C / \Delta I_B$ |
| $h_{oe}$ | Output admittance | siemens (S) | $\Delta I_C / \Delta V_{CE}$ |

The model is called **"hybrid"** (h) because the four parameters have **mixed (hybrid) units** — two are dimensionless and one each is in ohms and siemens. The parameter set was standardized by the IEEE in the 1950s for two-port network characterization.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to obtain the **input and output characteristics of a BJT in CE configuration**. (7 Marks)

**Q (b)** From the given output characteristics, calculate $h_{ie}$, $h_{fe}$, $h_{oe}$ at the Q-point $I_{BQ} = 40\text{ }\mu\text{A}$, $V_{CEQ} = 5\text{ V}$. The following data is given:

Input Characteristic Data:

| $V_{BE}$ (V) | 0.55 | 0.60 | 0.65 | 0.70 | 0.75 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_B$ ($\mu$A) at $V_{CE} = 1\text{ V}$ | 5 | 12 | 25 | 50 | 95 |
| $I_B$ ($\mu$A) at $V_{CE} = 5\text{ V}$ | 3 | 8 | 18 | 38 | 78 |

Output Characteristic Data (at $V_{CE} = 5\text{ V}$):

| $I_B$ ($\mu$A) | 20 | 40 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_C$ (mA) | 1.4 | 2.8 | 4.2 |

(7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Circuit Description:**
The CE test circuit consists of:
- A variable DC supply $V_{BB}$ (0–12 V) connected to the base through a base resistor $R_B \approx 100$ k$\Omega$ to limit base current.
- A variable DC supply $V_{CC}$ (0–12 V) connected to the collector through a collector resistor $R_C \approx 1$ k$\Omega$.
- The emitter is **common** (grounded) — hence the name.
- Ammeters $A_B$ and $A_C$ measure $I_B$ and $I_C$; voltmeters $V_{BE}$ and $V_{CE}$ measure the respective voltages.

**Procedure for Input Characteristics:**
1. Set $V_{CE} = 0$ V initially by adjusting $V_{CC} = 0$ V.
2. Vary $V_{BB}$ in steps and record corresponding $V_{BE}$ and $I_B$ readings.
3. Repeat the procedure for $V_{CE} = 1, 2, 4, 6, 8$ V (keeping each value constant).
4. Plot $I_B$ (y-axis) versus $V_{BE}$ (x-axis) — these are the input characteristics.

**Procedure for Output Characteristics:**
1. Set $I_B = 0\text{ }\mu\text{A}$ by setting $V_{BB} = 0$ V.
2. Vary $V_{CC}$ in steps of 1 V from 0 to 12 V and record $I_C$ and $V_{CE}$.
3. Repeat for $I_B = 20, 40, 60, 80, 100\text{ }\mu\text{A}$.
4. Plot $I_C$ (y-axis) versus $V_{CE}$ (x-axis) — these are the output characteristics.

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

**Step 1: Calculate $h_{ie}$ at $V_{CEQ} = 5$ V**

Using the input characteristic data at $V_{CE} = 5$ V, take the slope around $I_{BQ} = 38\text{ }\mu\text{A}$:

$$\Delta V_{BE} = 0.75 - 0.65 = 0.10\text{ V}$$

$$\Delta I_B = (78 - 18)\text{ }\mu\text{A} = 60\text{ }\mu\text{A} = 60 \times 10^{-6}\text{ A}$$

$$h_{ie} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta I_B} = \frac{0.10}{60 \times 10^{-6}} = 1666.67\text{ }\Omega$$

$$\boxed{h_{ie} \approx 1.67\text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $h_{fe}$ at $V_{CEQ} = 5$ V, $I_{BQ} = 40\text{ }\mu\text{A}$**

From the output characteristic data:

$$\Delta I_C = (4.2 - 1.4)\text{ mA} = 2.8 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}$$

$$\Delta I_B = (60 - 20)\text{ }\mu\text{A} = 40 \times 10^{-6}\text{ A}$$

$$h_{fe} = \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B} = \frac{2.8 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 70$$

$$\boxed{h_{fe} = 70}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $h_{re}$ at $I_{BQ} = 40\text{ }\mu\text{A}$**

At $I_B = 38\text{ }\mu\text{A}$ (closest to 40 $\mu$A):

$$\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE}\big|_{V_{CE}=5V} - V_{BE}\big|_{V_{CE}=1V} = 0.70 - 0.70 = 0\text{ V}$$

Using more accurate data points: take $V_{BE}$ at $I_B = 38\text{ }\mu\text{A}$ on both curves. Since the data points are tabulated at fixed $V_{BE}$, interpolate.

For $I_B = 38\text{ }\mu\text{A}$ at $V_{CE} = 1$ V: between $(V_{BE}=0.65, I_B=25)$ and $(V_{BE}=0.70, I_B=50)$:

$$V_{BE1} = 0.65 + \frac{(38-25)}{(50-25)} \times 0.05 = 0.65 + \frac{13}{25} \times 0.05 = 0.676\text{ V}$$

For $I_B = 38\text{ }\mu\text{A}$ at $V_{CE} = 5$ V: between $(V_{BE}=0.70, I_B=38)$ — direct reading $V_{BE2} = 0.70$ V.

$$\Delta V_{BE} = 0.70 - 0.676 = 0.024\text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{CE} = 5 - 1 = 4\text{ V}$$

$$h_{re} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{\Delta V_{CE}} = \frac{0.024}{4} = 6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{h_{re} \approx 6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $h_{oe}$**

Since the output characteristic data is limited, we estimate $h_{oe}$ from the typical slope. If the $I_C$ varies by $\pm 0.2$ mA over a $V_{CE}$ range of $\pm 5$ V (active region approximation):

$$h_{oe} \approx \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 4 \times 10^{-5}\text{ S} = 40\text{ }\mu\text{S}$$

$$\boxed{h_{oe} \approx 40\text{ }\mu\text{S},\quad r_o = 1/h_{oe} = 25\text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Transconductance and Voltage Gain (bonus)**

$$g_m = \frac{h_{fe}}{h_{ie}} = \frac{70}{1666.67} = 42\text{ mA/V} = 0.042\text{ S}$$

$$A_V = -g_m R_C = -0.042 \times 1000 = -42$$

$$\boxed{A_V = -42 \text{ (phase inversion of 180°)}}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q (a)** Derive the relationship between the **common-base current gain $\alpha$** and the **common-emitter current gain $\beta$**. State the significance of $\alpha$ being close to unity. (7 Marks)

**Q (b)** A silicon BJT operating in CE configuration has the following h-parameters at the Q-point: $h_{ie} = 1.5$ k$\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 100$, $h_{oe} = 25$ $\mu$S. If $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega$ and the source resistance $R_S = 600\text{ }\Omega$, calculate: (i) current gain $A_I$, (ii) input resistance $R_{in}$, (iii) voltage gain $A_V$, and (iv) power gain $A_P$ in dB. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Derivation of $\alpha$–$\beta$ Relationship:**

Applying KCL at the transistor:

$$I_E = I_B + I_C \quad \text{...(1)}$$

By definition of the common-base current gain:

$$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} \implies I_C = \alpha I_E \quad \text{...(2)}$$

By definition of the common-emitter current gain:

$$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \implies I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Substituting (2) and (3) into (1):

$$\frac{I_C}{\alpha} = \frac{I_C}{\beta} + I_C$$

Dividing throughout by $I_C$:

$$\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\beta} + 1 = \frac{1 + \beta}{\beta}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\beta = \alpha(1 + \beta) \implies \boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}}$$

**Significance of $\alpha$ being close to unity:**
- For a typical silicon BJT, $\alpha \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$, which means $I_C$ is very close to $I_E$.
- This implies $I_B = I_E - I_C = I_C\left(\frac{1}{\alpha} - 1\right) = I_C \cdot \frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}$ is very small.
- A small change in $I_B$ produces a large change in $I_C$, giving large current gain $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$.
- The closer $\alpha$ is to 1, the larger $\beta$ becomes (e.g., $\alpha = 0.99 \implies \beta = 99$).
- Physically, this means most emitter-injected carriers reach the collector (high transport factor).

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

**Given:**
- $h_{ie} = 1.5$ k$\Omega = 1500\text{ }\Omega$
- $h_{fe} = 100$
- $h_{oe} = 25\text{ }\mu\text{S} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\text{ S}$
- $R_C = 2.2$ k$\Omega = 2200\text{ }\Omega$
- $R_S = 600\text{ }\Omega$

**Standard CE h-parameter formulas (exact):**

$$R_{in} = h_{ie} - \frac{h_{re} h_{fe}}{h_{oe} + \frac{1}{R_C}}$$

(Assuming $h_{re}$ is very small and neglecting it for approximation: $R_{in} \approx h_{ie}$.)

$$A_I = \frac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_C}$$

$$A_V = \frac{-h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe}) R_C} \approx \frac{-h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}}$$

$$A_P = A_V \cdot A_I$$

**Step 1: Current Gain $A_I$**

$$A_I = \frac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_C} = \frac{100}{1 + (25 \times 10^{-6})(2200)}$$

$$A_I = \frac{100}{1 + 0.055} = \frac{100}{1.055} = 94.79$$

$$\boxed{A_I \approx 94.8}$$

**Step 2: Input Resistance $R_{in}$**

Neglecting $h_{re}$ (since $h_{re} \ll 1$):

$$R_{in} \approx h_{ie} = 1500\text{ }\Omega = 1.5\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{in} \approx 1.5\text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 3: Voltage Gain $A_V$**

$$A_V = \frac{-h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe}) R_C}$$

Neglecting the $h_{re}$ term:

$$A_V \approx \frac{-h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie} + h_{ie} h_{oe} R_C} = \frac{-h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}(1 + h_{oe} R_C)}$$

$$A_V = \frac{-(100)(2200)}{1500(1.055)} = \frac{-220000}{1582.5} = -138.97$$

$$\boxed{A_V \approx -139 \text{ (negative sign indicates 180° phase inversion)}}$$

**Step 4: Power Gain $A_P$**

$$A_P = |A_V| \cdot |A_I| = 138.97 \times 94.79 = 13172.7$$

In decibels:

$$A_P \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(13172.7) = 10 \times 4.1196 = 41.2\text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{A_P \approx 13173 \text{ or } 41.2\text{ dB}}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the CE Characteristics Lab Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing $h_{fe}$ with $\beta_{DC}$** — $h_{fe}$ is the *small-signal* (AC) current gain obtained from the **slope** of output characteristics at constant $V_{CE}$, while $\beta_{DC}$ is the *large-signal* (DC) ratio $I_C/I_B$ at a specific Q-point. They are close but not identical.
> 2. **Sign error in $A_V$** — The CE amplifier is an **inverting amplifier**. Forgetting the negative sign in voltage gain loses a mark.
> 3. **Unit confusion** — $h_{oe}$ is in **siemens (S) or $\mu$S**, not ohms. $1/h_{oe}$ gives $r_o$ in ohms. Mixing these units is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Drawing the input characteristic the wrong way** — The input characteristic is **not a straight line**; it is exponential (resembles a diode curve). Drawing it as a straight line will be penalized.
> 5. **Forgetting to mark the Q-point on the graph** — The h-parameters depend on the Q-point chosen. Always explicitly mark and label the Q-point $(I_{BQ}, V_{CEQ})$ on the graph.
> 6. **Not labeling the three regions** — The output characteristic must clearly show **cut-off, active, and saturation** regions. Missing the saturation knee at $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V is a common error.
> 7. **Using the wrong pair of curves for $h_{re}$** — Students often pick the wrong $V_{CE}$ values. Always use the **two $V_{CE}$ levels** at which the input characteristics are drawn, and compute $\Delta V_{BE}$ at the **same $I_B$**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — BJT CE Characteristics:**

- **CE configuration** = emitter is common to input and output; input at B-E, output at C-E.
- **Two characteristics** are drawn: (1) **Input**: $I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$ at constant $V_{CE}$; (2) **Output**: $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ at constant $I_B$.
- **Input characteristic** resembles a forward-biased silicon diode curve with $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7$ V.
- **Output characteristic** is a family of nearly horizontal curves in the active region, with three regions: **cut-off** ($I_B = 0$), **active** ($I_C = \beta I_B$), **saturation** ($V_{CE} \approx 0.2$ V).
- The **four h-parameters** $h_{ie}, h_{re}, h_{fe}, h_{oe}$ are obtained from the slopes of the characteristics at the Q-point.
- **h = "hybrid"** because the units are mixed: ohms, dimensionless, dimensionless, siemens.
- **DC current gain** $\beta_{DC} = I_C/I_B$; **AC current gain** $h_{fe} = \Delta I_C / \Delta I_B \big|_{V_{CE}}$.
- **Relation** $\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ and $\alpha = \beta/(1+\beta)$.
- **Thermal voltage** $V_T \approx 26$ mV at room temperature (300 K).
- **Voltage gain** $A_V = -h_{fe} R_C / h_{ie}$ — the **negative sign indicates phase inversion** (180°).
- **Transconductance** $g_m = h_{fe}/h_{ie} = I_C/V_T$.
- **Early Effect** causes the output curves to slope slightly upward with $V_{CE}$ — characterized by the Early voltage $V_A$.
- **Practical lab values:** $h_{ie} \approx 1$–$5$ k$\Omega$; $h_{fe} \approx 50$–$300$; $h_{oe} \approx 20$–$50$ $\mu$S; $h_{re} \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- **Power dissipation limit:** $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C < P_{D(max)}$ — defines the **SOA (Safe Operating Area)** on the output characteristic.
- **Q-point selection** is critical: midpoint of the load line in the active region gives maximum undistorted output swing.
- **Load line equation** on the output characteristic: $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ (DC load line).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JFET/MOSFET Characteristics CS Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** JFET/MOSFET Characteristics CS Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (JFET – Junction Field Effect Transistor)

A **JFET (Junction Field Effect Transistor)** is a **unipolar, voltage-controlled, three-terminal semiconductor device** in which the conduction between the Drain (D) and Source (S) terminals is controlled by a reverse-biased P–N junction at the Gate (G). For an N-channel JFET, conduction occurs through an N-type channel whose effective cross-sectional area is modulated by the depletion region width, which in turn is controlled by the Gate-to-Source voltage $V_{GS}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> "A JFET is a high-input-impedance voltage-controlled device in which the gate–source reverse bias controls the drain current. In Common Source (CS) configuration, the input is applied between gate and source, and the output is taken from the drain with respect to source, providing a phase inversion of 180°."

### 1.2 Formal Definition (MOSFET – Metal Oxide Semiconductor FET)

A **MOSFET (Metal Oxide Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor)** is a **unipolar, voltage-controlled, four-terminal device** (G, D, S, Body/Bulk) in which the gate is **insulated** from the channel by a thin layer of silicon dioxide ($SiO_2$). For an **N-channel Enhancement-type MOSFET (E-MOSFET)**, no conducting channel exists at $V_{GS}=0$; conduction begins only when $V_{GS}$ exceeds the **threshold voltage $V_t$**.

### 1.3 Common Source (CS) Configuration — Definition

In the **CS configuration**, the **Source terminal is common** to both the input loop (Gate–Source) and the output loop (Drain–Source). The input signal is fed between Gate and Source; the output is observed between Drain and Source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CS configuration is the most widely used in KTU labs and industry:**
> 1. It provides **moderate-to-high voltage gain**.
> 2. It introduces a **180° phase inversion** between input and output.
> 3. It has a **very high input impedance** (≈ $10^9\,\Omega$ for JFET, ≈ $10^{12}\,\Omega$ for MOSFET).
> 4. It allows easy **bias point (Q-point) stabilization** using self-bias or voltage-divider bias.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voltage-Controlled Water Tap"

Imagine a **horizontal water pipe (the channel)** through which water flows from Drain to Source. A **flexible rubber sleeve (the depletion region)** squeezes the pipe from the sides. The squeezing is controlled by turning a knob at the **Gate terminal**:

| Mechanical Analogy | JFET/MOSFET Parameter |
|---|---|
| Water flow rate | Drain Current $I_D$ |
| Pressure across pipe | Drain–Source Voltage $V_{DS}$ |
| Squeezing knob position | Gate–Source Voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| Maximum flow (no squeezing) | $I_{DSS}$ (Drain current at $V_{GS}=0$) |
| Complete closure (no flow) | Pinch-off Voltage $V_P$ |

- For **JFET**: even with the knob fully released, water flows ($I_{DSS}$ exists). Turning the knob *reverse-biases* the junction and constricts the pipe.
- For **E-MOSFET**: the pipe is *completely shut* at $V_{GS}=0$. Water only starts flowing when the knob crosses a **threshold voltage $V_t$**.

### 1.5 Standardized Symbol Conventions

| Device | Symbol Key Point |
|---|---|
| N-channel JFET | Arrow on gate points **inward** (P-gate to N-channel) |
| P-channel JFET | Arrow on gate points **outward** |
| N-channel E-MOSFET | Broken channel line; arrow points inward |
| N-channel D-MOSFET | Continuous channel line; arrow points inward |

### 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Electron mobility in Si (N-channel):** $\mu_n \approx 1350\,\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$
- **Hole mobility in Si (P-channel):** $\mu_p \approx 480\,\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$
- **Permittivity of $SiO_2$:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{F/cm} \approx 3.45 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{F/cm}$
- **Standard $I_{DSS}$ range (small-signal JFETs like 2N3819, BF245):** $\mathbf{2\,mA}$ to $\mathbf{20\,mA}$
- **Standard Pinch-off Voltage $V_P$:** $\mathbf{-0.5\,V}$ to $\mathbf{-10\,V}$ (N-channel JFET, always negative)
- **Standard threshold voltage $V_t$ for E-MOSFETs:** $\mathbf{+0.5\,V}$ to $\mathbf{+5\,V}$ (N-channel)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 JFET — Operating Regions (Logical Breakdown)

The JFET operation is divided into **three principal regions**, which the KTU lab manual repeatedly tests:

**Region 1 — Ohmic / Triode Region:**
- Condition: $0 \leq V_{DS} \leq V_{DS(sat)}$, where $V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$
- The channel acts like a **variable resistor** controlled by $V_{GS}$.
- $I_D$ is approximately linear with $V_{DS}$.

**Region 2 — Saturation / Pinch-off Region (Active Region):**
- Condition: $V_{DS} \geq V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$
- The channel is **pinched off** at the drain end; $I_D$ becomes nearly constant.
- This is the region used for **amplification in CS configuration**.

**Region 3 — Cut-off Region:**
- Condition: $V_{GS} \leq V_P$ (for N-channel, $V_P$ is negative, so $V_{GS}$ must be sufficiently negative).
- $I_D \approx 0$ — the device is OFF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Region:**
> - Why is pinch-off at $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_P$? Because the reverse bias across the gate-channel junction at the drain end is $(V_{GS} - V_{DS})$; when this equals $V_P$, the depletion regions from both sides meet → channel closed.
> - Why does $I_D$ saturate and not drop to zero? Because the depletion region supports the voltage; the field continues to push carriers across the pinched-off region (a "saturated velocity" effect).

### 2.2 JFET — Shockley's Equation (The Master Formula)

In the **saturation (active) region**, the drain current is governed by the famous **Shockley's Square-Law Equation**:

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

where:
- $I_{DSS}$ = Drain current with Gate shorted to Source ($V_{GS}=0$) — units: **mA**
- $V_P$ = Pinch-off voltage — units: **V** (negative for N-channel)
- $V_{GS}$ = Gate–Source bias voltage — units: **V**

### 2.3 MOSFET (Enhancement-type) — Square-Law Equation

For an N-channel E-MOSFET in saturation:

$$I_D = K \cdot (V_{GS} - V_t)^2$$

where $K = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L}$ is the **conduction parameter** (units: **mA/V²**), $V_t$ is the threshold voltage, and $W/L$ is the aspect ratio.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** — memorize these for Part A and Part B.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Notes | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ | Shockley's Equation (JFET saturation) | Valid only when $V_P \leq V_{GS} \leq 0$ (N-ch) | mA |
| 2 | $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ | E-MOSFET saturation drain current | Valid when $V_{GS} \geq V_t$ | mA |
| 3 | $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ | Maximum transconductance (at $V_{GS}=0$) | $g_{m0}$ is the slope at origin of transfer curve | mA/V (mS) |
| 4 | $g_m = g_{m0} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | Transconductance at operating $V_{GS}$ | Used for small-signal gain | mA/V (mS) |
| 5 | $r_d = \dfrac{\Delta V_{DS}}{\Delta I_D}\big\vert_{V_{GS}=\text{const}}$ | Drain (AC output) resistance | From slope of $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ in saturation | k$\Omega$ |
| 6 | $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ | Amplification factor of JFET | Dimensionless | — |
| 7 | $V_{GS} = -I_D \cdot R_S$ | Self-bias gate voltage (JFET) | For simple self-bias CS amp | V |
| 8 | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_d)$ | CS voltage gain (small-signal) | Negative sign → 180° phase shift | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{in} = R_G$ | Input resistance of CS JFET amp | $R_G$ is gate-bias resistor | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \,\vert\vert\, r_d \,\vert\vert\, R_L)$ | Voltage gain with load $R_L$ | Including external load | dimensionless |
| 11 | $g_m = 2 \sqrt{K \cdot I_D}$ | MOSFET transconductance | Equivalent to derivative of $I_D$ w.r.t. $V_{GS}$ | mA/V |
| 12 | $g_m = 2 K (V_{GS} - V_t)$ | Alternative MOSFET $g_m$ form | Linear in overdrive $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_t$ | mA/V |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the backbone of designing **low-noise RF amplifiers** (JFETs), **power switching circuits** (power MOSFETs), **voltage-controlled current sources**, and the **input stage of operational amplifiers** (e.g., TL072 uses JFETs). The high input impedance makes FETs ideal for **electrometer-grade instrumentation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shockley's Equation (JFET Saturation Region)

We start with the geometry of an N-channel JFET of total channel width $2a$, doping $N_D$ (donors per cm³), and channel length $L$.

**Step 1 — Channel Half-Width Without Bias**
At $V_{GS} = 0$, the built-in potential $V_{bi}$ of the gate-channel P–N junction creates a depletion region of half-width $h_0$ on each side:

$$h_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}$$

where $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{F/cm}$ is the permittivity of silicon.

**Step 2 — Effective Channel Half-Width Under Bias $V_{GS}$**
When a reverse bias $V_{GS}$ is applied (N-channel JFET: $V_{GS} < 0$):

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Pinch-off Voltage $V_P$**
Pinch-off occurs when the depletion regions from both sides meet the channel center, i.e., $h = a$:

$$V_P = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Channel Width at Position $x$**
At any point $x$ along the channel, the local reverse bias across the junction is $V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V(x)$ (where $V(x)$ is the channel potential w.r.t. source). The half-width is:

$$h(x) = a \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V(x)}{V_{bi} - V_P}}$$

**Step 5 — Drain Current Integral**
The drain current (using gradual-channel approximation and Ohm's law):

$$I_D = G_0 \left[ V_{DS} - \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{(V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V_{DS})^{3/2} - (V_{bi} - V_{GS})^{3/2}}{(V_{bi} - V_P)^{1/2}} \right]$$

where $G_0 = \dfrac{2 q N_D \mu_n a Z}{L}$ is the open-channel conductance (channel width $2a$, depth $Z$).

**Step 6 — Saturation Approximation (Key Simplification)**
At pinch-off, the term simplifies. Applying the engineering approximation that the dominant physics is the gate-controlled channel width, we get the **empirical Shockley's square law**:

$$I_{D,sat} = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

where $I_{DSS}$ is measured at $V_{GS} = 0$. This equation is what every KTU lab manual uses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic Recap:** Geometry ($h \propto \sqrt{V_{bias}}$) → integration of channel current → saturation condition at $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_P$ → square-law dependence on $(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Transconductance $g_m$ from Shockley's Equation

**Step 1:** Write Shockley's equation.

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. $V_{GS}$ (with $V_{DS}$ held constant — small-signal condition).

$$\frac{dI_D}{dV_{GS}} = I_{DSS} \cdot 2 \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Simplify.

$$g_m = -\frac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that at $V_{GS} = 0$, the transconductance is maximum:

$$g_{m0} = -\frac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$$

(N-channel JFET: $V_P < 0$, so the minus sign cancels with negative denominator.)

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$g_m = g_{m0} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Amplification Factor $\mu$

**Step 1:** In the saturation region, $I_D$ is approximately independent of $V_{DS}$ (very high output resistance). Define:

$$\mu = -\left.\frac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial V_{GS}}\right|_{I_D = \text{const}}$$

**Step 2:** The chain rule gives:

$$\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$$

where $r_d = \dfrac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D}\bigg\vert_{V_{GS}}$ is the output (drain) resistance.

**Step 3:** Physical meaning: $\mu$ tells you how much $V_{DS}$ must change to keep $I_D$ constant when $V_{GS}$ changes by 1 V. For a 2N3819 JFET, $\mu \approx 50 - 200$.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem (Dec 2023 Model):** For a JFET with $I_{DSS} = 8\,\text{mA}$ and $V_P = -4\,\text{V}$, find (a) the drain current at $V_{GS} = -2\,\text{V}$, (b) $g_{m0}$, and (c) $g_m$ at the operating point.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Apply Shockley's equation.

$$I_D = 8 \left(1 - \frac{-2}{-4}\right)^2 = 8 \times (1 - 0.5)^2 = 8 \times 0.25 = 2\,\text{mA}$$

**Part (b):** Maximum transconductance at $V_{GS} = 0$:

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 \times 8}{4} = 4\,\text{mA/V} = 4\,\text{mS}$$

**Part (c):** Transconductance at $V_{GS} = -2\,\text{V}$:

$$g_m = 4 \times \left(1 - \frac{-2}{-4}\right) = 4 \times 0.5 = 2\,\text{mS}$$

**Verification via $g_m = 2\sqrt{I_{DSS} \cdot I_D} / \vert V_P \vert$:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 \sqrt{8 \times 2}}{4} = \frac{2 \times 4}{4} = 2\,\text{mS} \;\checkmark$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of JFET vs E-MOSFET

| Parameter | N-channel JFET | N-channel E-MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction at $V_{GS} = 0$ | YES (depletion device) | NO (enhancement device) |
| Control mechanism | Reverse-biased P-N junction | Insulated gate (capacitive) |
| Input impedance | $\approx 10^9\,\Omega$ | $\approx 10^{12}\,\Omega$ |
| Equation (saturation) | $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$ | $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ |
| Threshold parameter | $V_P$ (negative) | $V_t$ (positive) |
| Gate current | Small leakage (nA) | Essentially zero (pA) |
| Static electricity risk | Low | **HIGH** (can rupture oxide) |
| Common lab part numbers | 2N3819, BF245, BFW10 | IRF540, BS170, 2N7000 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Common Source JFET Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional block architecture** of a CS JFET amplifier stage, mapping all signal paths, biasing rails, and bypass elements that you would wire on your KTU lab breadboard.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inNode[Vin AC Signal Source] -->|coupling C1| gate1[JFET Gate]
    rgNode[Bias Resistor RG to Ground] --> gate1
    vddNode[VDD DC Supply] --> drain1[JFET Drain]
    rdNode[Drain Resistor RD] --> drain1
    source1[JFET Source] --> rsNode[Source Resistor RS]
    rsNode --> gndNode[Circuit Ground]
    csBypass[Byass Capacitor CS] --> rsNode
    drain1 -->|coupling C2| outNode[Vout AC Load]
    outNode --> rlNode[Load Resistor RL]
    rlNode --> gndNode
    gate1 --> src1[Source Terminal JFET]
    drain1 --> drn1[Drain Terminal JFET]
    src1 --> source1
```

### 4.2 CS Configuration — Bias and Signal Path Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    psA[DC Supply VDD plus] --> rdB[Drain Resistor RD]
    rdB --> drnB[JFET Drain]
    sigA[Input Signal Vin] --> c1A[Coupling Cap C1]
    c1A --> gateB[JFET Gate]
    gateB --> rgB[Gate Resistor RG]
    rgB --> gndB[Ground]
    drnB --> c2A[Coupling Cap C2]
    c2A --> outA[Output Vout]
    outA --> rlA[Load RL]
    rlA --> gndB
    srcB[JFET Source] --> rsB[Source Resistor RS]
    rsB --> gndB
    srcB --> csA[Byass Cap CS]
    csA --> gndB
```

### 4.3 Drain Characteristics — Procedural Flow

The experiment plots $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ for several fixed $V_{GS}$ values. The flow is:

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start Lab Procedure] --> set1[Step 1 Set VGS to Zero]
    set1 --> vary1[Step 2 Vary VDS from 0 to 15 V]
    vary1 --> rec1[Step 3 Record ID at each VDS]
    rec1 --> step2[Step 4 Repeat for VGS minus 0.5 V]
    step2 --> step3[Step 5 Repeat for VGS minus 1 V]
    step3 --> step4[Step 6 Continue till ID approaches zero]
    step4 --> plotA[Step 7 Plot ID vs VDS curves]
    plotA --> paramA[Step 8 Extract IDSS, Vp, rd from graph]
    paramA --> doneA[Lab Completed]
```

### 4.4 Transfer Characteristics — Procedural Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startB[Start Transfer Plot] --> fix1[Step 1 Fix VDS at constant value 10 V]
    fix1 --> var1[Step 2 Vary VGS from 0 to VP]
    var1 --> rec2[Step 3 Record corresponding ID]
    rec2 --> plotB[Step 4 Plot ID vs VGS]
    plotB --> shock[Step 5 Verify Shockley equation fit]
    shock --> gm0Step[Step 6 Calculate gm0 and gm at Q point]
    gm0Step --> doneB[Transfer Characteristics Done]
```

### 4.5 Expected Graphical Output (Drain and Transfer Characteristics)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Manual — Expected Plot:** You will draw two graph sheets:
> 1. **Drain Characteristics** — A family of curves, each for a fixed $V_{GS}$ (0 V, −0.5 V, −1 V, … , $V_P$). Each curve has a linear (ohmic) part, then bends into a horizontal (saturation) part.
> 2. **Transfer Characteristics** — A parabolic curve in the third quadrant for N-channel JFET, peaking at $I_{DSS}$ when $V_{GS}=0$ and reaching zero at $V_{GS} = V_P$.

**Key Graphical Landmarks to Mark:**
- $I_{DSS}$ point on the topmost curve where $V_{GS} = 0$
- Pinch-off point where each curve just bends
- Q-point on the transfer curve (operating point)
- Slope of the transfer curve at the Q-point = $g_m$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each — Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define the term "pinch-off voltage" $V_P$ in a JFET. Why is it always negative for an N-channel JFET?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pinch-off voltage $V_P$ is the value of reverse gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ at which the drain current $I_D$ is reduced to zero (cut-off condition) for $V_{DS}$ greater than the saturation value. For an N-channel JFET, the channel consists of N-type material and the gate is P-type. A **negative** $V_{GS}$ reverse-biases the P-N junction, widening the depletion region. As $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative, the depletion region eventually consumes the entire channel, cutting off conduction. Hence $V_P$ must be negative to ensure the channel is fully pinched off. **Standard range:** $V_P \in [-0.5\,\text{V},\; -10\,\text{V}]$ for small-signal JFETs.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Differentiate between a JFET and an E-MOSFET based on (a) conduction at $V_{GS}=0$ and (b) input impedance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | JFET | E-MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Conduction at $V_{GS}=0$ | Conduction exists; $I_D = I_{DSS}$ (depletion-mode device) | No conduction; channel is induced only when $V_{GS} > V_t$ (enhancement-mode) |
| (b) Input Impedance | $\approx 10^8$ to $10^{10}\,\Omega$ (limited by reverse-biased junction leakage) | $\approx 10^{12}$ to $10^{15}\,\Omega$ (insulated gate — almost infinite DC) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Full Numerical / Analytical)

> **Important:** Under KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module Internal Question carries **14 marks** with a choice between two questions. Each sub-part is usually **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** For an N-channel JFET, the following readings are obtained from the drain characteristics experiment:

| $V_{DS}$ (V) | $I_D$ (mA) at $V_{GS}=0$ | $I_D$ (mA) at $V_{GS}=-1$ V | $I_D$ (mA) at $V_{GS}=-2$ V |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 3.20 | 1.80 | 0.80 |
| 5 | 4.00 | 2.20 | 1.00 |
| 8 | 4.10 | 2.25 | 1.02 |
| 12 | 4.12 | 2.27 | 1.03 |

> From the table, determine:
> 1. The saturation drain current $I_{DSS}$ and pinch-off voltage $V_P$.
> 2. Verify Shockley's equation for $V_{GS} = -1$ V and $V_{GS} = -2$ V.
> 3. The drain resistance $r_d$ at $V_{GS} = 0$ in the saturation region.

**Model Solution:**

**1) From the table at $V_{GS} = 0$ V:** As $V_{DS}$ increases, $I_D$ saturates at $\approx 4.12\,\text{mA}$ for $V_{DS} \geq 5$ V. Hence:

$$I_{DSS} \approx 4.10\,\text{mA}$$

**At $V_{GS} = -2$ V:** $I_D$ saturates at $\approx 1.02\,\text{mA}$. Applying Shockley's equation to find $V_P$:

$$1.02 = 4.10 \left(1 - \frac{-2}{V_P}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{1.02}{4.10} = \left(1 + \frac{2}{V_P}\right)^2$$

$$0.2488 = \left(1 + \frac{2}{V_P}\right)^2$$

$$1 + \frac{2}{V_P} = \pm 0.4988 \Rightarrow \frac{2}{V_P} = -0.5012 \Rightarrow V_P \approx -3.99\,\text{V} \approx -4\,\text{V}$$

So $V_P \approx -4$ V.

**2) Verification at $V_{GS} = -1$ V:**

$$I_D = 4.10 \left(1 - \frac{-1}{-4}\right)^2 = 4.10 \times (0.75)^2 = 4.10 \times 0.5625 = 2.31\,\text{mA}$$

Experimental value = 2.25 mA → **error ≈ 2.6%** ✓

**Verification at $V_{GS} = -2$ V:**

$$I_D = 4.10 \times (0.5)^2 = 4.10 \times 0.25 = 1.025\,\text{mA}$$

Experimental value = 1.02 mA → **error ≈ 0.5%** ✓

**3) Drain resistance at $V_{GS} = 0$** in saturation (using $V_{DS} = 8$ V to 12 V):

$$r_d = \frac{\Delta V_{DS}}{\Delta I_D} = \frac{12 - 8}{4.12 - 4.10} = \frac{4}{0.02} = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Using the JFET parameters extracted above, design a self-biased CS amplifier to operate at a quiescent drain current of $I_{DQ} = 2\,\text{mA}$ with $V_{DD} = 15\,\text{V}$. Assume $R_D = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$ and find (i) the required source resistance $R_S$, (ii) the quiescent drain-to-source voltage $V_{DSQ}$, and (iii) the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ if $r_d = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_{DSS} = 4.1\,\text{mA}$, $V_P = -4\,\text{V}$, $V_{DD} = 15\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $I_{DQ} = 2\,\text{mA}$, $r_d = 200\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Find $V_{GSQ}$ for $I_{DQ} = 2$ mA** using Shockley's equation:

$$2 = 4.1 \left(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{-4}\right)^2$$

$$\left(1 + \frac{V_{GSQ}}{4}\right)^2 = 0.4878$$

$$1 + \frac{V_{GSQ}}{4} = 0.6984 \Rightarrow \frac{V_{GSQ}}{4} = -0.3016 \Rightarrow V_{GSQ} \approx -1.21\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Find $R_S$ from self-bias equation** $V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} \cdot R_S$:

$$R_S = -\frac{V_{GSQ}}{I_{DQ}} = \frac{1.21}{2\,\text{mA}} = 605\,\Omega \approx 620\,\Omega\,\text{(standard E12)}$$

**Step 3 — Quiescent $V_{DSQ}$:**

$$V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = 15 - 2 \times (2.2 + 0.62) = 15 - 5.64 = 9.36\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $g_m$ at the Q-point:**

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2 \times 4.1}{4} = 2.05\,\text{mS}$$

$$g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\right) = 2.05 \times \left(1 - \frac{-1.21}{-4}\right) = 2.05 \times 0.6975 = 1.43\,\text{mS}$$

**Step 5 — Voltage gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \mid\mid r_d) = -1.43 \times (2.2 \mid\mid 200) = -1.43 \times 2.176 = -3.11\,\text{V/V}$$

In decibels: $A_v\,\text{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(3.11) = 9.85\,\text{dB}$.

> **[!WARNING] — KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention for $V_{GSQ}$:** In self-bias, $V_{GSQ}$ is **always negative** for N-channel JFET. Forgetting the negative sign flips the sign of $R_S$ — **−2 marks** typical deduction.
> 2. **Parallel resistance calculation:** Students often write $R_D + r_d$ instead of $R_D \mid\mid r_d$. Use $\frac{R_D \cdot r_d}{R_D + r_d}$ carefully.
> 3. **Sign of voltage gain:** The negative sign in $A_v = -g_m(R_D \mid\mid r_d)$ denotes the **180° phase inversion** — examiners award 1 mark for stating this explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Draw the drain and transfer characteristics of an N-channel JFET. Mark $I_{DSS}$, $V_P$, and the operating point clearly. Explain the three regions of operation.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Drain Characteristics** — A family of curves with $V_{DS}$ on x-axis and $I_D$ on y-axis, one curve per $V_{GS}$ value.

- For $V_{GS} = 0$ V → topmost curve, saturates at $I_{DSS}$.
- For $V_{GS} = V_P$ (most negative) → bottom curve, $I_D \approx 0$.

**Three regions marked on each curve:**

| Region | Boundary Condition | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Ohmic / Triode** | $0 \leq V_{DS} \leq V_{GS} - V_P$ | Channel acts as voltage-controlled resistor; $I_D$ rises linearly with $V_{DS}$ |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_P$ | Channel pinched-off at drain end; $I_D$ nearly constant; used for **amplification** |
| **Cut-off** | $V_{GS} \leq V_P$ | Channel fully depleted; $I_D \approx 0$; device is OFF |

**(ii) Transfer Characteristics** — $I_D$ on y-axis, $V_{GS}$ on x-axis, plotted for constant $V_{DS}$ in the saturation region.

- Parabolic (square-law) curve, given by $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$.
- At $V_{GS} = 0$: $I_D = I_{DSS}$ (point A on y-axis).
- At $V_{GS} = V_P$: $I_D = 0$ (point B on x-axis).
- Slope at any point = $g_m$ at that bias.
- Slope at $V_{GS} = 0$ = $g_{m0}$ (maximum).

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** An N-channel E-MOSFET has $V_t = 2$ V and $K = 0.5\,\text{mA/V}^2$. For $V_{GS} = 4$ V, find (i) the drain current, (ii) the transconductance, and (iii) the drain resistance if $V_{DS} = 10$ V and $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Drain Current:**

$$I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = 0.5 \times (4 - 2)^2 = 0.5 \times 4 = 2\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Transconductance:**

$$g_m = 2K(V_{GS} - V_t) = 2 \times 0.5 \times 2 = 2\,\text{mS}$$

(Verification: $g_m = 2\sqrt{K I_D} = 2\sqrt{0.5 \times 2} = 2 \times 1 = 2\,\text{mS}$ ✓)

**Step 3 — Drain Resistance (with channel-length modulation):**

$$r_d = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 2} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Amplification Factor (bonus):**

$$\mu = g_m \cdot r_d = 2 \times 25 = 50$$

> **[!WARNING] — Common Mistakes in MOSFET Problems:**
> 1. Confusing $V_t$ (threshold) with $V_P$ (pinch-off). They are **not** the same parameter — pinch-off is for JFET, threshold is for MOSFET.
> 2. Forgetting the factor of 2 in $g_m = 2K(V_{GS} - V_t)$. Many students write $g_m = K(V_{GS} - V_t)$.
> 3. Using $\lambda = 0$ (ideal case) when the question asks for $r_d$ — examiners explicitly ask for finite $\lambda$ in 2024 papers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This in Your Notebook!)**

- [x] **JFET = depletion device; conducts at $V_{GS}=0$.** E-MOSFET = enhancement device; conducts only when $V_{GS} > V_t$.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$ — memorize it!
- [x] **Three Regions:** Ohmic ($V_{DS} \leq V_{GS} - V_P$), Saturation ($V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_P$), Cut-off ($V_{GS} \leq V_P$).
- [x] **CS Configuration** provides voltage gain with 180° phase reversal.
- [x] **$g_{m0} = 2 I_{DSS} / \vert V_P \vert$** — maximum transconductance at $V_{GS}=0$.
- [x] **$g_m = g_{m0}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)$** — transconductance at operating point.
- [x] **$\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$** — amplification factor (dimensionless).
- [x] **Self-bias equation:** $V_{GSQ} = -I_{DQ} R_S$ (negative for N-channel JFET).
- [x] **Voltage gain:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \mid\mid r_d)$; negative sign = 180° phase shift.
- [x] **Input resistance of CS JFET amp = $R_G$** (since gate current is essentially zero).
- [x] **MOSFET square law:** $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ — substitute and solve quadratic.
- [x] **MOSFET $g_m$:** Use $g_m = 2K(V_{GS}-V_t) = 2\sqrt{K I_D}$.
- [x] **MOSFET $r_d$:** Use $r_d = 1/(\lambda I_D)$ when channel-length modulation is included.
- [x] **Lab Practical Tip:** Always **discharge your body** by touching the grounded chassis before handling MOSFETs — static electricity can destroy the gate oxide!
- [x] **Reading the drain curves:** The slope in the saturation region is $1/r_d$ (very small slope → high $r_d$, typically 50 k$\Omega$ to 500 k$\Omega$ for a JFET).
- [x] **Most common JFET in KTU labs:** **2N3819** ($I_{DSS} \approx 10$ mA, $V_P \approx -3.5$ V).
- [x] **Most common E-MOSFET in KTU labs:** **IRF540** (power) or **BS170** (small-signal, $V_t \approx 2$ V).

> **Final Exam Mnemonic:** **"Square Law, Saturation, Self-bias, S-curve"** — these four S-words define every JFET CS problem on a KTU paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled Amplifier
topics.content:
