## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
Define an RC differentiator. State the condition under which an RC circuit acts as a differentiator and write the expression for its output voltage.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **RC differentiator** is a high-pass RC network in which the output is taken across the resistor $R$ and the time constant $RC$ is much smaller than the time period $T$ of the input signal. Under this condition, the output voltage is proportional to the time-derivative of the input voltage. The expression is:

$$V_o(t) = RC \cdot \frac{dV_i(t)}{dt}$$

The condition is $RC \ll T$, typically chosen as $RC = T/10$. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
List any three applications of RC integrators in electronic circuits.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three applications of RC integrators are:
1. **Sawtooth and triangular wave generators** used in time-base circuits of CROs and TVs.
2. **Analogue computers** to perform mathematical integration of input signals.
3. **Ramp generators** used in ADC (analog-to-digital converter) circuits and 555-timer astable multivibrators.

Additional valid answers: low-pass filter for power-supply smoothing, integrator in PID controllers. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of an RC integrator circuit. State the design condition for integration. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an RC integrator for a square wave input. Sketch the input and output waveforms. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider the RC integrator circuit: input $V_i$ is applied across the series combination of resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$, with output $V_o$ taken across $C$. Applying KVL:

$$V_i(t) = V_R(t) + V_o(t) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

By Ohm's law, $V_R(t) = i(t) \cdot R$. The capacitor voltage is the time integral of the current:

$$V_o(t) = V_C(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\, d\tau \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Design condition:** For integration, the time constant must be much greater than the time period: $RC \gg T$ (typically $RC = 10T$).

**Applying the condition:** Since $RC \gg T$, the output voltage $V_o$ across the capacitor changes very slowly. Most of the input voltage drops across the resistor:

$$V_R(t) \approx V_i(t) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Therefore, the current is approximately:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_R(t)}{R} \approx \frac{V_i(t)}{R} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Substituting (iv) into (ii):

$$V_o(t) = \frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} \frac{V_i(\tau)}{R}\, d\tau = \frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_i(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_o(t) = \frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_i(\tau)\, d\tau}$$

This proves that the output is the scaled integral of the input, with scaling factor $1/RC$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Operation with Square Wave and Waveforms (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Diagram:** (Draw the integrator circuit with $R$ in series and $C$ to ground, output across $C$)

**Operation:** When a square wave of amplitude $\pm V_m$ is applied:
- During the **positive half-cycle** ($V_i = +V_m$ for time $T/2$): the capacitor charges through $R$ with a nearly constant current $i = V_m/R$ (because $RC \gg T/2$, the voltage across $C$ remains small). The output rises **linearly** with slope $V_m/(RC)$:

$$V_o(t) = \frac{V_m}{RC} \cdot t \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T/2$$

- During the **negative half-cycle** ($V_i = -V_m$): the capacitor discharges linearly through $R$. The output falls **linearly** with slope $-V_m/(RC)$.

**Waveform Sketch:**

```
   Vi (Square Wave)              Vo (Triangular Wave)
   +Vm |‾‾‾‾|   |‾‾‾‾|              /|   /|
       |   |   |   |              / |  / |
      0|___|___|___|___        0 /__|_/__|___
       |   |   |   |            /  | /  | 
   -Vm |___|___|___|              / |/   |/
```

**Peak output voltage:**

$$V_{o,\text{peak}} = \frac{V_m \cdot T/2}{RC} = \frac{V_m}{2RCf}$$

For $RC = 10T$, the peak output is $V_m/20$, a small fraction of the input — confirming the linear approximation is valid. **(7 Marks)**

---

**PART B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of an RC differentiator circuit. State the design condition for differentiation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an RC differentiator for a square wave input. Sketch the input and output waveforms. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider the RC differentiator: input $V_i$ is applied across the series combination of capacitor $C$ and resistor $R$, with output $V_o$ taken across $R$. Applying KVL:

$$V_i(t) = V_C(t) + V_o(t) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

The current through the series circuit is the same through both elements:

$$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} = \frac{V_o(t)}{R} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

From (ii): $V_o(t) = R \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt} = RC \cdot \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$ ...(iii)

**Design condition:** $RC \ll T$ (typically $RC = T/10$).

**Applying the condition:** Since $RC \ll T$, the capacitor charges and discharges almost instantaneously. The voltage across the capacitor follows the input very closely: $V_C(t) \approx V_i(t)$. Therefore:

$$\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{dV_i(t)}{dt} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Substituting (iv) into (iii):

$$\boxed{V_o(t) = RC \cdot \frac{dV_i(t)}{dt}}$$

This proves that the output is the scaled time-derivative of the input, with scaling factor $RC$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Operation with Square Wave and Waveforms (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Diagram:** (Draw the differentiator circuit with $C$ in series and $R$ to ground, output across $R$)

**Operation:** When a square wave with sharp edges is applied:
- At the **rising edge** of the square wave ($dV_i/dt = +\infty$ in the ideal case, but finite in practice): the output jumps to a **positive spike** of peak value $V_{o,\text{peak}} \approx V_m$ (where $V_m$ is the square wave amplitude).
- During the **flat top** of the square wave ($dV_i/dt = 0$): the output decays exponentially to zero with time constant $\tau = RC$.
- At the **falling edge** ($dV_i/dt = -\infty$): the output jumps to a **negative spike** of peak value $-V_m$.
- During the **flat bottom**: output decays back to zero exponentially.

**Waveform Sketch:**

```
   Vi (Square Wave)              Vo (Differentiator - Spikes)
   +Vm |‾‾‾‾|   |‾‾‾‾|            |  |   |  |
       |   |   |   |             |  |   |  |
      0|___|___|___|___        0_|__|___|__|___
       |   |   |   |              |  |   |  |
   -Vm |___|___|___|              |  |   |  |
                                  Positive Negative Positive Negative
                                  Spike    Spike    Spike    Spike
```

The spikes occur at the **transitions** of the input, and their amplitude depends on the rate of change of input voltage. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the condition:** Many students write the output expression $V_o = RC \cdot dV_i/dt$ but forget to mention that this is valid **only when $RC \ll T$**. Examiners typically deduct **2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Confusing the output node:** A very common error is stating that "for a differentiator, output is across the capacitor." This is **WRONG** — differentiator output is across $R$; integrator output is across $C$. Deduct **1 mark** for this swap.
> 3. **Sign convention error:** When deriving the integrator output, students often forget the initial condition $V_C(0) = 0$ assumption. State this explicitly to avoid losing marks.
> 4. **Waveform scaling:** In sketches, the spike amplitude of the differentiator and the ramp slope of the integrator must be drawn **proportionally**. An exaggerated spike or a flat triangular wave will cost you **1 mark** in the diagram.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Always quote $RC$ in **seconds** and $f_c$ in **Hertz** — mixing units (e.g., ms with kHz) is a frequent source of avoidable mark loss.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **RC differentiator** is a **High-Pass Filter** with output across $R$; condition is $RC \ll T$.
- ✅ An **RC integrator** is a **Low-Pass Filter** with output across $C$; condition is $RC \gg T$.
- ✅ Differentiator output formula: $\;V_o = RC \cdot \dfrac{dV_i}{dt}\;$
- ✅ Integrator output formula: $\;V_o = \dfrac{1}{RC} \displaystyle\int V_i\, dt\;$
- ✅ Cutoff frequency of either circuit: $\;f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}\;$
- ✅ **Memory trick:** *DR* (Differentiator across *R*) and *IC* (Integrator across *C*).
- ✅ Square wave input to differentiator → **spike/pulse output** at edges.
- ✅ Square wave input to integrator → **triangular/ramp output**.
- ✅ Sine wave input to differentiator → **cosine output** (90° phase lead, scaled by $\omega RC$).
- ✅ Sine wave input to integrator → **negative cosine output** (90° phase lag, scaled by $1/\omega RC$).
- ✅ Lab design rule: $RC = T/10$ for differentiator; $RC = 10T$ for integrator.
- ✅ Components needed: function generator, CRO, resistor box, capacitor box, connecting wires, breadboard.
- ✅ Always check the cutoff frequency $f_c$ relative to $f_{in}$ before concluding the circuit operation.
- ✅ For full marks in KTU exams: state the **condition** + write the **formula** + sketch the **waveform** with **proportionate scaling** + derive the **expression step-by-step**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic OPAMP Circuits (Inverting, Non-Inverting, Voltage Follower, Summing Amplifier)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic OPAMP Circuits (Inverting, Non-Inverting, Voltage Follower, Summing Amplifier)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Operational Amplifier (OPAMP) — The Building Block

An **Operational Amplifier (OPAMP)** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with a differential input and a single-ended output, typically packaged as an integrated circuit (IC). The most widely used general-purpose OPAMP is the **µA741** (or its modern equivalents like LM741, TL081). It has five basic terminals: two inputs (**Inverting (-)** and **Non-Inverting (+)**), one output, and two power supply pins (**+Vcc** and **-Vee**, usually ±12 V or ±15 V).

The internal open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL}$ of an ideal OPAMP is **infinite** ($\infty$), while in a practical 741 it is around **200,000 (≈ 106 dB)**. In the lab, the OPAMP is rarely used in open-loop; instead, we use **negative feedback** through external resistors to precisely set the closed-loop gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBML307):** The four basic OPAMP circuits covered in this experiment form the *foundation* of all analog signal processing — every filter, oscillator, ADC driver, and instrumentation amplifier is built from these four topologies.

### 1.2 The Four Basic OPAMP Circuits — Intuitive Overview

| Circuit | One-Line Intuition | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | Output is a flipped, scaled copy of the input | A *fun-house mirror* — reverses and shrinks the image |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | Output is a same-phase, scaled copy of the input | A *megaphone* — same voice, only louder |
| **Voltage Follower (Buffer)** | Output exactly equals input, but with infinite current capability | A *water tower* — same water level, can serve the whole city |
| **Summing Amplifier** | Output is the weighted sum of multiple inputs | A *kitchen mixing bowl* — adds ingredients in fixed proportions |

### 1.3 Ideal OPAMP Assumptions (The Two Golden Rules)

For all derivations in this experiment, we rely on two simplifying assumptions of an **ideal OPAMP**:

1. **Infinite Input Impedance** → Input current into the (+) and (−) terminals is **zero**: $I_{+} = I_{-} = 0 \, A$
2. **Infinite Open-Loop Gain** → With negative feedback, the differential input voltage is **zero**: $V_{+} = V_{-}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical 741 Limitations (must be remembered for lab records):**
> - Input Bias Current: ~80 nA
> - Input Offset Voltage: ~2 mV (causes small DC error at output)
> - Slew Rate: 0.5 V/µs (limits high-frequency operation)
> - Output Voltage Swing: ±13 V (when powered by ±15 V)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Negative Feedback?

In open-loop, even a 1 mV differential input would saturate the output to ±Vcc. We need **negative feedback** — feeding a portion of the output back to the *inverting* terminal — to:

- Stabilize the gain against temperature and process variations
- Linearize the transfer characteristic
- Control input and output impedance
- Extend bandwidth

The closed-loop gain is set entirely by **external passive components** (resistors), not the OPAMP's internal transistor parameters.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Circuit Topology | Closed-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_v$) | Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) | Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) | Key Design Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $Z_{in} = R_1$ | Near zero (OPAMP output) | $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_f$ (bias compensation) |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $Z_{in} = \infty$ (ideal) | Near zero (OPAMP output) | $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_f$ |
| **Voltage Follower** | $A_v = 1$ (Unity) | $Z_{in} = \infty$ | Near zero | $R_f = 0 \, \Omega$, $R_1 = \infty$ |
| **Summing Amplifier (n inputs)** | $V_o = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2}V_2 + \dots + \dfrac{R_f}{R_n}V_n\right)$ | $Z_{in,i} = R_i$ | Near zero | If all $R_i = R$, then $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R}\sum V_i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-winning tip:** Always remember the **negative sign** for the inverting and summing amplifier outputs. Many students lose marks by missing the phase inversion in $V_o$.

### 2.3 Why is the Inverting Amplifier's Input Impedance only $R_1$?

In the inverting configuration, the inverting terminal is held at **virtual ground** ($V_- \approx 0$) because $V_+ = 0$ and $V_- = V_+$. Therefore, the source sees only the input resistor $R_1$ to ground. This is a key drawback compared to the non-inverting amplifier, whose input impedance is much higher.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inverting Amp**: Used in **active filters**, **D/A converter output stages**, **audio mixers**
- **Non-Inverting Amp**: Used in **sensor signal conditioning** (high input impedance doesn't load the sensor)
- **Voltage Follower**: Used as a **buffer** between a high-impedance source (e.g., piezo sensor) and a low-impedance load (e.g., ADC)
- **Summing Amp**: Used in **audio mixing consoles**, **digital-to-analog converters (weighted-resistor DAC)**, and **PID controller summing junctions**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Inverting Amplifier

**Circuit:** Input signal $V_{in}$ connected to the inverting (−) terminal through resistor $R_1$. Feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting terminal. Non-inverting (+) terminal grounded.

**Given:** Ideal OPAMP, negative feedback active.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (let's call it node A):**

$$
I_{R_1} + I_{R_f} = I_{in,OPAMP}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ideal OPAMP Rule #1** (no current into the OPAMP input):

$$
I_{in,OPAMP} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_{R_1} + I_{R_f} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Express the currents using Ohm's Law:**

$$
\frac{V_{in} - V_A}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_A}{R_f} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ideal OPAMP Rule #2** (virtual short: $V_A = V_+ = 0$ V):

$$
\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the gain:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Non-Inverting Amplifier

**Circuit:** Input $V_{in}$ applied directly to the non-inverting (+) terminal. $R_1$ from inverting terminal to ground; $R_f$ from output to inverting terminal (feedback).

**Step 1 — Apply Ideal OPAMP Rule #2** (virtual short):

$$
V_- = V_+ = V_{in}
$$

**Step 2 — Observe that $R_1$ and $R_f$ form a voltage divider** from $V_{out}$ to ground. The voltage at the inverting terminal is:

$$
V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $V_-$:**

$$
V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}/V_{in}$:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = V_{in} \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Voltage Follower (Unity-Gain Buffer)

**Circuit:** Direct connection of output to the inverting terminal. $V_{in}$ goes to the non-inverting terminal. This is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier where $R_f = 0 \, \Omega$ (short) and $R_1 = \infty \, \Omega$ (open).

**Step 1 — Apply the non-inverting gain formula** with $R_f = 0$:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1 + 0 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Write the output equation:**

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = V_{in}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although the gain is 1, the buffer provides **infinite input impedance** and **near-zero output impedance**, making it ideal for impedance matching between stages.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Summing Amplifier (3-Input Inverting Summer)

**Circuit:** Three input voltages $V_1, V_2, V_3$ connected through resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ to the inverting node. Common feedback resistor $R_f$. Non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual ground, $V_A = 0$):**

$$
\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$
V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize for $n$ inputs:**

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{V_i}{R_i}}
$$

**Special Case — Equal Resistors ($R_1 = R_2 = \dots = R_n = R$):**

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R}(V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n)
$$

---

### 3.5 Numerical Design Example (Typical KTU Lab Problem)

**Design an inverting amplifier with a gain of −10. The input source can deliver at most 1 V peak. The OPAMP is powered by ±15 V. Choose standard E12 resistor values.**

**Step 1 — Pick a value for $R_1$:** $R_1 = 10 \, k\Omega$ (standard E12 value)

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$:** $R_f = 10 \times R_1 = 100 \, k\Omega$ (standard E12 value)

**Step 3 — Verify output swing:** $V_{out,\text{peak}} = 10 \times 1 \, V = 10 \, V_{peak}$, which is well within the ±13 V saturation limit of the 741.

**Step 4 — Compute bias compensation resistor:** $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_f = \dfrac{10 \, k\Omega \times 100 \, k\Omega}{110 \, k\Omega} \approx 9.09 \, k\Omega$ (use 9.1 kΩ standard).

---

### 3.6 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Parameter | Inverting | Non-Inverting | Voltage Follower | Summing Amplifier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Inversion | Yes (180°) | No (0°) | No (0°) | Yes (180°) |
| Input Impedance | Low ($R_1$) | Very High | Very High | Low (input resistor) |
| Minimum Gain | $\vert A_v \vert \geq 0$ (any) | $A_v \geq 1$ | $A_v = 1$ (fixed) | Depends on resistor ratios |
| Use as Adder? | Yes (inverting) | No (summing requires inverting topology) | No | Yes (primary purpose) |
| Common-Mode Noise | Rejects (virtual ground) | Rejects | Rejects | Rejects |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*In the inverting amplifier using an ideal OPAMP, derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain. Why is the inverting input terminal called a "virtual ground"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For an inverting amplifier, the non-inverting terminal is grounded ($V_+ = 0$). By the virtual short concept of an ideal OPAMP, $V_- = V_+ = 0$, even though no actual current flows into the OPAMP and the terminal is not physically connected to ground.

Applying KCL at the inverting node:

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0 \implies \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output. Hence, the inverting node sits at 0 V (ground potential) without being physically grounded — it is a **virtual ground**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*List any three ideal characteristics of an OPAMP. State the purpose of the bias compensation resistor $R_2$ in an inverting amplifier circuit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three ideal characteristics:**
1. Infinite open-loop voltage gain ($A_{OL} = \infty$)
2. Infinite input impedance ($Z_{in} = \infty$)
3. Zero output impedance ($Z_{out} = 0$)

**Purpose of $R_2$:** The bias compensation resistor $R_2$ is connected between the non-inverting terminal and ground. Its value is chosen as $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_f$ to minimize the effect of input bias currents flowing through the external resistors, thereby **reducing the DC output offset voltage**. (1 mark for bias current concept, 1 mark for $R_2$ formula, 1 mark for ideal characteristics list)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Design an **inverting amplifier** with a voltage gain of −20 and an input impedance of at least **15 kΩ**. Use a µA741 OPAMP powered by ±15 V. Determine suitable standard resistor values, the expected output voltage for a 0.2 V peak sine input, and explain how you would verify the gain experimentally using a CRO.

**(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Select $R_1$:** Input impedance of an inverting amplifier equals $R_1$. To meet $\geq 15 \, k\Omega$, choose the standard E12 value $R_1 = 15 \, k\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_f$:**

$$
R_f = \vert A_v \vert \times R_1 = 20 \times 15 \, k\Omega = 300 \, k\Omega
$$

Use the nearest E12 standard value: **$R_f = 330 \, k\Omega$**.

**Step 3 — Compute expected output:**

$$
V_{out,\text{peak}} = \vert A_v \vert \times V_{in,\text{peak}} = 20 \times 0.2 \, V = 4.0 \, V_{peak}
$$

This is well within the ±13 V saturation limit of the 741.

**Step 4 — Bias compensation resistor:**

$$
R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_f = \frac{15 \times 330}{15 + 330} = \frac{4950}{345} \approx 14.35 \, k\Omega
$$

Use **$R_2 = 15 \, k\Omega$** (closest E12).

**Step 5 — Experimental verification using CRO:** Apply a 1 kHz sine wave of 0.2 V peak from the function generator. Observe the output on the CRO. Measure the peak-to-peak voltage of input and output on the screen. The ratio $V_{o,pp}/V_{i,pp}$ should give approximately 20, and the output waveform must be **180° out of phase** with the input.

---

**(b)** With reference to the same OPAMP, derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a **non-inverting amplifier**. Compare its input impedance with that of the inverting amplifier and state one practical application where the non-inverting configuration is preferred.

**(7 marks)**

**Derivation:** For a non-inverting amplifier, $V_{in}$ is applied to the (+) terminal and feedback is taken from the output via $R_f$ and $R_1$ to the (−) terminal. By the virtual short:

$$
V_- = V_+ = V_{in}
$$

The voltage divider gives:

$$
V_- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}
$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$
A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

**Input Impedance Comparison:**

| Parameter | Inverting | Non-Inverting |
|---|---|---|
| $Z_{in}$ | Low (= $R_1$) | Very High (≈ OPAMP input impedance, several MΩ) |

**Application:** The non-inverting configuration is preferred in **sensor signal conditioning** (e.g., amplifying the output of a thermocouple or piezoelectric sensor) where the source impedance is high and any loading by the amplifier would distort the measurement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Design a **three-input inverting summing amplifier** that produces the output $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 4V_2 + V_3)$. Assume $R_f = 100 \, k\Omega$. Calculate the three input resistor values and verify the output when $V_1 = 1 \, V$, $V_2 = 0.5 \, V$, $V_3 = 2 \, V$.

**(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Write the general summer equation:**

$$
V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compare coefficients with desired $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 4V_2 + V_3)$:**

The gain for each input must equal $R_f / R_i$, so:

- $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \implies R_1 = \dfrac{100 \, k\Omega}{2} = 50 \, k\Omega$
- $\dfrac{R_f}{R_2} = 4 \implies R_2 = \dfrac{100 \, k\Omega}{4} = 25 \, k\Omega$
- $\dfrac{R_f}{R_3} = 1 \implies R_3 = \dfrac{100 \, k\Omega}{1} = 100 \, k\Omega$

**Step 3 — Verify the output:**

$$
V_{out} = -\left( 2(1) + 4(0.5) + 1(2) \right) = -(2 + 2 + 2) = -6 \, V
$$

This is within the ±13 V supply rails, so the OPAMP will not saturate.

---

**(b)** Explain the working of a **voltage follower** circuit. Why is it called a buffer? Mention two practical applications and explain its effect on the input and output impedance.

**(7 marks)**

**Working:** A voltage follower is a non-inverting amplifier with $R_f = 0$ (output shorted to inverting input) and $R_1 = \infty$ (no resistor to ground). Applying the non-inverting gain formula:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1 \implies V_{out} = V_{in}
$$

The output **exactly follows** the input — hence the name **voltage follower**. Because $V_- = V_{out}$ and $V_+ = V_{in}$, the virtual short forces $V_{out} = V_{in}$ at all times.

**Why is it called a buffer?** It **isolates** a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load without altering the signal level. The high input impedance (≈ ∞) prevents loading of the source, while the low output impedance (≈ 0) can drive heavy loads.

**Two Practical Applications:**
1. **Impedance matching between a sensor and an ADC** — a piezoelectric sensor has high output impedance; connecting it directly to an ADC would distort the signal. A buffer isolates them.
2. **Driving low-impedance headphones from a high-impedance audio source** — the buffer provides the necessary current drive without loading the source.

**Impedance Effect:**
- Input Impedance: $\approx \infty$ (does not load the source)
- Output Impedance: $\approx 0$ (can drive any load without voltage drop)

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in inverting amplifier / summer output. Even if the numerical value is correct, missing the inversion loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using wrong units** — always write kΩ, µF explicitly; sloppy unit notation is penalized.
> 3. **Not mentioning virtual ground / virtual short** — these terms are mandatory in KTU valuation keys for any OPAMP derivation.
> 4. **Ignoring saturation** — when designing, always verify that $V_{out,\text{peak}} < V_{cc} - 2 \, V$ (typically $\pm 13 \, V$ for a 741 on ±15 V rails). Designs that exceed saturation are considered practically infeasible.
> 5. **Not drawing the CRO connection** in the circuit diagram during the experiment write-up — this is a recurring 2-mark deduction in lab records.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Inverting Amplifier:** $A_v = -R_f / R_1$; input impedance is $R_1$; inverting input is at **virtual ground**.
- ⚡ **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_1$; input impedance is very high; **no phase inversion**.
- ⚡ **Voltage Follower:** $A_v = 1$; $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$; acts as a **unity-gain buffer** with infinite $Z_{in}$ and zero $Z_{out}$.
- ⚡ **Summing Amplifier:** $V_{out} = -R_f \sum (V_i / R_i)$; weighted sum with each input scaled by $R_f / R_i$.
- ⚡ **Bias Compensation:** $R_2 = R_1 \parallel R_f$ at the non-inverting input to cancel input bias current effects.
- ⚡ **Virtual Short:** $V_+ = V_-$ when negative feedback is present — the cornerstone of every OPAMP analysis.
- ⚡ **Virtual Ground:** The inverting node is at 0 V in inverting configurations, even though it is not physically grounded.
- ⚡ **Practical 741 Limits:** $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$, Slew Rate = 0.5 V/µs, Output Swing $\approx \pm 13 \, V$, Input Offset $\approx 2 \, mV$.
- ⚡ **Lab Safety:** Always power the IC (±15 V) **before** connecting input signals; reverse polarity destroys the chip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Comparator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage Comparator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Comparator:** An electronic circuit (typically built using an operational amplifier in open-loop configuration) that compares two analog input voltages and produces a **two-state binary output** (HIGH or LOW) depending on which input is larger. The transition between states is essentially instantaneous, making the comparator an **analog-to-digital decision element**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCBML307 — Analog Electronics Lab), the voltage comparator experiment is implemented using a **general-purpose op-amp (μA741 / LM741)** powered from symmetric dual supplies **±VCC = ±12 V**, producing saturated outputs of approximately **+Vsat ≈ +VCC − 2 V ≈ +10 V** and **−Vsat ≈ −VCC + 2 V ≈ −10 V**.

### Structural Classification of Voltage Comparators

| Type | Configuration | Reference Placement | Output Polarity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Inverting Comparator** | Vin → Non-Inverting (+) terminal | Vref at Inverting (−) terminal | In-phase with input crossing |
| **Inverting Comparator** | Vin → Inverting (−) terminal | Vref at Non-Inverting (+) terminal | Inverted relative to input |
| **Zero-Crossing Detector** | Vin → Inverting or Non-Inverting | Vref = 0 V (ground) | Detects polarity reversal of Vin |
| **Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative)** | Positive feedback from output → Non-Inverting | Fixed Vref at Inverting | Hysteresis — noise immunity |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Weighing Scale Analogy**
> Imagine a perfectly balanced two-pan weighing scale. You place voltage **Va** on the **left pan** and voltage **Vb** on the **right pan**. The scale has no middle state — the pointer is hard-pinned either to the **LEFT (HIGH)** or to the **RIGHT (LOW)**. If Va > Vb, the pointer slams to the LEFT. If Va < Vb, it slams to the RIGHT. The pointer *never* stays in the middle.
>
> - The **op-amp's open-loop gain (AOL ≈ 2 × 10⁵ for μA741)** acts as the *mechanical leverage* of the scale.
> - The **output saturation voltage (±Vsat)** acts as the *hard end-stop* of the pointer.
> - The **reference voltage Vref** is the *known weight* placed on one pan.
> - The **input voltage Vin** is the *unknown weight* placed on the other pan.

In a **Zero-Crossing Detector**, you remove the known weight (Vref = 0), and the comparator simply tells you whether your input signal is **positive or negative at any instant** — extremely useful in sinusoidal-to-square-wave conversion and phase-locked loops.

In a **Schmitt Trigger**, you add a *memory effect* (hysteresis) by feeding the output back to the input — analogous to the scale pointer nudging the pans slightly to prevent *vibration-induced false tipping* (noise).

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Operating Principle

A comparator operates in the **open-loop mode** of the op-amp (no negative feedback). The differential input voltage

$$V_d = V_+ - V_-$$

is multiplied by the enormous open-loop gain AOL. The output is forced into saturation:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_d$$

Since AOL is so large, even a few millivolts of differential input is enough to drive the output to the rail. The boundary condition (threshold) is precisely when **Vd = 0**, i.e., **V+ = V−**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Lab:** The op-amp used here is *not* operating in its linear region. The **virtual short and virtual open concepts do not apply** to a comparator in open loop. This is the single most common conceptual mistake students make in KTU viva voce.

---

## 1.4 Standard Lab Components & Operating Conditions

| Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Op-amp IC | **μA741 or LM741** | 8-pin DIP package |
| Supply Voltage | **±12 V (dual)** | Symmetric dual supply |
| +Vsat | **≈ +10 V** | Slightly less than +VCC |
| −Vsat | **≈ −10 V** | Slightly greater than −VCC |
| AOL (Open-loop gain) | **2 × 10⁵** | Typical for μA741 |
| Input Impedance | **≈ 2 MΩ** | High — negligible loading |
| Output Impedance (saturated) | **≈ 75 Ω** | Low — can drive LED/relay |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### Case A: Non-Inverting Comparator

**Configuration:** Input Vin applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**; reference Vref applied to the **inverting (−) terminal**.

**Step 1 — The decision rule:** The op-amp output saturates positive if the differential input is positive.
> If **Vin > Vref** ⟹ **Vout = +Vsat (HIGH)**

**Step 2 — The decision rule for the opposite case:** If the differential input is negative.
> If **Vin < Vref** ⟹ **Vout = −Vsat (LOW)**

**Step 3 — Threshold point:** At the boundary, Vin = Vref, the output is theoretically undefined and switches abruptly.

### Case B: Inverting Comparator

**Configuration:** Input Vin applied to the **inverting (−) terminal**; reference Vref applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.

> If **Vin < Vref** ⟹ **Vout = +Vsat (HIGH)**
>
> If **Vin > Vref** ⟹ **Vout = −Vsat (LOW)**

The output logic is *inverted* relative to the input, hence the name.

### Case C: Zero-Crossing Detector (Vref = 0)

This is a special case where the reference is grounded. The comparator detects the sign of Vin.

> If **Vin > 0** (in non-inverting mode) ⟹ **Vout = +Vsat**
>
> If **Vin < 0** ⟹ **Vout = −Vsat**

A sinusoidal input of amplitude, say, 5 V peak produces a square wave of amplitude ±Vsat switching at every zero crossing.

### Case D: Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with Hysteresis)

**Configuration:** A feedback resistor R₂ connects the output to the non-inverting (+) input, and resistor R₁ connects the inverting (−) input to ground (or to a fixed Vref). This forms a **positive feedback network** that creates two distinct threshold levels.

The output no longer switches at a single threshold; instead, it has an **upper threshold (VTH+)** and a **lower threshold (VTH−)**, with the switching depending on the *current state* of the output.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the *complete* formula inventory for any numerical question on Voltage Comparator that may appear in KTU University Examinations. **Memorize the threshold formulas for the Schmitt trigger** — they account for ~70% of 14-mark questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential input | $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ | Governing decision variable |
| 2 | Output (saturated) | $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot V_d$ | Open-loop op-amp relation |
| 3 | Saturation (typical) | $+V_{sat} \approx +V_{CC} - 2\,\text{V}$ | For ±12 V supply → +10 V |
| 4 | Saturation (typical) | $-V_{sat} \approx -V_{CC} + 2\,\text{V}$ | For ±12 V supply → −10 V |
| 5 | Threshold (basic comparator) | $V_{TH} = V_{ref}$ | Where V+ = V− |
| 6 | Schmitt Upper Threshold | $V_{TH+} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{ref} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,(+V_{sat})$ | Switches from LOW to HIGH |
| 7 | Schmitt Lower Threshold | $V_{TH-} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{ref} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,(-V_{sat})$ | Switches from HIGH to LOW |
| 8 | Hysteresis Width | $V_H = V_{TH+} - V_{TH-} = \dfrac{2R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | Noise-rejection band |
| 9 | Hysteresis Width (Vref = 0) | $V_H = \pm\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | Symmetric about zero |
| 10 | Switching Time | $t_{sw} \approx \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{S_R}$ | Slew-rate limited |
| 11 | Slew Rate (μA741) | $S_R = 0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$ | Output rate limit |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Voltage comparators are the **fundamental decision-making primitive** in nearly every mixed-signal electronic system:

- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Flash ADCs use $2^N - 1$ comparators in parallel to digitize analog signals in a single clock cycle.
- **Oscilloscope Triggering Circuits:** Comparators detect when a waveform crosses a user-defined threshold, allowing stable display.
- **Power Supply Protection (Over-Voltage / Under-Voltage Cut-off):** Comparators monitor the supply rail and disconnect the load on fault.
- **Motor Control:** Zero-crossing detectors interface microcontroller digital I/O with AC mains.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Converting noisy analog sensor outputs into clean digital edges for microcontroller input pins.
- **Schmitt Trigger Inverters (74HC14, CD40106):** The most ubiquitous digital IC family — every logic-gate input is a Schmitt-triggered comparator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Schmitt Trigger Matters in Lab:** In a plain comparator, a noisy input near the threshold causes the output to *chatter* (rapid unwanted switching) — a phenomenon called **"comparator oscillation"** or **"bouncing."** The Schmitt trigger's hysteresis band absorbs this noise. The KTU 2024 examiner *always* tests whether you can both design a comparator *and* explain why hysteresis is added.

---

## 2.4 Transfer Characteristics — Logic Summary

For a non-inverting comparator with Vref = 2 V and ±Vsat = ±10 V:

| Condition | Output Voltage | Logic State |
|---|---|---|
| Vin < 2 V | Vout = −10 V | LOW (Logic 0) |
| Vin = 2 V (exact) | Switching (unstable) | Transition |
| Vin > 2 V | Vout = +10 V | HIGH (Logic 1) |

The transfer characteristic is a **vertical step** at Vin = Vref, dropping from +Vsat to −Vsat (for non-inverting) or from −Vsat to +Vsat (for inverting) as Vin increases.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Threshold Voltage of a Basic Op-Amp Comparator

### Problem Statement
A non-inverting comparator is built with μA741, supply ±12 V. The reference Vref is obtained from a 10 kΩ–10 kΩ voltage divider across the +12 V supply. Derive the threshold voltage and the output voltage for Vin = +3 V.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the reference voltage from the voltage divider.**

$$V_{ref} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{CC}$$

Substituting R₁ = R₂ = 10 kΩ, VCC = 12 V:

$$V_{ref} = \frac{10\,\text{k}\Omega}{10\,\text{k}\Omega + 10\,\text{k}\Omega} \times 12\,\text{V} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12\,\text{V} = 6\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the comparator decision rule.**

For a non-inverting comparator, the comparison is between Vin (at +) and Vref (at −):

$$V_d = V_{in} - V_{ref} = 3\,\text{V} - 6\,\text{V} = -3\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the output state.**

Since $V_d < 0$ (i.e., Vin < Vref):

$$V_{out} = -V_{sat} \approx -V_{CC} + 2\,\text{V} = -12\,\text{V} + 2\,\text{V} = -10\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Express the threshold.**

The threshold is the point at which Vd = 0:

$$V_{TH} = V_{ref} = 6\,\text{V}$$

So for **Vin = +3 V**, the output is **−10 V (LOW)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Upper and Lower Thresholds of an Inverting Schmitt Trigger

### Problem Statement
Design an inverting Schmitt trigger using μA741 with R₁ = 10 kΩ (from inverting input to ground), R₂ = 100 kΩ (feedback from output to non-inverting input), and Vref = +2 V applied to the inverting input through R₁. Supply is ±12 V. Compute VTH+, VTH−, and the hysteresis width.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit topology.**

In this inverting Schmitt configuration:
- Vref = +2 V is applied to the **inverting (−) input** through R₁
- The **non-inverting (+) input** is at voltage V+, which is set by the superposition of Vref (via R₁) and Vout (via R₂)

**Step 2 — Apply superposition at the non-inverting (+) node.**

The voltage at V+ is:

$$V_+ = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the upper threshold VTH+ (output transitions from −Vsat to +Vsat).**

The switch occurs when V+ = V− = Vref, i.e.:

$$V_{ref} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Solving for Vout (which here equals VTH+ — the input voltage that triggers the switch):

$$V_{TH+} = V_{ref} + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_{ref} - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_{ref} + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_{out}$$

More cleanly, the standard textbook formula for the **upper threshold** (with the input on the inverting side and Vref = fixed bias):

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,(+V_{sat}) + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{ref}$$

Substituting values (R₁ = 10 kΩ, R₂ = 100 kΩ, +Vsat = 10 V, Vref = 2 V):

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{10}{10 + 100} \times 10 + \frac{100}{10 + 100} \times 2$$

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{10}{110} \times 10 + \frac{100}{110} \times 2$$

$$V_{TH+} = 0.909\,\text{V} + 1.818\,\text{V} = 2.727\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the lower threshold VTH− (output transitions from +Vsat to −Vsat).**

Replacing +Vsat with −Vsat:

$$V_{TH-} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,(-V_{sat}) + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{ref}$$

$$V_{TH-} = \frac{10}{110} \times (-10) + \frac{100}{110} \times 2$$

$$V_{TH-} = -0.909\,\text{V} + 1.818\,\text{V} = 0.909\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the hysteresis width.**

$$V_H = V_{TH+} - V_{TH-} = 2.727 - 0.909 = 1.818\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Verify using the closed-form shortcut.**

$$V_H = \frac{2R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} = \frac{2 \times 100}{110} \times 10 = 1.818\,\text{V} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Comparison

| Feature | Basic Comparator | Zero-Crossing Detector | Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback type | None (open loop) | None (open loop) | **Positive feedback** |
| Threshold levels | One (VTH = Vref) | One (VTH = 0) | **Two (VTH+, VTH−)** |
| Hysteresis | **0 V** | **0 V** | $> 0$ V |
| Noise immunity | **Poor** | **Poor** | **Excellent** |
| Output bouncing near threshold | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Transfer curve shape | Single vertical step | Single vertical step at 0 | Rectangular loop (hysteresis loop) |
| Lab time-trace | Square wave with ringing at edges | Square wave at every zero | Clean square wave, no ringing |
| VCC for μA741 | ±12 V | ±12 V | ±12 V |
| Output swing | ±10 V (saturated) | ±10 V (saturated) | ±10 V (saturated) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Shortcut:** When asked to draw the transfer characteristic of a Schmitt trigger on graph paper, always draw a **clockwise rectangular hysteresis loop**. The vertical jump up occurs at VTH+ (on the rising Vin axis) and the vertical jump down occurs at VTH− (on the falling Vin axis). KTU examiners award full marks only if the loop direction is correct.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Output Frequency of a Zero-Crossing Sine-to-Square Converter

### Problem Statement
A 1 kHz sine wave of amplitude 5 V peak is fed to a zero-crossing detector using μA741 with ±12 V supply. What is the frequency and amplitude of the output square wave?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the principle.**
The output switches every time the input crosses 0 V. For a sine wave of frequency f, there are **2 zero crossings per cycle** — but in the comparator, the output toggles at *each* crossing, producing an output frequency **equal to the input frequency** with a 50% duty cycle.

**Step 2 — Output frequency.**

$$f_{out} = f_{in} = 1\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Output amplitude.**

$$V_{out} = \pm V_{sat} = \pm 10\,\text{V (peak-to-peak: 20 V)}$$

**Step 4 — Slew-rate check (μA741: S_R = 0.5 V/μs).**

Total voltage swing = 2 × 10 = 20 V.

$$t_{rise} = \frac{\Delta V}{S_R} = \frac{20\,\text{V}}{0.5\,\text{V/}\mu\text{s}} = 40\,\mu\text{s}$$

Since the input period is 1 ms, the rise time (40 μs) is **4% of the period** — fast enough to approximate a clean square wave. For higher-frequency inputs (>25 kHz), the μA741's slew rate would *limit* the rise/fall times, and a faster comparator IC like **LM311** would be required.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a voltage comparator. Why is an op-amp used in open-loop configuration for comparator applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **voltage comparator** is an electronic circuit that compares two analog input voltages and produces a binary output indicating which one is larger. The output is forced to one of two saturation levels (+Vsat or −Vsat).

An op-amp is used in **open-loop configuration** (without negative feedback) for two reasons:

1. The op-amp's open-loop gain AOL is extremely high (≈ 2 × 10⁵ for μA741), so even a few millivolts of differential input is sufficient to drive the output into saturation, providing a sharp, well-defined switching action.
2. In closed loop, the op-amp operates in its linear region, producing an *amplified* version of the input rather than a binary decision. Open-loop operation makes the device behave as a *decision element* — exactly what a comparator requires.

> **(3 marks awarded for: definition 1M, reason 1 1M, reason 2 1M)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Schmitt trigger? How does it differ from a basic op-amp comparator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator circuit with **positive feedback** from the output to the non-inverting input, producing two distinct switching thresholds — an upper threshold VTH+ and a lower threshold VTH−.

**Differences from a basic comparator:**

| Feature | Basic Comparator | Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None (open loop) | Positive feedback |
| Number of thresholds | One (VTH = Vref) | Two (VTH+ and VTH−) |
| Hysteresis | 0 V | $> 0$ V |
| Noise immunity | Poor — output chatters near threshold | Excellent — noise within hysteresis band is rejected |

The **hysteresis width** is the *dead zone* within which input noise cannot cause false switching, making the Schmitt trigger the preferred choice in noisy industrial environments.

> **(3 marks: definition 1M, difference table 2M)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Design an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using μA741 op-amp with R₁ = 1 kΩ, R₂ = 10 kΩ, and Vref = 0 V. The supply voltages are ±15 V. Compute the upper and lower threshold voltages, the hysteresis width, and draw the transfer characteristic. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram how the same μA741 can be configured as a **zero-crossing detector**. What is the output waveform when a 5 V peak, 1 kHz sine wave is applied? Mention one practical application. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify saturation voltages.**

For ±15 V supply, the typical saturation is ±13 V (≈ VCC − 2 V). Let us take **+Vsat = +13 V, −Vsat = −13 V**.

**Step 2 — Apply the upper-threshold formula.**

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,(+V_{sat}) + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{ref}$$

With R₁ = 1 kΩ, R₂ = 10 kΩ, Vref = 0 V:

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{1}{11} \times 13 + \frac{10}{11} \times 0 = 1.182\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the lower-threshold formula.**

$$V_{TH-} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,(-V_{sat}) + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{ref}$$

$$V_{TH-} = \frac{1}{11} \times (-13) + 0 = -1.182\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis width.**

$$V_H = V_{TH+} - V_{TH-} = 1.182 - (-1.182) = 2.364\,\text{V}$$

Alternatively, using the shortcut:

$$V_H = \frac{2R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} = \frac{2 \times 10}{11} \times 13 = 2.364\,\text{V}\;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Transfer characteristic.**

The transfer curve is a **clockwise rectangular hysteresis loop** centered on Vref = 0:
- For Vin rising from below VTH−: Vout stays at +13 V until Vin reaches +1.182 V
- For Vin > 1.182 V: Vout snaps to −13 V
- For Vin falling back: Vout stays at −13 V until Vin reaches −1.182 V
- For Vin < −1.182 V: Vout returns to +13 V

> **Mark distribution (a): Threshold formula application 3M, VTH+ value 1M, VTH− value 1M, hysteresis 1M, transfer curve 1M = 7M**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Circuit diagram (described in text for compactness):**

- μA741 op-amp powered from ±15 V
- Pin 3 (non-inverting +): connected to **ground** (Vref = 0)
- Pin 2 (inverting −): connected to the input sine wave through a 1 kΩ series resistor
- Pin 6 (output): connected to CRO Channel 2 and a 1 kΩ + LED indicator
- Pins 1, 5, 8: left unconnected (or offset-null potentiometer for fine adjustment)

**Step 2 — Working principle.**

With Vref = 0, the comparator output is:
- **+Vsat = +13 V** when **Vin > 0 V**
- **−Vsat = −13 V** when **Vin < 0 V**

**Step 3 — Output waveform for Vin = 5 sin(2π × 1000 × t):**

| Condition | Output |
|---|---|
| Positive half-cycle (Vin > 0) | +13 V (HIGH) |
| Negative half-cycle (Vin < 0) | −13 V (LOW) |

The output is a **square wave of frequency 1 kHz** (same as input), with amplitude ±13 V and approximately 50% duty cycle.

**Step 4 — Practical application:**

**Sine-to-square wave converter** for use as a clock generator in digital systems, or as a **phase detector input** in phase-locked loops (PLLs).

> **Mark distribution (b): Circuit description 2M, working principle 2M, output waveform derivation 2M, application 1M = 7M**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **non-inverting comparator** using μA741. The reference voltage is 3 V and Vin is a triangular wave of amplitude 5 V. Sketch the output waveform and determine the duty cycle. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Schmitt trigger is to be designed with VTH+ = +4 V and VTH− = +1 V using ±12 V supply. The reference is Vref = 0 V. Determine the values of resistors R₁ and R₂ (assume R₂ = 10 R₁). Verify the hysteresis width. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Circuit diagram (text description).**

- μA741 op-amp, supply ±12 V
- Pin 3 (+): connected to triangular input Vin (5 V peak, DC offset 0)
- Pin 2 (−): connected to a fixed +3 V reference obtained from a 10 kΩ–10 kΩ divider across +12 V, or from a DC source
- Pin 6: connected to CRO

**Step 2 — Decision rule.**

$$V_{out} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} = +10\,\text{V}, & \text{if } V_{in} > V_{ref} = 3\,\text{V} \\ -V_{sat} = -10\,\text{V}, & \text{if } V_{in} < V_{ref} = 3\,\text{V} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Output waveform sketch.**

A symmetric triangular wave of amplitude 5 V crosses the +3 V threshold once per cycle (on the rising slope, going up) and crosses it again on the falling slope (going down). However, since the threshold is asymmetric with respect to the triangle's mean, the duty cycle is *not* 50%.

**Step 4 — Duty cycle calculation.**

Let the triangular wave be: $V_{in}(t) = 5 \cdot \text{tri}(t)$, with peak +5 V and trough −5 V, and period T.

The time at which Vin crosses +3 V on the rising slope:

$$t_1 = \frac{T}{4} \cdot \frac{3 - (-5)}{5 - (-5)} = \frac{T}{4} \cdot \frac{8}{10} = 0.2\,T$$

(measured from the start of the rising slope at Vin = −5 V)

The time at which Vin crosses +3 V on the falling slope:

$$t_2 = t_1 + \frac{T}{2} = 0.2\,T + 0.5\,T = 0.7\,T$$

Time duration during which Vin > 3 V (output = +10 V) within one period:

$$T_{HIGH} = T - 0.2\,T + 0.7\,T - T = 0.5\,T$$

Wait — re-deriving carefully: from t = 0.2 T to t = 0.7 T, the input is above +3 V.

$$T_{HIGH} = 0.7\,T - 0.2\,T = 0.5\,T$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

> **Note:** The duty cycle is exactly **50%** *only* when Vref is the DC mean of the symmetric triangular wave. For Vref = 0 (the symmetric center) with ±5 V triangular input, duty cycle = 50%. Since Vref = 3 V is not centered, we recompute properly:

Correcting the rising-slope crossing (Vin goes from −5 to +5 in T/2):

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{10}{T/2} = \frac{20}{T} \;\text{V/s}$$

Time from Vin = −5 to Vin = +3 on rising slope:

$$\Delta t = \frac{3 - (-5)}{20/T} = \frac{8\,T}{20} = 0.4\,T$$

This is *after* the midpoint (T/4 = 0.25 T) by 0.15 T.

Time from Vin = +5 to Vin = +3 on falling slope:

$$\Delta t = \frac{5 - 3}{20/T} = \frac{2\,T}{20} = 0.1\,T$$

So Vin > 3 V for the interval from (0.25 + 0.15) T = 0.40 T to (0.75 + 0.10) T = 0.85 T.

$$T_{HIGH} = 0.85\,T - 0.40\,T = 0.45\,T$$

$$\boxed{\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{0.45\,T}{T} \times 100\% = 45\%}$$

> **Mark distribution (a): Circuit 2M, decision rule 2M, waveform sketch 1M, duty cycle derivation 2M = 7M**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall the threshold formulas (Vref = 0).**

$$V_{TH+} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$$

$$V_{TH-} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Use the given condition R₂ = 10 R₁.**

Substitute R₂ = 10 R₁ into the upper-threshold formula:

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{10\,R_1}{R_1 + 10\,R_1} \times 10 = \frac{10}{11} \times 10 = \frac{100}{11} = 9.09\,\text{V}$$

This is *not* +4 V — therefore, the assumption R₂ = 10 R₁ is inconsistent with VTH+ = 4 V for ±12 V supply.

**Re-solving without the R₂ = 10 R₁ constraint, using both threshold equations:**

From VTH+ = +4 V:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \times 10 = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_2 = 0.4\,(R_1 + R_2)$$

$$R_2 = 0.4\,R_1 + 0.4\,R_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.6\,R_2 = 0.4\,R_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_2 = \frac{2}{3}\,R_1$$

**Step 3 — Choose R₁ and compute R₂.**

Select **R₁ = 30 kΩ** (standard value). Then:

$$R_2 = \frac{2}{3} \times 30\,\text{k}\Omega = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Verify VTH+ and VTH−.**

$$V_{TH+} = \frac{20}{30 + 20} \times 10 = \frac{20}{50} \times 10 = 4\,\text{V} \;\;\checkmark$$

$$V_{TH-} = \frac{20}{50} \times (-10) = -4\,\text{V}$$

**Note:** VTH− comes out to −4 V, not +1 V as specified. This indicates the original design specifications (VTH+ = +4 V, VTH− = +1 V, Vref = 0) are *not simultaneously achievable* with a symmetric ±12 V supply and a *single* resistor pair. The student should explicitly state this constraint and propose:

- **Option A:** Use a non-zero Vref to shift the hysteresis band to the desired location.
- **Option B:** Reduce the supply voltage to achieve the required VTH+ = +4 V and VTH− = +1 V band.

**Step 5 — Verify the hysteresis width.**

$$V_H = V_{TH+} - V_{TH-} = 4 - (-4) = 8\,\text{V}$$

Or using the shortcut:

$$V_H = \frac{2R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,V_{sat} = \frac{2 \times 20}{50} \times 10 = 8\,\text{V}\;\;\checkmark$$

> **Mark distribution (b): Threshold formula 2M, algebraic setup 2M, R₁ and R₂ values 1M, verification 1M, design-constraint note 1M = 7M**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong formula direction.** Students frequently interchange R₁ and R₂ in the Schmitt trigger threshold formulas. *Memorize:* R₁ is the resistor on the **inverting side** (to Vref/ground), and R₂ is the **feedback resistor** from output to the non-inverting input.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of Vsat.** The lower threshold VTH− uses **−Vsat**, not +Vsat. Mixing up signs gives a hysteresis value of zero, which the examiner will mark as a fundamental conceptual error (2-mark penalty).
>
> 3. **Confusing inverting and non-inverting comparator logic.** In the **non-inverting** comparator, output goes HIGH when **Vin > Vref**. In the **inverting** comparator, output goes HIGH when **Vin < Vref**. State this clearly in your answer.
>
> 4. **Drawing the hysteresis loop in the wrong direction.** A counter-clockwise loop is incorrect; it should always be **clockwise** on the Vin–Vout plane.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning ±Vsat values explicitly.** Examiners expect to see the saturation voltage computed (e.g., "+Vsat = +10 V for ±12 V supply"), not just a vague "+VCC."
>
> 6. **Skipping the slew-rate limitation discussion.** A complete answer should note that the μA741's slew rate (0.5 V/μs) limits high-frequency operation and that faster comparators (LM311, LM339) are used above ~25 kHz.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the LED current-limiting resistor in the circuit diagram.** Always include a series resistor (typically 1 kΩ) with the indicator LED.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Voltage Comparator (PCBML307 Module Experiment 2)**

- [x] **Definition:** A circuit that compares two voltages and outputs a binary HIGH or LOW.
- [x] **Core element:** Op-amp in **open-loop** (no negative feedback) configuration.
- [x] **Standard IC:** μA741 with **±12 V** dual supply; ±Vsat ≈ **±10 V**.
- [x] **Decision rule:** Vout = +Vsat when V+ > V−; Vout = −Vsat when V+ < V−.
- [x] **Non-inverting comparator:** Vin at (+), Vref at (−). Output HIGH when Vin > Vref.
- [x] **Inverting comparator:** Vin at (−), Vref at (+). Output HIGH when Vin < Vref.
- [x] **Zero-crossing detector:** Special case with Vref = 0 V. Converts sine to square.
- [x] **Schmitt trigger:** Adds **positive feedback** to create two thresholds (VTH+, VTH−).
- [x] **Hysteresis width:** $V_H = \dfrac{2R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ — must be greater than peak input noise.
- [x] **Transfer curve of Schmitt trigger:** Clockwise rectangular loop.
- [x] **Slew rate of μA741:** 0.5 V/μs — limits use above ~25 kHz.
- [x] **Key applications:** ADC, sine-to-square conversion, over-voltage protection, Schmitt-triggered logic gates (74HC14).
- [x] **Lab precaution:** Always include a 1 kΩ current-limiting resistor with the indicator LED.
- [x] **Avoid:** Virtual short / virtual open concepts do *not* apply to an open-loop comparator.
- [x] **Pin map:** Pin 2 = inverting (−); Pin 3 = non-inverting (+); Pin 6 = output; Pin 4 = V− ; Pin 7 = V+.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmitt Trigger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Schmitt Trigger # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Schmitt Trigger** is a comparator circuit incorporating **positive feedback** (regenerative feedback) that converts a slowly varying or noisy analog input signal into a clean, sharply defined binary (square-wave) output. The defining feature of a Schmitt Trigger is its **hysteresis** — the switching threshold for a rising input is *different* from the switching threshold for a falling input. In the KTU 2024 Analog Electronics Lab syllabus, the Schmitt Trigger is implemented using an **operational amplifier (op-amp)** configured with the non-inverting input receiving a portion of the output through a feedback network, while the analog input is applied to the inverting input (inverting configuration) or the non-inverting input (non-inverting configuration). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > The Schmitt Trigger is the *second experiment* in the analog lab sequence. Students are expected to (i) design the circuit, (ii) wire it on a breadboard/trainer kit using an op-amp such as the **μA741**, (iii) apply a sinusoidal/slowly varying input from a function generator, and (iv) observe the square-wave output on a CRO/DSO, verifying the **Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP)** and **Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP)** experimentally. ## 1.2 Two Canonical Configurations | Configuration | Input Applied To | Feedback Applied To | Output Polarity | |---|---|---|---| | **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Inverting (−) terminal | Non-inverting (+) terminal | Inverted w.r.t. input | | **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | Non-inverting (+) terminal | Inverting (−) terminal | Same polarity as input | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Thermostat:** > Think of a household air-conditioner thermostat set to 24 °C, but with a *±1 °C dead band*. The compressor does **not** switch ON at exactly 24.0 °C while cooling and OFF at 24.0 °C while warming. Instead, it switches ON only when the room *rises above 25 °C* (UTP) and stays ON until the room *falls below 23 °C* (LTP). The 2 °C gap is the **hysteresis band**, and it prevents the compressor from rapidly chattering ON/OFF due to small temperature fluctuations. > > A Schmitt Trigger does **exactly the same thing for voltage signals** — it ignores small noise fluctuations around the switching point and produces a stable, noise-immune output. The two threshold levels (UTP and LTP) are set by the feedback resistor ratio. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard op-amp supply rails:** ±12 V or ±15 V (typical lab values). - **Saturation voltage of μA741:** $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\text{ V}$ for a ±15 V supply. - **Hysteresis Voltage** $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$ — measured in **Volts (V)**. - **Threshold Voltages** $V_{UTP}$ (Upper) and $V_{LTP}$ (Lower) — measured in **Volts (V)**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" of Hysteresis In a plain (open-loop) comparator, **any** input noise that crosses the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ causes a spurious output transition. A Schmitt Trigger solves this by using **positive feedback** to create **two** distinct switching points: 1. When the input is rising and crosses **$V_{UTP}$**, the output snaps to **$+V_{sat}$**. 2. Once the output is at $+V_{sat}$, the new switching point becomes **$V_{LTP}$** (a *lower* value). 3. The input must now fall *below* $V_{LTP}$ for the output to flip to **$-V_{sat}$**. 4. Once at $-V_{sat}$, the switching point jumps back up to $V_{UTP}$. This memory-like behavior (the threshold depends on the *current* output state) is the **hysteresis loop**. ## 2.2 Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Step-by-Step Logic Assume a non-inverting Schmitt Trigger with resistors $R_1$ (input) and $R_2$ (feedback to + input). The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is found by the **superposition principle** (since op-amp input current is ideally zero): $$V_+ = V_{in}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} + V_{out}\,\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$$ Switching occurs when $V_- = V_+$, and because $V_- = 0$ (grounded in inverting configuration), we have: $$V_{in} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{out}$$ - If $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$, switching happens at $V_{in} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat}$ → this is the **LTP**. - If $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$, switching happens at $V_{in} = +\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat}$ → this is the **UTP**. > [!NOTE] > **Naming Convention Check:** In the *inverting* Schmitt Trigger, $V_{UTP}$ is *positive* and $V_{LTP}$ is *negative* — opposite to intuition. This is because a *positive* input is required to force the output *down* to $-V_{sat}$. ## 2.3 Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Step-by-Step Logic The input is now applied to the non-inverting terminal; the inverting terminal samples a fraction of the output through $R_1$ and $R_2$. The voltage at the inverting terminal is: $$V_- = V_{out}\,\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$$ The op-amp switches when $V_{in} = V_-$: $$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}V_{sat}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}V_{sat}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **In a non-inverting Schmitt Trigger, $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ are *symmetric* about 0 V** and are *smaller* in magnitude than $V_{sat}$. This is a common board exam trap — students often mistakenly use the inverting-trigger formula here. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | Quantity | Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Upper Threshold Voltage** $V_{UTP}$ | $+\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | $+\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | **V (Volts)** | | **Lower Threshold Voltage** $V_{LTP}$ | $-\dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | $-\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | **V (Volts)** | | **Hysteresis Voltage** $V_H$ | $\dfrac{2R_1}{R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | $\dfrac{2R_1}{R_1+R_2}\,V_{sat}$ | **V (Volts)** | | **Threshold Width Ratio** | $V_{UTP}/\vert V_{sat}\vert = R_1/R_2$ | $V_{UTP}/\vert V_{sat}\vert = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ | dimensionless | | **Condition for Schmitt Action** | $R_1/R_2 < 1$ (inverting) or $R_1 < R_2$ | Always satisfies for any $R_1,R_2>0$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** The ratio $R_1/R_2$ (inverting) or $R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ (non-inverting) is the **loop gain** $\beta$ of the positive feedback network. Schmitt action requires that the **closed-loop gain** $1/\beta$ be *less* than the open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ — a condition virtually always satisfied by a real op-amp. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Waveform Shaping:** Converting a sine/triangle wave into a clean square wave. - **Noise Immunity in Sensor Interfaces:** Debouncing mechanical switches and Schmitt-triggering Hall-effect/optical sensor outputs. - **Oscillators & Relaxation Oscillators:** Combined with an RC timing network, a Schmitt Trigger forms the classic **astable multivibrator**. - **Line Receivers & Digital Logic Input Buffers:** The 74LS14 / 74HC14 are dedicated Schmitt-trigger hex inverters used in digital design. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Inverting Schmitt Trigger **Step 1 — Define the circuit variables.** Let $R_1$ be the input resistor (connected between the input source $V_{in}$ and the inverting node). Let $R_2$ be the feedback resistor (connected between the output and the inverting node). The non-inverting (+) input is grounded, so $V_+ = 0$. **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (virtual-open assumption of op-amp).** Since no current flows into the op-amp's inverting input, the sum of currents through $R_1$ and $R_2$ must equal zero: $$\frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V_-}{R_2} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $V_- = 0$ (virtual ground for the threshold condition).** $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_2} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_{in}$ at the switching instant.** $$V_{in} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}\,V_{out}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the two cases.** *Case A:* The output has been at $+V_{sat}$ and the input is rising. The output will flip to $-V_{sat}$ when: $$V_{in}\big\vert_{flip \downarrow} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}(+V_{sat}) = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat} = V_{LTP}$$ *Case B:* The output has been at $-V_{sat}$ and the input is rising. The output will flip to $+V_{sat}$ when: $$V_{in}\big\vert_{flip \uparrow} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}(-V_{sat}) = +\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat} = V_{UTP}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the hysteresis band.** $$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat} - \left(-\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat}\right) = \frac{2R_1}{R_2}\,V_{sat}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign convention locked:** For the *inverting* Schmitt Trigger, $V_{UTP} > 0$ and $V_{LTP} < 0$. This is non-negotiable and is the single most-asked sub-question in the KTU ESE paper. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger **Step 1 — Define the circuit variables.** Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal. The inverting (−) terminal is tied to a voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (from output) and $R_2$ (to ground). So $V_- = V_{out} \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$. **Step 2 — Switching condition $V_{in} = V_-$.** $$V_{in} = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\,V_{out}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_{in}$ at switching.** $$V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}(+V_{sat}), \quad V_{LTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}(-V_{sat})$$ **Step 4 — Hysteresis band.** $$V_H = \frac{2R_2}{R_1+R_2}\,V_{sat}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Designer Insight:** In a non-inverting Schmitt Trigger, the *hysteresis* is controlled by the *lower* resistor in the divider. Setting $R_1 = R_2$ gives $V_{UTP} = \pm 0.5\,V_{sat}$, the canonical symmetric case. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Board-Exam Style) **Problem:** A non-inverting Schmitt Trigger uses an op-amp powered by $\pm 15$ V. Take $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. The feedback resistors are $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (from output to inverting input) and $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (from inverting input to ground). A triangular wave of peak amplitude **8 V** is applied. Sketch the input and output waveforms and label the switching instants. **Solution:** $$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{10k}{10k+10k} = 0.5$$ $$V_{UTP} = +0.5 \times 13 = +6.5\text{ V}$$ $$V_{LTP} = -0.5 \times 13 = -6.5\text{ V}$$ $$V_H = 13\text{ V}$$ Switching behaviour: - As $V_{in}$ rises from $-8$ V, it crosses $+6.5$ V → output snaps from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$. - As $V_{in}$ falls, it crosses $-6.5$ V → output snaps from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$. - Since the input peak (8 V) > $V_{UTP}$ (6.5 V), the circuit *will* switch — a necessary condition. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** If the input peak is *smaller* than $V_{UTP}$, the Schmitt Trigger **never switches** and the output stays stuck at one saturation level. Always verify the input peak > $V_{UTP}$ *and* the input trough < $V_{LTP}$ before drawing the waveform. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Configuration | Criterion | Inverting | Non-Inverting | |---|---|---| | Output level when $V_{in} < V_{UTP}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | | Output level when $V_{in} > V_{UTP}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | | DC input resistance | $R_1$ (low) | Very high (≈ op-amp input impedance) | | Preferred for sensor interfaces | No (loading) | **Yes** | | Used in relaxation oscillator | Less common | **Yes (canonical)** | | Sign of $V_{UTP}$ | Positive | Positive | | Sign of $V_{LTP}$ | Negative | Negative | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Topology — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **signal flow** of the canonical non-inverting Schmitt Trigger used in the KTU lab experiment. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[("Vin
Function Generator
Sine / Triangle")] r1(["R1 = 10k"]) r2(["R2 = 10k"]) gnd(("GND 0V")) vplus{{"Op-Amp
Non-Inverting
Terminal V+"}} vminus{{"Op-Amp
Inverting
Terminal V-"}} opamp["OP-AMP 741
Open Loop Gain ~ 2e5"] vout[("Vout
Square Wave
+/- Vsat")] vccp[("+Vcc = +15V")] vccn[("-Vee = -15V")] src -- "Vin applied here" --> vplus vplus --> opamp vout -.->|"Positive Feedback Path"| r1 r1 --> vminus vminus --> opamp r2 --> gnd vminus --- r2 opamp --> vout vccp --- opamp vccn --- opamp classDef source fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef opamp fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#b5e48c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class src source class vout output class r1,r2 resistor class opamp opamp ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** > - The **input** $V_{in}$ goes straight to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal — this is the *non-inverting* configuration. > - The **feedback resistor $R_1$** carries the output voltage back to the **inverting (−)** terminal — this is the **positive feedback** that creates hysteresis. > - **$R_2$** ties the inverting input to ground, forming a voltage divider with $R_1$. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Hysteresis State Machine The Schmitt Trigger behaves as a **two-state memory element**. The Mermaid state diagram below captures its regenerative behaviour. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StateNeg StateNeg : OUTPUT AT MINUS Vsat
Threshold now equals VUTP StatePos : OUTPUT AT PLUS Vsat
Threshold now equals VLTP StateNeg --> StatePos : Vin rises past VUTP
Comparator flips high StatePos --> StateNeg : Vin falls past VLTP
Comparator flips low note right of StateNeg In this state, Vplus must exceed Vminus for switching. Vplus = Vin Vminus = Vsat x R2 over R1 plus R2 end note note right of StatePos In this state, Vminus is now negative, so Vin must drop below VLTP to switch back. end note ``` ## 4.3 Transfer Characteristic (Hysteresis Loop) The transfer curve is a **rectangular hysteresis loop** traversed in the *opposite* sense for the two configurations. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Loop["Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic - Non Inverting"] A1["Vout = +Vsat"] -->|"Vin rises past VUTP"| B1["Vout stays +Vsat"] B1 -->|"Vin falls past VLTP"| C1["Vout = -Vsat"] C1 -->|"Vin rises past VUTP"| A1 end subgraph Axes[""] axisX["Horizontal: Vin"] axisY["Vertical: Vout"] end classDef sat fill:#f1c0e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef thr fill:#caffbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A1,C1 sat class B1 thr ``` **Visual convention:** - **Top edge of the loop:** Output is locked at $+V_{sat}$ for the entire region $V_{LTP} < V_{in} < V_{UTP}$ — this *flat top* is the hallmark of hysteresis. - **Vertical sides of the loop:** The instantaneous jump at $V_{in} = V_{UTP}$ (upward) and $V_{in} = V_{LTP}$ (downward). - **Bottom edge of the loop:** Output is locked at $-V_{sat}$ for $V_{LTP} < V_{in} < V_{UTP}$. ## 4.4 Input–Output Waveform Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Timeline["Time Axis: t"] t1["t0"] --- t2["t1 - Vin crosses VUTP"] --- t3["t2 - Vin crosses VLTP"] --- t4["t3 - Vin crosses VUTP"] end subgraph VinWave["Vin - Sinusoidal Input"] vinA["0V"] --- vinB["+Vpeak above VUTP"] --- vinC["0V"] --- vinD["-Vpeak below VLTP"] end subgraph VoutWave["Vout - Regenerated Square Wave"] voutA["-Vsat"] --- voutB["+Vsat jumps up at VUTP"] --- voutC["+Vsat held during descent"] --- voutD["-Vsat jumps down at VLTP"] end t1 --- vinA t2 --- vinB t2 --- voutB t3 --- vinC t3 --- voutC t4 --- vinD t4 --- voutD ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** The output transitions occur at *different* input levels on the rising and falling edges — this is the *hysteresis* visibly demonstrated. Note the output remains at $+V_{sat}$ for a longer time than a simple zero-crossing comparator would produce. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a Schmitt Trigger. Why is positive feedback essential in its design?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Schmitt Trigger is a regenerative comparator circuit that uses **positive feedback** to convert a slowly varying input into a sharp square-wave output with **two distinct switching thresholds** ($V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$), creating a hysteresis band $V_H$. Positive feedback is essential because **(1 mark)** it forces the output to snap rapidly to $\pm V_{sat}$ (regenerative action eliminates the slow slew-rate limitation of a normal comparator), and **(1 mark)** it establishes the two threshold levels whose values depend on the *current* output state, thereby providing **noise immunity** within the hysteresis band. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between the inverting and non-inverting Schmitt Trigger configurations.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Inverting | Non-Inverting | |---|---|---| | Input applied to | Inverting (−) terminal | Non-inverting (+) terminal | | Feedback to | Non-inverting (+) terminal | Inverting (−) terminal | | $V_{UTP}, V_{LTP}$ formula | $\pm (R_1/R_2) V_{sat}$ | $\pm [R_1/(R_1+R_2)] V_{sat}$ | | Input impedance | $R_1$ (low) | Very high | **(1 mark per correct row, capped at 3 marks.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **inverting Schmitt Trigger** using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for $V_{UTP}$, $V_{LTP}$, and the hysteresis voltage $V_H$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the circuit in part (a), the op-amp saturates at $\pm 12$ V and $R_1 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$. A triangular input of peak **4 V** is applied. **(i)** Calculate $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$. **(ii)** Will the circuit switch? Justify. **(iii)** If $R_1$ is changed to $20\text{ k}\Omega$, what happens? **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** Refer to the schematic in Section 4 (the same op-amp topology with input applied to the inverting terminal and $R_1, R_2$ forming the divider to the non-inverting terminal). The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** above. Final results: $$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_1}{R_2}V_{sat}, \quad V_H = \frac{2R_1}{R_2}V_{sat}$$ **Mark split (7 marks):** Circuit diagram — **2 marks**, KCL/virtual-ground setup — **2 marks**, Final formulas — **2 marks**, Mention of hysteresis/noise immunity — **1 mark**. #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i)** $R_1/R_2 = 5/10 = 0.5$ $$V_{UTP} = 0.5 \times 12 = +6\text{ V}, \quad V_{LTP} = -0.5 \times 12 = -6\text{ V}$$ **(ii)** Input peak = $\pm 4$ V. Since $4\text{ V} < V_{UTP} = 6\text{ V}$, the input *never* crosses either threshold. **The circuit will NOT switch**; the output remains stuck at one of the saturation levels (specifically at $+V_{sat}$ if the input is below $V_{LTP}$, or at $-V_{sat}$ if above $V_{LTP}$ but below $V_{UTP}$ — depends on power-on history). **(2 marks)** **(iii)** New $R_1/R_2 = 20/10 = 2.0$ $$V_{UTP} = 2 \times 12 = +24\text{ V}, \quad V_{LTP} = -24\text{ V}$$ Since the op-amp saturates at $\pm 12$ V, the output can never reach $\pm 24$ V. The *effective* thresholds collapse to $\pm 12$ V (any input above $+12$ V drives the output to $-V_{sat}$ and vice-versa), but the **loop gain $\beta = R_1/R_2 = 2 > 1$** violates the Schmitt-action condition. The op-amp no longer behaves as a Schmitt Trigger — it degenerates into a marginally stable or latch-up state. **The design must ensure $R_1 < R_2$ for an inverting Schmitt Trigger.** **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **(a)** Design a **non-inverting Schmitt Trigger** to have $V_{UTP} = +4$ V, $V_{LTP} = -4$ V, given $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. Specify the resistor ratio and draw the circuit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Sketch the input-output waveforms when a sine wave of peak 6 V, 1 kHz is applied to the designed circuit. Mark all critical voltage and time points.
