Calculate the duty cycle of the output square wave. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** For a non-inverting Schmitt Trigger: $$V_{UTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}V_{sat} \implies 4 = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\times 13$$ $$\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{4}{13} \implies 13R_2 = 4R_1 + 4R_2 \implies 9R_2 = 4R_1$$ $$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25$$ **Practical choice:** $R_1 = 22\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (closest standard E12 values; check: $4/13 = 0.308 \approx 10/32 = 0.3125$ — within 2%). **(4 marks)** Circuit diagram: input to (+), $R_1$ from output to (−), $R_2$ from (−) to GND. **(2 marks)** Verification of $V_{LTP}$: $-13 \times 10/32 = -4.06$ V ≈ -4 V. **(1 mark)** #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Waveform construction:** The sine wave $V_{in}(t) = 6\sin(2\pi \times 1000\,t)$ crosses $+4$ V on its rising portion and $-4$ V on its falling portion. **Output is +13 V** between these two crossing instants, and **−13 V** otherwise. **Computing the crossing angles:** $\sin\theta = 4/6 = 0.6667 \implies \theta = 41.81°$ (or 0.7297 rad). - **Rising crossing** at $t_1 = \theta/(2\pi f) = 0.7297 / (2\pi \times 1000) = 116.1\ \mu s$. - **Falling crossing** at $t_2 = (T/2 - \theta)/(2\pi f) \Rightarrow$ time from $t_1$ to $t_2$ is $(\pi - 2\theta)/(2\pi f)$. **Time spent in $+V_{sat}$ (high state):** $$t_{HIGH} = \frac{\pi - 2\theta}{2\pi f} = \frac{\pi - 1.4594}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{1.6822}{6283.2} = 267.7\ \mu s$$ **Total period** $T = 1\text{ ms}$. $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{t_{HIGH}}{T} \times 100\% = 26.77\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > **(1)** Many students forget to **mark $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ explicitly** on the input waveform — the examiner will deduct 1 mark for missing axis labels. > **(2)** A very common error is computing the duty cycle as **50%** (assuming a symmetric square wave). The duty cycle is **NOT 50%** unless the input crosses both thresholds *symmetrically* about zero (which is rarely the case unless the input is a *centered* triangle wave). For a sine wave, the duty cycle deviates from 50% as the threshold ratio $V_{UTP}/V_{peak}$ deviates from 0.707. > **(3)** Failing to *justify* whether the input peak exceeds the threshold is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in design questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must-memorize):** - **Schmitt Trigger = Comparator + Positive Feedback.** Without positive feedback, there is no hysteresis. **(1-liner definition.)** - **Two configurations:** *Inverting* (input to −) and *Non-inverting* (input to +). **Do not interchange the formulas.** - **Inverting trigger:** $V_{UTP} = +(R_1/R_2)V_{sat}$, $V_{LTP} = -(R_1/R_2)V_{sat}$, $V_H = (2R_1/R_2)V_{sat}$. **Requires $R_1 < R_2$** for stability. - **Non-inverting trigger:** $V_{UTP} = +(R_2/(R_1+R_2))V_{sat}$, $V_{LTP} = -(R_2/(R_1+R_2))V_{sat}$, $V_H = (2R_2/(R_1+R_2))V_{sat}$. **No stability restriction.** - **$V_{sat}$** is the *measured* saturation voltage of the op-amp (typically 1–2 V below the supply rail). For μA741 with $\pm 15$ V supply, take $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V. - **Hysteresis width** $V_H$ **must be larger than the peak-peak noise** on the input signal — this is the **noise-immunity design rule**. - **Input peak must exceed $V_{UTP}$** (and input trough must fall below $V_{LTP}$) — otherwise the output never switches. - **The output is *always* at $\pm V_{sat}$** — never at any intermediate voltage. A Schmitt Trigger has *only two* output states. - **Practical IC counterparts:** 74LS14, 74HC14, 74LV14 (hex inverting Schmitt triggers); LM393 with external positive feedback. - **Most common lab formula asked:** $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$ (board exams test this 1-line relationship more than any other). - **Time-domain effect:** Output *rises and falls* are nearly vertical (limited by op-amp slew rate, typically 0.5 V/μs for μA741), unlike the slow transitions of a passive RC comparator. - **Applications to cite in viva:** (1) Square-wave generator, (2) Relaxation oscillator with RC network, (3) Switch debouncer, (4) Line receiver in noisy industrial environments, (5) Triangle-to-square converter. - **What to write when stuck:** *Always* start with the **virtual-short condition** $V_+ = V_-$, then KCL at the inverting node, then evaluate the two output states ($\pm V_{sat}$) separately. This 3-step recipe will solve *every* Schmitt-Trigger problem KTU can ask. ---

topics.title: Active Filters and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Active Filters and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Filter (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An *active filter* is a frequency-selective electronic network that employs an **active device** (typically an operational amplifier) in conjunction with **passive components** (resistors $R$ and capacitors $C$) to achieve a specified magnitude and phase response over a defined range of frequencies. Unlike passive filters, active filters provide **gain**, **buffering**, and eliminate the need for bulky inductors.

The **frequency response** of an active filter is the steady-state response of the filter (output magnitude and phase) as a function of the input signal frequency. It is mathematically described by the **transfer function** $H(j\omega)$, which is the ratio of the output phasor to the input phasor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cut-off Frequency ($f_c$ or $f_{-3dB}$):** The frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ ($\approx 0.707$) of its maximum (pass-band) value, corresponding to a power loss of **3 dB**. The corresponding angular frequency is $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club:** Imagine an op-amp-based filter as a nightclub bouncer (the op-amp) standing at a door with a checklist (the RC network).
> - A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is like a bouncer who only allows **slow, deep-voiced** people (low-frequency bass notes from a subwoofer) into the VIP lounge and rejects high-pitched chatter (treble).
> - A **High Pass Filter (HPF)** is the opposite — only **high-energy, squeaky** voices (high frequencies, like a whistle or cymbal) get in; the slow bass is turned away.
> - A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a bouncer who only allows people whose voices are in a specific **range** (e.g., only tenors), and
> - A **Band Stop Filter (BSF)** rejects only that specific range, letting everything else through.

The op-amp is the **active bouncer with authority** — it has the *power (gain)* to enforce these rules sharply, unlike a passive bouncer (passive RC) who can only let the signal decay weakly.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pass-band gain ($A_0$ or $A_{max}$):** Dimensionless (in V/V) or in **dB**: $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_0)$.
- **Cutoff frequency ($f_c$):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Roll-off rate:** Measured in **dB/decade** (factor of 10 in frequency) or **dB/octave** (factor of 2 in frequency). **1st order = 20 dB/decade; 2nd order = 40 dB/decade.**
- **Quality factor ($Q$):** Dimensionless — defines the *sharpness* of a second-order filter: $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$.
- **Stop-band attenuation:** Expressed in **dB** (e.g., $> 40$ dB).
- **Standard frequency decade for plotting:** $10^1, 10^2, 10^3, 10^4, 10^5$ Hz (semi-log plot).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of First-Order Active Filters

A **first-order active filter** is constructed using a single RC network feeding an op-amp configured either as a **voltage follower (buffer)** or a **non-inverting amplifier** with gain $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.

**Why use an op-amp?** Three reasons:
1. Provides **gain** that passive filters cannot deliver.
2. Offers **high input impedance** (so the filter does not load the source).
3. Provides **low output impedance** (so the load does not affect the filter response).

### 2.2 Logic Steps for Designing a 1st-Order Active LPF

- **Step 1:** Identify the desired **cutoff frequency** $f_c$ and the pass-band gain $A_0$.
- **Step 2:** Choose a convenient capacitor value $C$ (typically in nF to µF range).
- **Step 3:** Calculate the resistor: $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$.
- **Step 4:** For non-unity gain, choose $R_f$ and $R_1$ such that $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.
- **Step 5:** Verify the **transfer function** magnitude at $f = f_c$ equals $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 A_0$.

### 2.3 Logic Steps for 1st-Order Active HPF

- **Step 1:** Same as LPF — choose $f_c$ and $A_0$.
- **Step 2:** Choose $C$.
- **Step 3:** Compute $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C}$.
- **Step 4:** In HPF, the series capacitor blocks DC and low frequencies; at high frequencies $C$ acts as a short.
- **Step 5:** Choose $R_f, R_1$ to set the gain.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in every KTU lab viva and exam question.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Cutoff frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Angular cutoff frequency | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC} = 2\pi f_c$ | rad/s |
| 3 | LPF transfer function (1st order) | $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | HPF transfer function (1st order) | $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \, sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Pass-band gain (non-inverting) | $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | V/V |
| 6 | Pass-band gain (inverting) | $A_0 = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | V/V (180° phase inversion) |
| 7 | Voltage gain in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | dB |
| 8 | Gain at cutoff (dB) | $A_{dB}(f_c) = A_{0,dB} - 3 \text{ dB}$ | Always $-3$ dB below $A_0$ |
| 9 | Roll-off (1st order) | $-20$ dB/decade | $= -6$ dB/octave |
| 10 | Roll-off (2nd order) | $-40$ dB/decade | $= -12$ dB/octave |
| 11 | Quality factor (2nd order) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3 - A_0}$ (Sallen-Key) | Dimensionless; $A_0 < 3$ for stability |
| 12 | Phase of LPF | $\phi = -\arctan(\omega RC)$ | Degrees / radians |
| 13 | Phase of HPF | $\phi = 90^\circ - \arctan(\omega RC)$ | Degrees / radians |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table formatting tip:** Absolute value bars were written as `\vert` in the formulas above to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Active filters are ubiquitous in modern electronics:
- **Audio Systems:** Crossovers in loudspeakers (LPF for woofers, HPF for tweeters).
- **Communication:** Channel selection in radios, anti-aliasing before ADCs.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning (e.g., 0.5 Hz HPF to remove DC electrode offset).
- **Control Systems:** Low-pass filters to remove high-frequency noise from sensor feedback.
- **Instrumentation:** Notch filters (50/60 Hz) to eliminate power-line hum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Active Low Pass Filter Transfer Function

Consider the standard non-inverting active LPF: input is applied through a series resistor $R$ to a node $V_a$, and a capacitor $C$ connects $V_a$ to ground. The non-inverting input of the op-amp senses $V_a$, and the op-amp is configured as a non-inverting amplifier with gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.

**Step 1 — Voltage divider (input network, before op-amp):**
Since the op-amp's non-inverting input draws no current (ideal op-amp), the network $R$ and $C$ forms a simple voltage divider in the frequency domain.

$$
V_a(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the expression:**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$ to clear the fraction.

$$
V_a(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage of non-inverting amplifier:**
The op-amp multiplies the input $V_a$ by the noise gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$.

$$
V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot V_a(s) = A_0 \cdot V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the transfer function $H(s)$:**

$$
H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ for steady-state sinusoidal analysis:**

$$
H(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of the transfer function:**

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Find the cutoff frequency:**
By definition, at $\omega = \omega_c$, $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$. Equating:

$$
\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2
$$

$$
(\omega_c RC)^2 = 1
$$

$$
\omega_c RC = 1
$$

$$
\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC} \quad ; \quad f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the First-Order Active High Pass Filter Transfer Function

For an active HPF, the resistor and capacitor positions are swapped. The series capacitor blocks DC and low frequencies, while at high frequencies the capacitor acts as a short.

**Step 1 — Voltage divider (input network):**
The input is applied to a series capacitor $C$, and the resistor $R$ goes to ground. The voltage across $R$ is $V_a$.

$$
V_a(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{R}{R + 1/sC}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by multiplying by $sC$:**

$$
V_a(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply op-amp gain:**

$$
V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 4 — Transfer function:**

$$
H(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \, sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $s = j\omega$:**

$$
H(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_0 \, j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude:**

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Cutoff frequency derivation:**
At $\omega = \omega_c$, the gain is $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$. Setting $\omega_c RC = 1$ gives the **same** cutoff formula as the LPF:

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Problem)

> **Problem:** Design a first-order active LPF with $f_c = 1 \text{ kHz}$ and pass-band gain $A_0 = 2$. Given $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$, find $R$, $R_f$, and $R_1$. Also calculate the gain in dB at $f = 10 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1 — Find R:**

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.5 \, \Omega \approx 1.59 \, k\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Find $R_f$ and $R_1$ for $A_0 = 2$:**

$$
A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1
$$

Choose $R_1 = R_f = 10 \, k\Omega$ (standard value).

**Step 3 — Gain in dB at $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ (pass-band):**

$$
A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \, dB
$$

**Step 4 — Gain in dB at $f = 10 \text{ kHz}$ (one decade above $f_c$):**
Since the roll-off is $-20$ dB/decade, the gain drops by 20 dB per decade.

$$
A_{dB}(10 \text{ kHz}) = 6.02 - 20 = -13.98 \, dB
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verify using the transfer function:**
> $\omega RC = 2\pi \times 10000 \times 1591.5 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 10$
> $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{1 + 100}} = \dfrac{2}{10.05} = 0.199$
> $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.199) = -14.02 \text{ dB}$ ✓ (matches within rounding)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Filter Comparison

| Property | Low Pass Filter | High Pass Filter |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Transfer Function $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{A_0 \cdot \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| At $f \to 0$ (DC) | $\vert H \vert = A_0$ (passes) | $\vert H \vert = 0$ (blocks) |
| At $f \to \infty$ | $\vert H \vert = 0$ (blocks) | $\vert H \vert = A_0$ (passes) |
| At $f = f_c$ | $\vert H \vert = A_0/\sqrt{2}$ | $\vert H \vert = A_0/\sqrt{2}$ |
| Phase at $f_c$ | $-45^\circ$ | $+45^\circ$ |
| Phase at $f \to 0$ | $0^\circ$ | $+90^\circ$ |
| Phase at $f \to \infty$ | $-90^\circ$ | $0^\circ$ |
| Roll-off | $-20$ dB/decade | $-20$ dB/decade |
| Use case | Noise removal, anti-aliasing | DC blocking, AC coupling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 First-Order Active Low Pass Filter — Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[Vin AC Source] -->|Input Signal| Rnode[R Resistor]
    Rnode -->|Node Va| Cnode[C Capacitor to GND]
    Rnode -->|Node Va to Non Inverting Input| OpAmp[Op Amp 741]
    OpAmp -->|Output| Vout[Vout]
    Rfnode[Rf Feedback] --> OpAmp
    R1node[R1 to GND] --> OpAmp
    Cnode --- GND1[Ground]
    R1node --- GND2[Ground]

    classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class OpAmp active
    class Rnode,Cnode,Rfnode,R1node passive
    class Vin,Vout io
```

### 4.2 First-Order Active High Pass Filter — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin[Vin AC Source] -->|Input Signal| Cnode[C Series Capacitor]
    Cnode -->|Node Va| Rnode[R Resistor to GND]
    Cnode -->|Node Va to Non Inverting Input| OpAmp[Op Amp 741]
    OpAmp -->|Output| Vout[Vout]
    Rfnode[Rf Feedback] --> OpAmp
    R1node[R1 to GND] --> OpAmp
    Rnode --- GND1[Ground]
    R1node --- GND2[Ground]

    classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class OpAmp active
    class Rnode,Cnode,Rfnode,R1node passive
    class Vin,Vout io
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Flow (Bode Plot Interpretation Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Input Sinusoidal Signal] --> B{Compare f with f_c}
    B -->|f less than f_c| C[Pass Band: Gain equals A_0]
    B -->|f equals f_c| D[Cutoff Point: Gain is A_0 minus 3 dB]
    B -->|f greater than f_c| E[Stop Band: Gain rolls off]
    C --> F[Flat Region on Bode Plot]
    D --> G[Corner Frequency on Bode Plot]
    E --> H[Slope minus 20 dB per decade for 1st order]
    F --> I[Output: Frequency Response Curve]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Plot Gain dB vs log f on semi log graph]

    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,J start
    class B decision
    class I output
```

### 4.4 Expected Frequency Response Graph (LPF — Schematic Visualization)

```
   Gain (dB)
     |
  A0 |----------------------____           ← Pass-band (flat region)
     |                  ___/
   -3|_________________/   ← f_c (cutoff) - 3 dB point
     |              ___/
 -23 |          ___/        ← One decade above f_c
     |      ___/
 -43 |__ ___/               ← Two decades above f_c
     |_____________________________
     10    10²   10³  10⁴   10⁵     Frequency (Hz)
              ↑
             f_c
        (Slope = -20 dB/decade)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Bode Plot:** Each **decade** (factor of 10) along the frequency axis beyond $f_c$ causes the gain to drop by **20 dB** (1st-order) or **40 dB** (2nd-order). This is the *asymptotic* roll-off.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define an active filter. List two advantages of active filters over passive filters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An active filter is a frequency-selective network that uses an **active device** (typically an op-amp like µA741) along with **passive components** ($R$ and $C$) to filter signals. **(1 mark)**
**Two advantages:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Gain capability:** Active filters can provide gain $> 1$; passive filters always have gain $\leq 1$.
2. **No inductors required:** Active filters use small, cheap $R$ and $C$ instead of bulky, expensive, and lossy inductors.
3. (Bonus point) **Isolation/buffering:** High input impedance and low output impedance prevent loading effects.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. For a first-order active low pass filter with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$, calculate the cutoff frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{10^4}{2\pi} = 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(1 mark)** Formula, **(1 mark)** substitution, **(1 mark)** final answer with units.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Level Model Question, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order active **low pass filter** using a non-inverting op-amp configuration. Clearly show the steps from the voltage divider to the final $H(j\omega)$ expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the above filter, given $R = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.033 \, \mu F$, $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, calculate: **(i)** the cutoff frequency $f_c$, **(ii)** the pass-band gain $A_0$ (in V/V and dB), and **(iii)** the output voltage magnitude when $V_{in} = 1 \text{ V}_{rms}$ at $f = 2 f_c$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a non-inverting op-amp with the input network consisting of $R$ in series from $V_{in}$ to node $V_a$, and $C$ from $V_a$ to ground. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Since the op-amp draws no current at its inputs, the RC network is a simple voltage divider in the s-domain. **(1 mark)**

$$
V_a(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$: **(1 mark)**

$$
V_a(s) = \dfrac{V_{in}(s)}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 4:** The non-inverting op-amp multiplies $V_a$ by the noise gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$: **(1 mark)**

$$
V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot \dfrac{V_{in}(s)}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 5:** Define the transfer function: **(1 mark)**

$$
H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ for sinusoidal steady-state: **(1 mark)**

$$
H(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 7:** Magnitude form: **(1 mark)**

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Cutoff frequency:** **(2 marks)**

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 0.033 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1.551 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{9.745 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1026.2 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.026 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Pass-band gain:** **(2 marks)**

$$
A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \dfrac{10k}{10k} = 2 \text{ V/V}
$$

$$
A_{0,dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) Output voltage at $f = 2 f_c$:** **(3 marks)**

First, compute $\omega RC$ at $f = 2 f_c$:

$$
\omega RC = 2\pi (2f_c) R C = 2 \times (2\pi f_c RC) = 2 \times 1 = 2
$$

Then:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{2}{2.236} = 0.894
$$

$$
V_{out} = \vert H \vert \times V_{in} = 0.894 \times 1 \text{ V}_{rms} = 0.894 \text{ V}_{rms}
$$

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a first-order active **high pass filter** using op-amp µA741. Derive its transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a first-order active LPF with cutoff frequency $f_c = 5 \text{ kHz}$ and unity pass-band gain. Choose a suitable capacitor and find the resistor. What is the gain in dB at $50 \text{ kHz}$? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Working and derivation of HPF (7 marks):**

**Working:** The series capacitor $C$ at the input blocks DC and low frequencies, presenting a high impedance at low $f$. At high frequencies, $C$ acts as a short, and the input is passed to the non-inverting terminal. The op-amp is in non-inverting configuration with gain $A_0 = 1 + R_f/R_1$. The output is a high-pass-filtered version of the input. **(3 marks for explanation + diagram)**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The input divider now has $C$ in series and $R$ to ground:

$$
V_a(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{R}{R + 1/sC} = V_{in}(s) \cdot \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

After amplification:

$$
H(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \, sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

$$
H(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_0 \, j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

---

**(b) Design calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Choose C.** Let $C = 0.01 \, \mu F = 10 \text{ nF}$ (a standard, easily available value). **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Calculate R.** **(2 marks)**

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 3183.1 \, \Omega
$$

Use standard value $R = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 series). **(1 mark for choosing standard value)**

**Step 3: Unity gain.** For $A_0 = 1$, configure the op-amp as a **voltage follower** ($R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$, or simply short output to inverting input). **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Gain in dB at $f = 50 \text{ kHz}$ (one decade above $f_c$):** **(2 marks)**

Since 50 kHz is exactly one decade (factor of 10) above $f_c = 5$ kHz, the gain drops by 20 dB:

$$
A_{dB}(50 \text{ kHz}) = A_{0,dB} - 20 = 0 - 20 = -20 \text{ dB}
$$

**Verification using transfer function:** $\omega RC = 2\pi \times 50000 \times 3183.1 \times 10^{-8} = 10$

$$
\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1 \times 10}{\sqrt{1 + 100}} = \dfrac{10}{10.05} = 0.995
$$

$$
A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.995) = -0.043 \text{ dB} \text{ (at 50 kHz, after decade)}
$$

Wait — let me re-verify. The magnitude equation at $f = 10 f_c$ (one decade above):

$$
\vert H \vert = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot (\omega RC)}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10}{\sqrt{1+100}} \approx 0.995 \approx 0 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL — KTU Valuation:** A common student mistake is **confusing LPF roll-off with HPF roll-off** in the pass-band direction. The **-20 dB/decade** for an LPF happens *above* $f_c$ (in the stop band). For an HPF, the **+20 dB/decade** slope happens *below* $f_c$ (in the stop band, where the signal is being rejected). At $f = 10 f_c$ for a **1st-order HPF**, the gain is approximately $A_0$ (in the pass band), NOT $-20$ dB below $A_0$.

The correct statement: For a **1st-order LPF**, at $f = 10 f_c$, the gain is $A_0 - 20$ dB. For a **1st-order HPF**, at $f = 0.1 f_c$, the gain is $A_0 - 20$ dB. At $f = 10 f_c$ for an HPF, the gain is essentially $A_0$ (in pass-band). The **Question B's design is for an LPF** — so the original analysis is correct.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $2\pi$** when computing $f_c$ from $R$ and $C$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing LPF and HPF equations** — the $sRC$ term is in the denominator for LPF and numerator for HPF. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Not specifying units** in the final answer ($Hz$ for $f_c$, $dB$ for gain). (–0.5 to –1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps** — KTU examiners allocate marks for *each* step; do not jump from $V_a$ to $H(s)$. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting to convert to RMS or peak** when giving the final output voltage.
> 6. **Using a non-standard resistor value** in design problems — always mention the **nearest E12 standard value**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Active Filter:** Uses op-amp + $R$ + $C$. Provides **gain**, **isolation**, and **no inductors**.
- [x] **Cutoff frequency formula (universal):** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — applies to **both** LPF and HPF (1st-order).
- [x] **LPF Transfer function:** $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega RC}$ — gain **decreases** with frequency.
- [x] **HPF Transfer function:** $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$ — gain **increases** with frequency.
- [x] **At $f = f_c$:** Gain drops by exactly **3 dB** below $A_0$, regardless of filter type.
- [x] **Roll-off rate:** 1st order = **20 dB/decade**; 2nd order = **40 dB/decade**.
- [x] **Pass-band gain (non-inverting):** $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$. For **unity gain**, use voltage follower.
- [x] **Phase:** LPF goes from $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$; HPF goes from $+90^\circ$ to $0^\circ$. At $f_c$, phase shift is $\pm 45^\circ$.
- [x] **Standard op-amp for lab:** **µA741** (single op-amp, 8-pin DIP).
- [x] **Standard capacitor values:** $0.01 \, \mu F$, $0.033 \, \mu F$, $0.1 \, \mu F$, $1 \, \mu F$ (common in lab).
- [x] **Bode plot:** X-axis = $\log_{10}(f)$ (semi-log paper); Y-axis = gain in dB.
- [x] **Lab procedure tip:** Set input amplitude constant (e.g., $V_{in} = 1 \text{ V}_{pp}$), vary frequency from $10 \text{ Hz}$ to $100 \text{ kHz}$, and measure $V_{out}$ at each frequency. Calculate gain in dB and plot.
- [x] **Quality factor $Q$** matters for **2nd-order** filters (Sallen-Key, Multiple-Feedback). $A_0$ must be **strictly less than 3** to avoid oscillation.
- [x] **Instrumentation check:** Always verify op-amp supply voltages ($\pm 12$ V or $\pm 15$ V) and **null offset** before taking readings.
- [x] **Final answer format in KTU:** Always state the **formula used**, **substitution step**, and **boxed final answer with correct units**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Active Filters and Frequency Response**
> These notes are aligned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** syllabus for **PCBML307 - Analog Electronics Lab**, covering the design, analysis, and verification of first-order active filters. The frequency response plotting technique described here is directly applicable to your **continuous evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Examination (ESE)** record submission.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision Rectifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Precision Rectifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Precision Rectifier** (also called a *Super Diode* or *Active Rectifier*) is an op-amp based electronic circuit that performs rectification of alternating input signals whose amplitude is **less than the forward voltage drop (≈ 0.7 V for silicon diodes)** of conventional diodes. By placing the diode inside the feedback loop of an operational amplifier, the op-amp's high open-loop gain effectively cancels the diode's non-linear forward voltage drop, producing an output that is a near-ideal rectified replica of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBML307):** In the Analog Electronics Lab, Experiment 2 typically involves the design, hardware realization, and verification of Half-Wave and Full-Wave Precision Rectifiers using the µA741 / LM741 op-amp. Students are expected to plot the input-output transfer characteristics and compute the rectification efficiency.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to bail water out of a boat using a small bucket with a hole in it. No matter how carefully you scoop, water leaks out before you can transfer it. A **normal diode** is exactly like that leaky bucket: when the input voltage is smaller than **0.7 V**, the water (current) leaks out through the diode's barrier potential, and the output never sees the small signal.

Now place the entire bucket inside a **hydraulic press (the op-amp)**. The press senses the leak and squeezes the bucket harder, compensating for every drop lost. The result: even a tiny trickle of water is successfully transferred. That compensating force is what the op-amp's enormous open-loop gain does for the diode — it **forces the diode to behave as an ideal switch**, regardless of how small the input signal is.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Diode threshold voltage ($V_D$):** $\approx 0.7 \text{ V}$ for silicon, $\approx 0.3 \text{ V}$ for germanium.
- **Op-amp open-loop gain ($A_{OL}$):** typically $2 \times 10^5$ for µA741.
- **Effective diode drop after compensation:** $\dfrac{V_D}{1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta} \approx \mu\text{V}$ range.
- **Saturation voltage of op-amp output:** $\pm V_{sat} \approx \pm 13 \text{ V}$ when powered by $\pm 15 \text{ V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** A precision rectifier allows you to rectify signals in the **mV range** — something completely impossible with a diode-and-resistor network alone. This is the foundation of medical instrumentation (ECG/EEG), signal demodulation, and AC voltmeters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Ordinary Diodes Fail Below 0.7 V

A standard half-wave rectifier built with a single diode and resistor works only when the peak input voltage $V_{in(peak)} > 0.7 \text{ V}$. For $V_{in} < 0.7 \text{ V}$, the diode remains reverse-biased (or barely forward-biased) and the output stays at zero. This non-linearity introduces:

1. **Dead zone / dead-band** in the transfer characteristic.
2. **Distortion** for low-amplitude signals.
3. **Temperature-dependent errors** because $V_D$ changes by $-2 \text{ mV/}^\circ\text{C}$.

### 2.2 The "Super Diode" Concept — Putting Diode Inside the Feedback Loop

The core idea is to **enclose the diode within the negative feedback path of an op-amp**. The op-amp continuously adjusts its output until the voltage across the diode (as "sensed" at the inverting input) is correct. Mathematically, if the op-amp output is $V_o'$ and the inverting input is at virtual ground ($V^- \approx 0$):

$$V_o' = V^- + V_D \approx V_D \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$$

Because of the open-loop gain, the op-amp output $V_o'$ actually swings to whatever value is necessary to make the feedback equation true — so even a few microvolts of input can be rectified.

### 2.3 Half-Wave Precision Rectifier (Super Diode) Operation

Consider the standard configuration: op-amp with $R_1$ from $V_{in}$ to the inverting input, $R_f$ in feedback, and a diode pair ($D_1$, $D_2$) arranged so that only one conducts per half-cycle.

**Case 1: $V_{in} > 0$**
- Op-amp output swings negative.
- $D_1$ (reverse path) conducts, $D_2$ (feedback path) is OFF.
- The feedback loop is broken → op-amp saturates negative.
- $V_{out} = 0 \text{ V}$ (output is connected to ground through $D_1$ and the load).

**Case 2: $V_{in} < 0$**
- Op-amp output swings positive.
- $D_2$ conducts, $D_1$ is OFF.
- Negative feedback is active → $V^- = V^+ = 0$ (virtual ground).
- The op-amp acts as an inverting amplifier: $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The output is non-inverting in the conventional super-diode configuration shown in most textbooks. The sign depends on whether the op-amp is inverting or non-inverting topology. KTU lab manuals typically use the **non-inverting super diode** for direct rectification without sign inversion.

### 2.4 Full-Wave Precision Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)

The full-wave version is built by:
1. A half-wave precision rectifier (front-end).
2. A summing amplifier (back-end) that adds the half-wave output to the original input with proper scaling.

The result is a true absolute-value function:

$$V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert \quad \text{for all } V_{in}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Effective diode drop inside feedback | $V_{D,eff} = \dfrac{V_D}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ | Volts | $V_{D,eff} \to 0$ as $A_{OL} \to \infty$ |
| 2 | Super-diode output (positive half) | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | Volts | $V_{in} > 0$ |
| 3 | Super-diode output (negative half) | $V_{out} = 0$ | Volts | $V_{in} \le 0$ |
| 4 | Full-wave rectified output | $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$ | Volts | All $V_{in}$ |
| 5 | Rectification Efficiency (Full-Wave) | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 0.812$ | Dimensionless | Pure sine wave |
| 6 | Rectification Efficiency (Half-Wave) | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{\pi^2/4} \approx 0.405$ | Dimensionless | Pure sine wave |
| 7 | Ripple Frequency (Full-Wave) | $f_r = 2 f_{in}$ | Hz | Mains frequency 50 Hz → 100 Hz ripple |
| 8 | Peak Inverse Voltage (no diode in P.R.) | $PIV = V_{in(peak)}$ | Volts | Limited by op-amp rails |
| 9 | Closed-loop gain (inverting amp) | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Dimensionless | Resistor matched |
| 10 | Slew-rate limited bandwidth | $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_{in(peak)}}$ | Hz | $SR \approx 0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ for µA741 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Precision rectifiers are the heart of **AC voltmeters**, **audio signal demodulators**, **RMS converters**, **peak detectors** in instrumentation, **signal conditioning** for ADCs in sensor interfaces, and **medical electronics** (ECG machines) where signals of a few millivolts must be rectified for analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Diode Voltage Drop in the Super Diode

**Goal:** Prove that placing a diode inside the op-amp's feedback loop reduces its effective forward drop to a near-zero value.

**Starting Point — The op-amp open-loop equation:**

$$V_o' = A_{OL}\,(V^+ - V^-)$$

where $V_o'$ is the op-amp output *before* the diode, $V^+ = V_{in}$ (applied to the non-inverting terminal via a resistor), and $V^-$ is the inverting terminal voltage.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL through the feedback network:**

When the feedback diode $D_2$ is forward-biased, the voltage at the inverting terminal $V^-$ equals the output node voltage $V_{out}$ (since no current flows into the ideal op-amp input). The voltage across the diode is:

$$V_D = V_o' - V_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Express $V_{out}$ in terms of $V_o'$ and the diode drop:**

$$V_{out} = V_o' - V_D$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the op-amp equation:**

Because the op-amp forces $V^- \approx V^+$ (virtual short), and the input resistor connects $V_{in}$ to $V^-$ through $R_1$, we have:

$$V_o' = A_{OL}\,(V_{in} - V_{out})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

Rearranging:

$$V_o' = A_{OL}\,V_{in} - A_{OL}\,V_{out}$$

$$V_{out} = V_o' - V_D$$

Substituting the second into the first:

$$A_{OL}\,V_{in} = V_o' + A_{OL}\,V_o' - A_{OL}\,V_D$$

$$A_{OL}\,V_{in} = V_o'(1 + A_{OL}) - A_{OL}\,V_D$$

$$V_o' = \dfrac{A_{OL}\,V_{in} + A_{OL}\,V_D}{1 + A_{OL}}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out} = V_o' - V_D = \dfrac{A_{OL}\,V_{in} + A_{OL}\,V_D}{1 + A_{OL}} - V_D$$

Carrying out the algebra:

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{A_{OL}\,V_{in} + A_{OL}\,V_D - V_D(1 + A_{OL})}{1 + A_{OL}}$$

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{A_{OL}\,V_{in} + A_{OL}\,V_D - V_D - A_{OL}\,V_D}{1 + A_{OL}}$$

The two $A_{OL}\,V_D$ terms cancel:

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{A_{OL}\,V_{in} - V_D}{1 + A_{OL}}$$

**Step 6 — Take the limit as $A_{OL} \to \infty$:**

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{A_{OL}\,V_{in} - V_D}{1 + A_{OL}} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_D/A_{OL}}{1/A_{OL} + 1}$$

$$\lim_{A_{OL} \to \infty}\, V_{out} = V_{in}$$

**Conclusion:** The diode's $V_D$ is divided by $A_{OL}$, making the effective threshold voltage virtually zero. For a µA741 with $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$:

$$V_{D,eff} = \dfrac{0.7}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.5 \,\mu\text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why a precision rectifier can rectify a **5 mV** AC signal — a feat utterly impossible for a passive diode rectifier.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Output of the Full-Wave Precision Rectifier (Absolute Value Circuit)

**Circuit Topology:**
- Stage 1: Super-diode half-wave rectifier producing $V_A$.
- Stage 2: Summing amplifier combining $V_{in}$ and $V_A$ to produce $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$.

**Step 1 — Half-wave stage output $V_A$:**

For a unity-gain super-diode with $R_1 = R_f$:

$$V_A = \begin{cases} V_{in} & \text{if } V_{in} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } V_{in} \le 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Summing amplifier output $V_{out}$:**

The summing amplifier has two inputs: $V_{in}$ (through $R_1$) and $V_A$ (through $R_2$), with feedback resistor $R_f$ (typically $R_f = R_1 = R_2$).

$$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_A}{R_2} \right) = -(V_{in} + V_A)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for $V_{in} > 0$:**

$$V_{out} = -(V_{in} + V_{in}) = -2 V_{in}$$

But because of the inverting sign convention, the actual output (after the typical textbook sign arrangement using a non-inverting summer or output inversion stage) is:

$$\vert V_{out} \vert = 2 V_{in}$$

To obtain a true $\vert V_{in} \vert$ with peak equal to $V_{in(peak)}$, the resistors are scaled so that the gain is $\frac{1}{2}$ for each input:

$$V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{2R_1}\right)(V_{in} + V_A) = -\dfrac{1}{2}(V_{in} + V_A)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for both halves:**

For $V_{in} > 0$ ($V_A = V_{in}$):

$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{2}(V_{in} + V_{in}) = -V_{in}$$

For $V_{in} < 0$ ($V_A = 0$):

$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{2}(V_{in} + 0) = -\dfrac{V_{in}}{2}$$

Wait — this does not give $\vert V_{in} \vert$ directly. The classic absolute-value circuit (Fig. 5.21 in Sedra/Smith) uses a slightly different topology with **two op-amps**: the first super-diode inverts and rectifies, and the second summer produces the final magnitude.

The **canonical form** is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} - 2 V_A$$

For $V_{in} > 0$: $V_{out} = V_{in} - 2V_{in} = -V_{in}$
For $V_{in} < 0$: $V_{out} = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$

In both cases: $V_{out} = -\vert V_{in} \vert$. The sign is inverted relative to a true absolute value, but the magnitude is identical, and adding a final unity-gain inverter yields a positive absolute value.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Rectification Efficiency of a Full-Wave Rectifier

**Given:** Input $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ applied to a full-wave precision rectifier.

**Step 1 — DC (average) component of full-wave rectified output:**

$$V_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin\theta\, d\theta = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}[-\cos\theta]_0^{\pi} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 2 — RMS value of the input sine wave:**

$$V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 — DC power delivered to load resistor $R_L$:**

$$P_{DC} = \dfrac{V_{DC}^2}{R_L} = \dfrac{(2V_m/\pi)^2}{R_L} = \dfrac{4V_m^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$

**Step 4 — AC power in the input:**

$$P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_{RMS}^2}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2R_L}$$

**Step 5 — Rectification efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} = \dfrac{4V_m^2/\pi^2 R_L}{V_m^2/2R_L} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 0.812$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Passive Diode Rectifier | Precision Rectifier (Op-Amp) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Minimum input voltage | $\ge 0.7 \text{ V}$ (Si) | $\ge 0 \text{ V}$ (theoretically) |
| Effective threshold | $V_D = 0.7 \text{ V}$ | $V_D / (1 + A_{OL}\beta) \to 0$ |
| Transfer linearity | Piecewise, dead-zone | Near-ideal, continuous |
| Temperature sensitivity | $-2 \text{ mV/}^\circ\text{C}$ | Negligible (op-amp compensates) |
| Frequency response | Wide (diode only) | Limited by op-amp slew rate |
| Cost / complexity | Very low | Moderate (IC + few Rs + 2 D) |
| Output drive capability | High | Limited by op-amp output current |
| Typical application | Power supplies | Instrumentation, signal processing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Half-Wave Precision Rectifier (Super Diode) — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **signal-flow architecture** of the non-inverting half-wave precision rectifier, mapping the functional interaction between the op-amp, diodes, and feedback network.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeIn["INPUT V_IN SINE WAVE"] --> nodeR1["INPUT RESISTOR R1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeInv["INVERTING NODE V_NEG"]
    nodeNonInv["NON INVERTING INPUT GROUND"] --> nodeInv
    nodeInv --> nodeOpAmp["OP AMP UA741"]
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeOutA["OP AMP OUTPUT NODE"]
    nodeOutA --> nodeD1["DIODE D1 FEEDBACK PATH"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeFB["FEEDBACK NODE V_OUT"]
    nodeOutA --> nodeD2["DIODE D2 TO GROUND"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeGND["GROUND"]
    nodeFB --> nodeRf["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R_F"]
    nodeRf --> nodeInv
    nodeFB --> nodeLoad["LOAD R_L"]
    nodeLoad --> nodeFinalOut["RECTIFIED OUTPUT V_OUT"]
```

**Operating Logic:**
- During the **positive half-cycle**, the op-amp output swings negative, reverse-biasing $D_1$ and forward-biasing $D_2$. $D_2$ shunts the output to ground → $V_{out} = 0$.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, the op-amp output swings positive, forward-biasing $D_1$ and reverse-biasing $D_2$. The feedback loop closes through $D_1$ → $V_{out} = -(R_f/R_1) \cdot V_{in}$.

---

### 4.2 Full-Wave Precision Rectifier — Two-Stage Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSrc["INPUT V_IN"] --> nodeR1a["R1 TO STAGE 1 INVERTING INPUT"]
    nodeR1a --> nodeOp1["OP AMP 1 SUPER DIODE"]
    nodeOp1 --> nodeDiodeA["DIODE D_A FORWARD PATH"]
    nodeDiodeA --> nodeVA["NODE V_A HALF WAVE OUTPUT"]
    nodeOp1 --> nodeDiodeB["DIODE D_B RETURN PATH"]
    nodeDiodeB --> nodeGND2["GROUND"]
    nodeVA --> nodeR2a["RESISTOR R2 INTO SUMMER"]
    nodeSrc --> nodeR2b["RESISTOR R3 DIRECT INPUT TO SUMMER"]
    nodeR2a --> nodeOp2["OP AMP 2 SUMMING AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeR2b --> nodeOp2
    nodeOp2 --> nodeRf2["FEEDBACK R_F OF SUMMER"]
    nodeRf2 --> nodeOp2
    nodeOp2 --> nodeOutFinal["FULL WAVE RECTIFIED OUTPUT V_OUT_EQUALS_ABS_V_IN"]
```

**Operating Logic:**
- **Stage 1** produces $V_A = V_{in}$ when $V_{in} > 0$ and $V_A = 0$ when $V_{in} \le 0$.
- **Stage 2** combines $V_{in}$ and $V_A$ in a weighted summer such that the output is the full-wave rectified version: $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$.

---

### 4.3 Transfer Characteristic Curves (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeTCin["V_IN AXIS"] --> nodeTC0["ORIGIN 0 VOLT"]
    nodeTC0 --> nodeTCslope1["POSITIVE SLOPE +1 REGION FOR V_IN NEGATIVE"]
    nodeTCslope1 --> nodeTCflat["FLAT ZERO REGION FOR V_IN POSITIVE"]
    nodeTCflat --> nodeTCout["V_OUT AXIS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The slope of the transfer characteristic in the conducting half-cycle is set by the ratio $-R_f / R_1$. With $R_f = R_1$, the slope is $-1$ (inverting) or $+1$ (non-inverting super diode).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is a precision rectifier? Why is it called a "super diode"?

**Model Answer:**

A precision rectifier is an op-amp based circuit that performs rectification of AC signals whose amplitude is **less than the cut-in voltage (≈ 0.7 V) of a silicon diode**. It uses an op-amp with the diode placed inside its negative feedback loop, so the op-amp's high open-loop gain compensates for the diode's forward voltage drop.

It is called a "**super diode**" because the combination behaves as an **ideal diode** — a perfect one-way switch with **zero forward voltage drop** and **infinite reverse resistance**. The effective threshold becomes $V_D / (1 + A_{OL}\beta)$, which for a typical op-amp is a few microvolts.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for op-amp feedback concept, 1 for "super diode" justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State two advantages of a precision rectifier over a conventional diode rectifier.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Ability to rectify small signals:** A precision rectifier can rectify input voltages of the order of millivolts, whereas a conventional diode rectifier requires inputs greater than **0.7 V** to overcome the diode's cut-in voltage.

2. **Temperature stability:** The forward voltage drop of a diode changes by $-2 \text{ mV/}^\circ\text{C}$, but in a precision rectifier the op-amp's feedback loop continuously compensates for this drift, making the output nearly independent of temperature.

*(Additional valid points: low distortion, ideal transfer characteristic, sharp rectification threshold.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **half-wave precision rectifier** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the output voltage in both half-cycles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **full-wave precision rectifier** to produce an output $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$ for a sinusoidal input of amplitude **2 V peak** and frequency **1 kHz**. Specify the op-amp, resistor values, and diode type. Compute the rectification efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Half-Wave Precision Rectifier Operation:**

- Draw the circuit: op-amp µA741, $R_1$ from $V_{in}$ to inverting input, $R_f$ as feedback resistor, two diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ at the op-amp output.
- **Positive half-cycle ($V_{in} > 0$):**
  - Op-amp output $V_o'$ goes **negative** because of inverting amplifier action.
  - $D_2$ (connected from output to ground with correct polarity) becomes **forward-biased** and clamps the output to $V_{out} \approx -0.7 \text{ V}$ (one diode drop).
  - $D_1$ (feedback path) is **reverse-biased** → feedback loop is open.
  - $V_{out} = 0 \text{ V}$ (effectively at the load through $R_f$).

- **Negative half-cycle ($V_{in} < 0$):**
  - Op-amp output $V_o'$ goes **positive**.
  - $D_1$ is **forward-biased**, closing the feedback loop.
  - $D_2$ is **reverse-biased** → disconnected.
  - The op-amp acts as an inverting amplifier:

$$V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$$

- **Derivation of effective diode drop:**

$$V_{D,eff} = \dfrac{V_D}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}, \quad \beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

For $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ and $\beta = 0.5$:

$$V_{D,eff} = \dfrac{0.7}{1 + 10^5} = 7 \,\mu\text{V} \approx 0$$

- **Transfer characteristic:** Piecewise linear with zero slope for $V_{in} > 0$ and slope $-R_f/R_1$ for $V_{in} < 0$.

**Part (b) — Full-Wave Precision Rectifier Design:**

**Specifications:**
- Input: $V_m = 2 \text{ V}$ peak, $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$
- Output: $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$
- Op-amp: **µA741** (or LM741, dual supply $\pm 15 \text{ V}$)
- Diodes: **1N4148** (signal diode, low forward drop, fast switching) or **1N914**

**Resistor Selection:**
- For unity-gain super-diode stage: $R_1 = R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- For summing stage with $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$: choose $R_1' = R_2' = R_f' = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and add a unity-gain inverter if positive output is required.

**Output Verification:**
- For $V_{in} = 2 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot t)$:
  - Positive half: $V_{out} = +2 \text{ V}$
  - Negative half: $V_{out} = +2 \text{ V}$ (rectified)
  - Output is a **pulsating DC** with peak 2 V and frequency **2 kHz** (double the input).

**Rectification Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} = 0.812 = 81.2\%$$

**Slew Rate Check:**
- $SR_{required} = 2\pi f V_m = 2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 2 = 12{,}566 \text{ V/s} = 0.0126 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$
- µA741 slew rate is $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ → **design is valid**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the limitations of a **conventional half-wave diode rectifier**. How does a precision rectifier overcome these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a precision full-wave rectifier, derive the expression for **rectification efficiency**. Compare the efficiency of a full-wave precision rectifier with that of a half-wave precision rectifier for a **sine wave input of 5 V RMS**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Limitations of Conventional Rectifier:**

1. **Diode forward voltage drop:** A silicon diode requires a minimum of **0.7 V** to conduct. Signals below this threshold are not rectified — a "dead zone" appears in the transfer characteristic.

2. **Non-linear V-I characteristic:** Diode current is exponential in voltage ($I = I_S(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1)$), causing harmonic distortion for small signals.

3. **Temperature dependence:** $V_D$ decreases by **2 mV/°C**, shifting the rectification threshold with temperature.

4. **Limited frequency response at low voltages:** The diode's junction capacitance causes roll-off, distorting high-frequency small signals.

**How the Precision Rectifier Overcomes These:**

- The op-amp's **high open-loop gain** $A_{OL}$ divides the diode's $V_D$, making the effective threshold:

$$V_{D,eff} = \dfrac{V_D}{1 + A_{OL}\beta} \approx 0$$

- The diode operates inside the feedback loop, so its non-linearity is "swallowed" by the linearizing action of negative feedback.
- Temperature drift of $V_D$ is automatically corrected by the op-amp's output adjustment.
- For small signals (mV range), the precision rectifier delivers near-ideal rectification.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Rectification Efficiency:**

For a full-wave rectified sine wave $V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

**DC component:**

$$V_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin\theta \, d\theta = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$$

**RMS value of the input:**

$$V_{RMS,in} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**RMS value of the full-wave output:**

$$V_{RMS,out} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Power delivered to load $R_L$:**

$$P_{DC} = \dfrac{V_{DC}^2}{R_L} = \dfrac{4 V_m^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$

$$P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_{RMS,in}^2}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{FW} = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} = \dfrac{4 V_m^2/\pi^2 R_L}{V_m^2/2R_L} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \approx 0.812$$

**For half-wave rectification:**

$$V_{DC,HW} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$$

$$\eta_{HW} = \dfrac{V_m^2/\pi^2 R_L}{V_m^2/2R_L} = \dfrac{2}{\pi^2} \approx 0.405$$

**Numerical Comparison for $V_{RMS} = 5 \text{ V}$:**

- $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 5 = 7.07 \text{ V}$
- $V_{DC,FW} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 7.07}{\pi} = 4.50 \text{ V}$
- $V_{DC,HW} = \dfrac{7.07}{\pi} = 2.25 \text{ V}$

| Parameter | Half-Wave | Full-Wave |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $V_{DC}$ | 2.25 V | 4.50 V |
| Ripple frequency | 1 kHz | 2 kHz |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | 40.5% | 81.2% |
| Ripple factor $\gamma$ | 1.21 | 0.482 |

The full-wave precision rectifier is **twice as efficient** as the half-wave version and produces a smoother output (lower ripple).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the feedback path:** Many students draw the op-amp with the diode but forget that the diode must be **inside the negative feedback loop**. Without feedback, the op-amp simply saturates and the circuit does not work.
> 2. **Sign errors in transfer equations:** Students often confuse inverting and non-inverting super-diode configurations. Always re-derive using the virtual short principle.
> 3. **Ignoring slew rate:** For high-frequency inputs, the op-amp's slew rate may not be fast enough to follow the signal. Compute $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_m)$ and verify before claiming the design works.
> 4. **Mixing up rectification efficiency formulas:** Half-wave gives $2/\pi^2 \approx 40.5\%$; full-wave gives $8/\pi^2 \approx 81.2\%$. Marks are commonly lost for swapping these.
> 5. **Diode polarity mistakes:** Reversing $D_1$ and $D_2$ results in zero output or saturation. Always verify with the input polarity during design.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precision Rectifier Definition:** Op-amp based rectifier that compensates for the diode's forward voltage drop by placing the diode inside the feedback loop.
- **Super Diode Threshold:** $V_{D,eff} = V_D / (1 + A_{OL}\beta) \to 0$ as $A_{OL} \to \infty$.
- **Half-Wave Output:** $V_{out} = -(R_f/R_1) V_{in}$ for one half-cycle; $0$ for the other.
- **Full-Wave Output:** $V_{out} = \vert V_{in} \vert$ (true absolute-value circuit).
- **Rectification Efficiency:** Full-wave = **81.2%**; Half-wave = **40.5%**.
- **Ripple Frequency:** Full-wave = **$2f$**; Half-wave = **$f$**.
- **Ripple Factor:** Full-wave = **0.482**; Half-wave = **1.21**.
- **Op-Amp IC Used in KTU Lab:** µA741 / LM741 with $\pm 15 \text{ V}$ dual supply.
- **Diodes Preferred:** 1N4148 (signal) or 1N4007 (power) depending on current.
- **Slew Rate Constraint:** $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_m)$ — must be greater than input frequency.
- **Component Rule of Thumb:** $R_1 = R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ is a safe starting choice for unity-gain designs.
- **Output Polarity:** Depends on whether the configuration is inverting or non-inverting. Check before connecting to the next stage.
- **Lab Verification:** Always plot $V_{out}$ vs $V_{in}$ on an oscilloscope or X-Y mode to confirm the piecewise linear transfer characteristic.
- **Real-World Applications:** AC voltmeters, peak detectors, signal demodulators, instrumentation amplifiers, medical electronics (ECG/EEG).
- **Key Limitation:** Precision rectifiers are for **low-power signal applications**, not for power rectification. For power supplies, use a transformer + diode bridge + filter capacitor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrators (Using IC 555/Op-Amp)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML307 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrators (Using IC 555/Op-Amp)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Multivibrator?

A **Multivibrator** is an electronic circuit that switches between two stable (or quasi-stable) output states using regenerative positive feedback. It is essentially a two-stage amplifier whose output is cross-coupled back to the input such that one stage conducts (ON) while the other remains cut-off (OFF). Multivibrators are the fundamental building blocks of pulse, square-wave, and timing circuits in all digital and analog timing systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Lab Manual):** "A multivibrator is a regenerative circuit that employs two active devices (transistors/op-amp/555 timer) cross-coupled by resistive or RC networks to generate non-sinusoidal waveforms such as square, rectangular, or pulse outputs."

## 1.2 Three Classes of Multivibrators

| Type | Stable States | External Trigger? | Typical Output |
|------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Astable** (Free-running) | None — oscillates continuously | Not required | Continuous Square Wave |
| **Monostable** (One-shot) | One stable, one quasi-stable | Required to switch | Single Pulse of fixed width |
| **Bistable** (Flip-flop) | Two stable states | Required to toggle | Latched HIGH or LOW |

> [!NOTE]
> For **PCBML307 – Module Experiment 2**, only **Astable** and **Monostable** configurations are in the syllabus, using either the **IC 555 timer** or an **Op-Amp (µA741)** as the active element.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket Switch"

Imagine a bucket with a hole at the bottom, placed under a tap:

- **Astable (Auto-Switch Tap):** The bucket fills up automatically, tips over, empties, comes back upright, and the cycle repeats **forever** without any human help. That is exactly how an **astable multivibrator** behaves — it has no stable state and keeps flipping between HIGH and LOW on its own.
- **Monostable (Push-Button Tap):** The bucket sits empty (stable state). When you **press a button (trigger)**, it tips, pours out a fixed amount of water for a fixed time (quasi-stable state), then rights itself and waits for the next button press. This is the **monostable multivibrator** — it stays at rest until triggered, then produces **one** pulse of fixed width.

## 1.4 The IC 555 Timer — A Quick Introduction

The **IC 555** is an 8-pin monolithic timing circuit introduced by **Signetics in 1972**. It can operate in three modes: **Astable, Monostable, and Bistable**. Internally, it contains two comparators, an SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider that creates two precise threshold levels at **1/3 Vcc** and **2/3 Vcc**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NE555** (bipolar) and **TLC555** (CMOS) variants are used in the KTU lab. Pin 8 = Vcc, Pin 1 = GND, Pin 3 = OUTPUT, Pin 7 = DISCHARGE.

## 1.5 Why this Experiment Matters

Astable and Monostable multivibrators are used in:
- **Traffic light controllers** (Astable)
- **Missing-pulse detectors** (Monostable)
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** for motor control
- **Debounce circuits** for mechanical switches
- **Analog-to-Digital converters** (dual-slope type)
- **Function generators** and **digital logic clocks**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IC 555 — Internal Architecture

The IC 555 contains four key sub-blocks:

1. **Resistive Voltage Divider (3 × 5 kΩ):** Creates reference voltages of **(1/3)Vcc** at the non-inverting input of the lower comparator, and **(2/3)Vcc** at the inverting input of the upper comparator.
2. **Comparator 1 (Threshold Comparator):** Compares the THRESHOLD pin (Pin 6) voltage with **(2/3)Vcc**.
3. **Comparator 2 (Trigger Comparator):** Compares the TRIGGER pin (Pin 2) voltage with **(1/3)Vcc**.
4. **SR Flip-Flop:** Latches the comparator outputs.
5. **Discharge Transistor (Pin 7):** An open-collector NPN that provides a low-resistance path to ground when activated.

**Operation Rule (KTU Board Exam Favourite):**
- When **Vthreshold > 2Vcc/3** → Flip-Flop RESET → Output LOW, Discharge ON.
- When **Vtrigger < Vcc/3** → Flip-Flop SET → Output HIGH, Discharge OFF.
- When **Vcc/3 < Vc < 2Vcc/3** → Output retains previous state (memory).

## 2.2 IC 555 in Astable Mode — Step-by-Step Logic

In the astable configuration, the capacitor **C** charges through **(R1 + R2)** toward Vcc, and discharges through **R2** only via the discharge transistor (Pin 7) to ground.

**Phase 1 — Capacitor Charging (Output HIGH):**
- Initially, the discharge transistor is OFF.
- C charges from **Vcc/3** toward Vcc through **R1** and **R2**.
- Output remains **HIGH** until capacitor voltage reaches **2Vcc/3**.
- The threshold comparator then trips → Flip-Flop resets → Output goes **LOW**, Discharge turns **ON**.

**Phase 2 — Capacitor Discharging (Output LOW):**
- Discharge transistor shorts Pin 7 to ground.
- C discharges from **2Vcc/3** toward **0V** through **R2** only.
- Output remains **LOW** until capacitor voltage falls to **Vcc/3**.
- The trigger comparator then trips → Flip-Flop sets → Output goes **HIGH**, Discharge turns **OFF**, and the cycle restarts.

## 2.3 IC 555 in Monostable Mode — Step-by-Step Logic

In the monostable configuration, the capacitor is connected only between **THRESHOLD (Pin 6) and DISCHARGE (Pin 7)** to a common node, with Pin 7 going to ground through the discharge transistor.

**Stable State (No Trigger):**
- The discharge transistor is **ON**, keeping the capacitor shorted to ground.
- Vthreshold < 2Vcc/3, so the upper comparator does not trip.
- Output is **LOW** (stable).

**Triggered State (After Negative Trigger Pulse on Pin 2):**
- A negative-going pulse pulls Vtrigger below **Vcc/3**.
- Lower comparator trips → Flip-Flop sets → Output goes **HIGH**, Discharge transistor turns **OFF**.
- Capacitor C now charges from **0V** toward Vcc through external resistor **R** only.
- Output stays **HIGH** until Vthreshold reaches **2Vcc/3**.
- At that instant, the upper comparator resets the flip-flop → Output returns to **LOW**, Discharge transistor turns **ON**, capacitor rapidly discharges to **0V**, and the circuit returns to its stable state — **awaiting the next trigger**.

## 2.4 Op-Amp Multivibrator — Conceptual Difference

An op-amp multivibrator uses the op-amp as a **Schmitt trigger** (regenerative comparator) combined with an **RC timing network**. The op-amp output saturates at **+Vsat** and **−Vsat** (typically ±13.5V for ±15V supply). The threshold voltages are set by the positive feedback fraction **β = R2/(R1+R2)**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table for the **ESE (End Semester Exam)** — direct formula questions carry **3 to 5 marks**.

| Circuit | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **555 Astable** | Charging time (t_HIGH) | $t_1 = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C$ | seconds |
| **555 Astable** | Discharging time (t_LOW) | $t_2 = 0.693 \, R_2 \, C$ | seconds |
| **555 Astable** | Total Time Period (T) | $T = 0.693 \, (R_1 + 2 R_2) \, C$ | seconds |
| **555 Astable** | Frequency (f) | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2 R_2) \, C}$ | Hz |
| **555 Astable** | Duty Cycle (D) | $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| **555 Monostable** | Output Pulse Width (T) | $T = 1.1 \, R \, C$ | seconds |
| **Op-Amp Astable** | Time Period (T) | $T = 2 R C \, \ln \left( \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta} \right)$ where $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | seconds |
| **Op-Amp Astable** (β = 0.473) | Frequency (f) | $f \approx \dfrac{1}{2.2 \, R C}$ | Hz |
| **Op-Amp Monostable** | Pulse Width (T) | $T = R C \, \ln \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \right)$ | seconds |
| **555 Threshold** | Upper Threshold | $V_{TH} = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ | volts |
| **555 Threshold** | Lower Threshold | $V_{TL} = \dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ | volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Duty Cycle Tip:** In a 555 astable, the duty cycle can **never** reach exactly 50% with a single R1, R2 network — minimum is 50.01%. For variable duty cycle, a **diode** is added in parallel with R2 to bypass it during charging.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astable:** Heart of digital wall clocks (32.768 kHz crystal divided down), PWM motor drivers in drones, strobe lights, and tone generators in electronic sirens.
- **Monostable:** Debouncing mechanical keyboard switches, generating precise time delays in industrial relay logic, and creating window comparators for safety interlocks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of 555 Astable Time Period (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

The capacitor charges from a starting voltage of **V_L = Vcc/3** toward a final voltage **Vcc** through the series combination **R1 + R2**. The general charging equation for a capacitor is:

$$V_c(t) = V_{\text{final}} - (V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}) \, e^{-t / \tau}$$

where **τ = (R1 + R2) C** is the charging time constant.

**Step 1 — Set up the charging condition.**
The charging phase ends when the capacitor voltage equals the upper threshold **V_TH = 2Vcc/3**.

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \left( V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3} \right) e^{-t_1 / ((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the bracket term.**

$$V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3}$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_1 / ((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the exponential term.**

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_1 / ((R_1+R_2)C)} = V_{CC} - \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$$

$$e^{-t_1 / ((R_1+R_2)C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-\frac{t_1}{(R_1 + R_2) C} = \ln \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = -0.693$$

**Step 5 — Solve for t1.**

$$t_1 = 0.693 \, (R_1 + R_2) \, C$$

This is the **HIGH time** of the output.

**Step 6 — Derive the discharging time (t2) using the same approach.**
Now C discharges from **V_TH = 2Vcc/3** down to **V_TL = Vcc/3** through resistor **R2** only, since Pin 7 shorts to ground. The time constant is **τ_disch = R2 C**.

Using the discharging equation:

$$V_c(t) = V_{\text{initial}} \, e^{-t / \tau}$$

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} \, e^{-t_2 / (R_2 C)}$$

$$e^{-t_2 / (R_2 C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$t_2 = 0.693 \, R_2 \, C$$

This is the **LOW time** of the output.

**Step 7 — Total Time Period T.**

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C + 0.693 \, R_2 \, C = 0.693(R_1 + 2 R_2) C$$

**Step 8 — Frequency f.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 \, (R_1 + 2R_2) \, C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2 R_2) \, C}$$

**Step 9 — Duty Cycle D.**

$$D = \frac{t_1}{T} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many students forget that the **discharge resistor is R2 only**, not (R1 + R2), because the discharge current flows directly from Pin 7 to ground, bypassing R1 entirely. This is the most common mistake costing 2 marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of 555 Monostable Pulse Width

In monostable mode, the capacitor charges from **0V** to **2Vcc/3** through a single resistor **R** (R1 in standard monostable schematics). The initial voltage across the capacitor is **0V** because the discharge transistor was ON before the trigger arrived.

**Step 1 — Write the charging equation.**

$$V_c(t) = V_{CC} - V_{CC} \, e^{-t/RC} = V_{CC} \left( 1 - e^{-t/RC} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Set the end condition Vc(t) = 2Vcc/3.**

$$\frac{2 V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} \left( 1 - e^{-T/RC} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by Vcc.**

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange.**

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Take natural log.**

$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln \left( \frac{1}{3} \right) = -1.0986$$

**Step 6 — Solve for T.**

$$T = 1.0986 \, R C \approx 1.1 \, R C$$

> [!NOTE]
> The pulse width is **independent of Vcc** — a key advantage of the 555. Changing the supply voltage does NOT change the output pulse width, only the amplitude.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style, 7-Mark Question)

**Problem:** Design a 555 astable multivibrator to generate a square wave of **1 kHz** frequency with a **60% duty cycle** using a **0.1 µF** capacitor. Calculate R1 and R2. Assume standard design.

**Given:** f = 1000 Hz, D = 0.60, C = 0.1 × 10⁻⁶ F.

**Step 1 — Express D in terms of R1, R2.**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2} = 0.60$$

**Step 2 — Express f in terms of R1, R2, C.**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2 R_2) C} = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Solve for (R1 + 2R2) from the frequency equation.**

$$R_1 + 2 R_2 = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{10^{-4}} = 14{,}400 \, \Omega = 14.4 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 4 — From the duty cycle equation, find R1 in terms of R2.**

$$0.60(R_1 + 2R_2) = R_1 + R_2$$
$$0.60 R_1 + 1.2 R_2 = R_1 + R_2$$
$$0.2 R_2 = 0.4 R_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 = 0.5 R_2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into R1 + 2R2 = 14.4 kΩ.**

$$0.5 R_2 + 2 R_2 = 14.4 \, k\Omega$$
$$2.5 R_2 = 14.4 \, k\Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_2 = 5.76 \, k\Omega \approx 5.6 \, k\Omega \text{ (standard E12)}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate R1.**

$$R_1 = 0.5 \times 5.6 \, k\Omega = 2.8 \, k\Omega \approx 2.7 \, k\Omega \text{ (standard E12)}$$

**Step 7 — Verify by computing T, t_HIGH, t_LOW.**

$$T = 0.693(2.7k + 2 \times 5.6k)(0.1\mu) = 0.693 \times 13.9k \times 0.1\mu = 0.963 \, ms \approx 1 \, ms \checkmark$$
$$f = 1/T \approx 1.038 \, kHz \, (\text{close to 1 kHz, within component tolerance}) \checkmark$$
$$D = \frac{2.7k + 5.6k}{2.7k + 11.2k} = \frac{8.3}{13.9} = 0.597 \approx 60\% \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **±5% tolerance** of standard E12 resistors (and ±10% for E6) means the designed frequency will deviate slightly. For precise frequencies, use **1% metal-film resistors** or a **trimmer potentiometer** in series with R1.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Op-Amp Astable Frequency

The op-amp astable uses a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger** with feedback fraction **β = R2/(R1+R2)**. The output saturates at **+Vsat** and **−Vsat**.

The upper and lower threshold voltages are:

$$V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \beta V_{sat}$$
$$V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = -\beta V_{sat}$$

The capacitor charges from **−βVsat** to **+βVsat** through R. Using the charging equation (similar to §3.1):

$$+\beta V_{sat} = -V_{sat} + (V_{sat} + \beta V_{sat}) (1 - e^{-t_1/RC})$$

Solving gives $t_1 = RC \ln \left( \dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta} \right)$.

By symmetry, **t_2 = t_1**, so the total period is:

$$T = 2RC \ln \left( \frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta} \right) = 2RC \ln \left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_2} \right)$$

The frequency:

$$f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln \left( 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_2} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case (R1 = R2):** Then β = 0.5, and $\ln(3) = 1.0986$, so $T = 2.197 RC \approx 2.2 RC$, giving $f \approx 1/(2.2RC)$. This is the **simplest textbook design** and is preferred for KTU lab experiments.

## 4.6 Component Selection Guidelines for PCBML307 Lab

| Component | Recommended Value (1 kHz Astable) | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|---------|
| R1 | 6.8 kΩ | Charging path resistor |
| R2 | 6.8 kΩ | Discharge path resistor |
| C | 0.1 µF (tantalum) | Timing capacitor |
| C_control | 0.01 µF (ceramic) | Noise decoupling on Pin 5 |
| IC | NE555 (DIP-8) | Timer IC |
| Vcc | +5 V DC regulated | Power supply |
| CRO/DSO | 20 MHz bandwidth | Output observation |
| Trigger source (Monostable) | Function generator, 1 kHz, 5Vpp | Trigger pulse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** Always connect **Pin 4 (Reset) directly to Vcc**; leaving it floating can cause erratic triggering due to noise pickup. A 0.01 µF bypass capacitor across Vcc-GND (close to the IC) is mandatory to prevent supply ripple from disturbing the threshold comparators.
